unit.name: Isolator System (Contamination Control & Containment)
unit.cfj: Isolate aseptic processing zone from surrounding environment to prevent microbial and particulate ingress

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

stakeholders.title: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a glove, seal, or pressure-boundary breach remains undetected during sterile work
rows.plain: Make sure a torn glove, leaking seal, or loss of the protective air boundary is noticed during the work. This helps keep germs and other unwanted material away from the product.
rows.imp_rat: A glove, seal, or pressure-boundary breach undetected during active sterile work directly breaches containment during the highest-risk moment; consequences include immediate exposure and contamination, so severity is maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Small breaches frequently go undetected during work because routine pressure alarms are coarse and glove pinholes evade notice until failure. Pressure-decay and glove-integrity checks add measurable setup time and are often skipped under schedule pressure. Detecting a subtle breach mid-work relies heavily on operator vigilance and interpretation, a skill-dependent weak point. Continuous integrity monitoring instrumentation is available but expensive and not universally deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that isolation status is indicated as acceptable while boundary integrity is compromised
rows.plain: Avoid showing that an area is safely separated when its physical barrier is damaged or no longer sealed. This helps prevent unwanted movement of air, particles, or germs between areas.
rows.imp_rat: A false 'acceptable' status while integrity is compromised is a dangerous instrumentation failure that lets contaminated processing proceed unchecked — catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor drift, miscalibration, and single-point indications mean the status quo can still show green while integrity is compromised, a recognized latent-failure risk. A false 'acceptable' indication is not a timing problem; the danger is that it persists undetected. Detecting a misleading status indication requires cross-checking multiple sensors, demanding vigilant expert oversight. An undetected compromised boundary can produce a contaminated batch representing a very high hidden cost.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial or particulate ingress at decision-relevant levels remains undetected
rows.plain: Avoid missing harmful amounts of germs or particles that enter through a protective barrier. Detecting these problems helps show whether the barrier is keeping the product protected.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected ingress at decision-relevant levels means contaminated product passes as sterile; detection is central to isolation assurance and the harm is catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Established EM and integrity-testing regimes catch many excursions yet still miss low-level or transient ingress, leaving a real recurring detection gap. Real-time and continuous particle counting shortens detection lag, but microbial detection still depends on incubation delays that leave windows of undetected ingress. Interpreting whether a detected excursion reflects decision-relevant ingress demands seasoned microbiologists whose judgement varies between sites. Comprehensive environmental monitoring instrumentation and media programs are costly but broadly funded in regulated fill-finish operations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a containment-critical anomaly remains undetected until sterile conditions are compromised
rows.plain: Make sure an important problem is noticed before it harms the clean conditions needed to make the medicine. This allows people to respond while there is still time to protect the process.
rows.imp_rat: A containment-critical anomaly undetected until sterile conditions are compromised strikes at the core job of maintaining isolation; the consequence is loss of the protected environment and possible product or personnel harm.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic microbial and pressure monitoring leaves gaps between checks where a critical anomaly can persist unseen, making this a chronic residual failure mode. Latent anomalies often surface only at the next environmental monitoring cycle, so detection lag is inherently long under status quo practice. Catching subtle containment drift before sterility loss demands experienced staff interpreting trends, and that expertise is scarce and inconsistently applied. Undetected anomalies that compromise sterility trigger costly batch losses and re-validation, which routine monitoring only partially averts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator contact with hazardous compounds through a breached handling interface during active manipulation
rows.plain: Prevent workers from touching dangerous substances through a damaged barrier while handling them. Keeping the barrier intact protects people from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact through a breached handling interface during active manipulation is the core exposure event the isolator exists to prevent, making relevance maximal. Severity is catastrophic — direct contact with hazardous compounds during handling causes acute personnel harm.
rows.sat_rat: Glove/half-suit interfaces are the recognized weakest containment link, and pinholes or seam failures during active manipulation remain a real recurring exposure risk. Glove and gauntlet manipulation is inherently slower but does not add exposure delay during normal use. Safe glove-port manipulation depends heavily on trained operator technique and disciplined attention to avoid a breach. Glove replacement and integrity testing consumables carry recurring cost that is largely predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a leaking glove or sleeve assembly passing the integrity test
rows.plain: Make sure a glove or flexible sleeve with a leak cannot pass the safety check. This protects workers and keeps the clean area from being exposed to unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: A leaking glove passing integrity testing defeats the primary barrier and the verification meant to guard it; this false-negative allows undetected breaches during handling of potent or sterile material, risking both operator exposure and batch contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure-decay glove testing reliably catches most leaks, though very small pinholes near detection thresholds can still occasionally pass. Glove integrity testing is quick with automated pressure-decay glove testers. Automated glove leak testers reduce reliance on operator judgement compared with manual visual inspection. Glove testers are a capital cost but reduce costly undetected-leak deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing contained process material into the technician's work area during sample withdrawal
rows.plain: Prevent process material from escaping into the technician’s work area while a sample is being taken. This helps protect the technician and keep the surrounding area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing contained hazardous material into the technician's work area during sampling is central to the containment CFJ (high relevance) and carries catastrophic operator-exposure consequences (max severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling systems substantially contain process material during withdrawal, though residual release into the work area still occurs when connections are imperfect. Sample withdrawal through contained ports adds modest handling time. Preventing release of process material during sampling depends on careful closed-system technique. Closed sampling devices and consumables represent recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial or particulate contamination remains undetected during processing
rows.plain: Find germs, dust, or other unwanted particles during processing instead of allowing them to remain hidden. This helps keep the work area and medicine clean.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination during processing means compromised product moves forward unflagged; detection failure is central with catastrophic patient-safety stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Non-viable particles are detected continuously but viable contamination remains undetected during processing until incubation completes, a persistent gap. Environmental monitoring results, especially viable counts, lag processing by incubation days so detection is not real-time. Interpreting particle trends and placing samples well requires trained microbiologists. Continuous particle counting and viable sampling programs are costly but standard.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination excursions remain undetected during sterile fill-finish
rows.plain: Detect any spread of contamination during the medicine-filling process instead of allowing it to go unnoticed. This helps show that the process meets safety requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination excursions during fill-finish let compromised product proceed; detection is central with catastrophic patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Excursions can still go undetected between sampling points and during incubation windows, a persistent gap in real-time assurance. Incubation-based detection introduces inherent delay before excursions are confirmed during fill-finish. Recognizing subtle excursions in-process requires trained monitoring staff and interpretive judgement. Continuous monitoring programs are expensive but standard in aseptic isolator lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of puncturing or tearing an enclosure glove while manipulating materials or instruments
rows.plain: Avoid puncturing or tearing the built-in glove used to handle materials inside the enclosure. A damaged glove could let contamination escape or enter.
rows.imp_rat: Puncturing or tearing a glove during manipulation is one of the most common real-world breach events central to routine use; it creates an immediate direct exposure path with severe consequences for the operator.
rows.sat_rat: Punctures and tears remain a common, largely unavoidable failure mode inherent to manipulating instruments through flexible gloves. Glove punctures cause significant downtime for change-out and re-qualification when they occur. Avoiding tears depends almost entirely on careful manual technique with sharp tools inside gloves, a persistent finesse burden. Gloves are consumables and frequent replacement plus lost material adds real cost.

rows.source_job: Return isolator through applicable return program
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual hazardous material remains when the isolator is released to the approved recipient
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous material is left inside the enclosed work chamber before it is returned to the approved recipient. This helps protect the people who receive and handle it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous material remaining when the isolator is released exposes downstream recipients directly, defeating the containment job (high relevance) with potentially irreversible exposure harm (max severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal cleaning verification and release certificates make confirmed-clean release fairly dependable when the program is followed. Final verification swabbing and hold for lab results delay release to the recipient. Interpreting release criteria and documentation requires qualified QA judgement. Analytical testing and certification add cost per unit released.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a glove or sleeve breach exposing the isolated zone during processing
rows.plain: Prevent gloves or sleeves from tearing or developing holes that could expose the protected work area during processing. This helps keep outside contamination away from the product.
rows.imp_rat: A glove/sleeve breach exposing the zone is one of the most common aseptic-isolator failures and a direct opening in the barrier; highly central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Pre- and post-use glove testing helps but in-process pinhole breaches frequently go undetected until after the fact, a recognized weak point. Glove breaches are detected by periodic tests, so a mid-process breach may persist unseen for the run's duration. Recognizing subtle breaches during manipulation demands constant operator vigilance. Glove replacement and physical/pressure testing carry ongoing cost.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate ingress during material transfers
rows.plain: Prevent germs or unwanted particles from getting in when materials are moved from one place to another. This helps keep the process and product clean.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial/particulate ingress during material transfers is a core defined failure of the transfer aspect of isolation; highly central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Material transfers are a known ingress point that mature transfer technology addresses well, though residual failures during manual steps persist. Transfer validation (e.g. VHP airlocks, RTP) is time-bounded and routinely performed. Transfer decontamination cycles reduce dependence on operator skill once validated. Rapid transfer ports and airlock systems are established capital costs in isolators.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch below the required sterility assurance threshold is judged compliant
rows.plain: Prevent a group of products that is not sterile enough from being incorrectly approved. This helps keep unsafe products from being released.
rows.imp_rat: Judging a below-threshold batch compliant releases non-sterile injectable product; final disposition error with catastrophic patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Defined acceptance criteria and dual review reduce misjudgement, but sterility-test insensitivity means confidence is inherently imperfect. The judgement occurs at review time and is not primarily time-constrained. Assessing whether a batch meets the sterility threshold requires expert sterility-assurance interpretation. Wrongly judging a sub-threshold batch compliant risks patient harm and recall, an extreme cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial ingress into the enclosed processing zone during aseptic filling
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting into the enclosed processing area while sterile products are being filled. This helps keep the products safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial ingress during aseptic filling is the central failure the CFJ exists to prevent; occurrence means a non-sterile injectable product reaching patients — catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Validated isolators with continuous pressure monitoring make microbial ingress rare, though glove and transfer breaches remain the residual failure mode. Continuous overpressure and unidirectional airflow maintain the aseptic barrier around the clock without operator waiting. Once qualified, the isolator holds the barrier automatically so the operator relies on the system rather than personal finesse. The engineered enclosure and HEPA filtration represent a large capital outlay that is amortized across many runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous material escaping from the chamber into the surrounding laboratory
rows.plain: Keep dangerous material inside the chamber so it does not escape into the laboratory. This protects people and the surrounding work area.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous material escaping the chamber into the lab is the central failure of an isolation glove box's containment job; when it occurs it exposes personnel to toxic, biological, or radiological harm — catastrophic and irreversible.
rows.sat_rat: Mature engineered containment with pressure cascades and gloves makes gross escape uncommon, though slow leaks and human error remain residual risks. Containment is a continuous passive function of the sealed enclosure so no operator time is spent maintaining it during work. Proper glove-box use for containment is well codified in SOPs and training, requiring competence but not rare expertise. Well-designed enclosures and negative-pressure controls carry meaningful capital cost but are standard budgeted infrastructure.

rows.source_job: Handle hazardous substances within the isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a hazardous substance escaping from the isolator into the surrounding cleanroom during handling
rows.plain: Prevent a dangerous substance from escaping the enclosed work area into the surrounding clean room. This helps protect workers, the room, and nearby products.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous substance escaping into the cleanroom during handling is the exact failure the containment isolator exists to prevent, so relevance is maximal. Severity is catastrophic given operator exposure, facility contamination, and regulatory fallout.
rows.sat_rat: Validated negative-pressure isolators with sealed gloveports and integrity testing make containment breach during routine handling uncommon, so the need is well served by mature practice. Negative-pressure containment with HEPA exhaust acts continuously, so protection is not gated on how quickly operators act during handling. Established SOPs and gloved-port designs deskill safe handling, though operator technique still influences residual escape risk. Isolators, HEPA filtration and validated pressure cascades are expensive but are standard sunk infrastructure for this hazard class.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the isolator returns to service without restored containment
rows.plain: Do not put the enclosure back into use until it can again keep unwanted material contained. This prevents work from restarting while people or products remain unprotected.
rows.imp_rat: Returning to service without restored containment defeats the isolator's protective purpose across every subsequent use, making it highly central. Severity is extreme because an unknowingly breached unit exposes staff and product silently over many operations.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and formal release gates in regulated labs make undetected premature return uncommon, though it hinges on every upstream test being done and read correctly, leaving a real residual gap. Return-to-service decisions follow defined requalification protocols that add time but are routinely scheduled, so delay is bounded rather than chronic. Correctly integrating leak, decontamination, and pressure results into a defensible release judgement still leans heavily on experienced QA and validation staff. Repeat requalification cycles and downtime cost real money when a premature release is caught, but formal gating avoids catastrophic recalls.

rows.stk: Laboratory manager, procurement officer, principal investigator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stated vacuum-oven capability does not hold for intended sterile fill-finish loads and cycles
rows.plain: Make sure the oven can really handle the planned batches and heating processes used to prepare germ-free medicines, as the manufacturer claims. This avoids choosing equipment that cannot do the job reliably.
rows.imp_rat: If stated capability does not hold for intended sterile fill-finish loads, the oven fails its qualified purpose in a regulated context (relevance high) with serious sterility-assurance and compliance consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Vendor datasheets are generic and rarely tested against the buyer's exact cycles, so mismatches between claimed and real-load performance surface routinely despite standard qualification protocols. Verifying that stated capability holds for actual sterile loads requires factory acceptance testing and installation/operational qualification that add weeks before capability is confirmed. Judging whether vendor performance claims translate to the buyer's specific sterile fill-finish loads demands scarce validation and process-engineering expertise that many acquisition teams lack. Discovering a capability shortfall late forces costly re-qualification, retrofits or replacement, though upfront FAT mitigates some of this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial ingress affecting a batch remains undetected during sterility assurance review
rows.plain: If germs enter a product group, the review should be able to detect that problem. This helps prevent a contaminated product group from being judged safe.
rows.imp_rat: Ingress affecting a batch left undetected at sterility review means contaminated product is released; final-gate detection failure with catastrophic stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Review relies on limited sampling and sterility tests of low sensitivity, so low-level ingress affecting a batch can genuinely remain undetected. Sterility assurance review happens after incubation, so detection of undetected ingress is inherently delayed. Recognizing subtle ingress evidence during review demands deep sterility-assurance expertise that is scarce and error-prone. An ingress that slips through review can cause a recall, an extremely high-consequence cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of accidental contact between intervention gloves or manipulated items and exposed product or critical product-contact surfaces
rows.plain: Prevent gloves or handled items from accidentally touching the product or the surfaces that touch it. This helps keep the product free from harmful germs.
rows.imp_rat: Accidental glove/item contact with exposed product is a leading direct contamination mechanism in aseptic work; highly central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Accidental glove-to-product contact during interventions is a well-known recurring failure that technique only partly controls. Deliberate glove movements to avoid product contact slow interventions modestly. Glove-port interventions require substantial dexterity and constant attention to avoid touching critical surfaces. Contact events cause line clearance and rejected units, which is costly, but prevention itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants crossing the isolated boundary on transferred materials
rows.plain: Prevent germs from being carried across the sealed barrier on materials being moved inside. This helps keep the protected work area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants crossing the boundary on transferred materials is a primary defined ingress route; highly central to the CFJ with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer hatches and surface sanitization reduce ingress but incomplete coverage on complex items remains a recurring contamination pathway. Surface decontamination of transferred items adds transfer-hatch cycle time. Correct loading and no-touch transfer technique depend heavily on operator skill. Transfer decontamination consumes agent and hatch cycle resources.

rows.source_job: Evacuate chamber to target vacuum level
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that chamber leakage prevents attainment of the target vacuum
rows.plain: Prevent leaks in the chamber from stopping it from reaching the required low-pressure level. This ensures the chamber is emptied of air as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Chamber leakage preventing attainment of target vacuum defeats the fundamental purpose of a vacuum oven (relevance maximal); the consequence is a failed cycle rather than a safety catastrophe, so severity is high but not extreme.
rows.sat_rat: Seals and door gaskets wear unpredictably, so leaks that block target vacuum remain a recurring, only partially mitigated failure mode. Leak checks and pump-down-time monitoring add setup time, and a leaking chamber can waste a full cycle before target vacuum is even reached. Diagnosing whether a seal, gasket, or fitting is leaking still relies on operator experience with rate-of-rise or soap tests. Failed pump-downs waste energy, gas, and consumable seals, and gaskets degrade and need periodic replacement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into the sterile process during sample withdrawal
rows.plain: Keep dirt or germs from entering the sterile process while taking a sample. This protects both the sample and the remaining product.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during sample withdrawal directly attacks the isolator's core sterility-preservation job; a breach here compromises the sterile process (high relevance) and can force batch loss or patient-harming contamination (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sampling remains a recognized contamination-introduction risk despite sterile sampling devices, since the act breaches the process boundary and depends on operator aseptic skill. Aseptic sampling through dedicated ports is a deliberate, moderately time-consuming step. Sterile sample withdrawal is highly technique-dependent and a frequent source of operator-induced contamination. Sampling systems, sterile connectors and any contaminated repeats add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of isolator interior surface area retaining particulate, chemical, or microbial residues after cleaning and decontamination
rows.plain: Remove as much dust, chemicals, and germs as possible from the inside surfaces after cleaning and treatment. This helps prevent the next product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Residual particulate, chemical, or microbial contamination on interior surfaces after cleaning directly defeats decontamination and threatens every subsequent batch; a core measurable indicator of the isolator's sterility-assurance job.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and vapor decontamination cover most surfaces, but shadowed areas, crevices and hard-to-reach geometry routinely retain measurable residues that standard practice struggles to fully eliminate. Achieving and verifying near-total residue removal requires extended cleaning and sampling cycles. Thorough manual cleaning of complex interior geometry through gloveports depends heavily on operator technique and diligence. Cleaning agents, swab sampling and analytical testing are significant recurring costs.

rows.source_job: Decontaminate the isolator prior to disposal
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous contamination remaining on the isolator interior, pass-throughs, and external contact zones after decontamination
rows.plain: Remove as much dangerous contamination as possible from the inside, transfer openings, and outside touch points before the equipment is thrown away. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous contamination remaining on interior, pass-throughs, and external contact zones after decontamination is central to the isolator's cleaning/containment job (relevance high); residual potent contamination causes cross-contamination and exposure across subsequent operations, a severe consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Validated decon plus surface sampling reliably lowers contamination, though pass-throughs and crevices leave recurring residual gaps. Achieving low residual levels requires repeated decon and swab cycles that extend the timeline. Reaching pass-throughs and external contact zones thoroughly depends on meticulous, trained technicians. Repeat cleaning, swab testing and lab analysis consume real resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an adjusted alarm threshold suppresses detection of a genuine enclosure excursion
rows.plain: Avoid changing an alarm limit so much that it hides a real loss of protection inside the sealed enclosure. The warning should still appear when conditions become unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: An adjusted alarm threshold that suppresses detection of a genuine excursion silently disables the safety net, letting real containment failures go unnoticed; central to reliable monitoring with severe consequences given the loss of any warning.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and alarm rationalization exist but nuisance-alarm suppression practices still let poorly chosen thresholds mask real excursions. A suppressed threshold delays or eliminates timely detection, which is the very failure mode in question. Judging safe alarm-threshold adjustments demands expert understanding of the process envelope, a scarce and fallible competency. A missed genuine excursion can cost an entire batch, and current safeguards only partly guard against ill-set thresholds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unintended contact between nonsterile surfaces and exposed product or product-contact surfaces
rows.plain: Reduce accidental contact between dirty surfaces and exposed product or surfaces that touch it. This lowers the chance of germs reaching the product.
rows.imp_rat: Contact between nonsterile surfaces and exposed product is a direct, immediate contamination event defeating the CFJ; highly central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Even skilled operators occasionally make inadvertent contact, so this remains an error-prone manual dependency. Careful non-contact technique slows manipulations but is absorbed into normal pace. Avoiding nonsterile-to-sterile contact depends heavily on trained operator hand discipline, a persistent skill demand. No direct spend beyond training, but contamination events from contact are expensive when they occur.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an enclosure with an undetected boundary breach
rows.plain: Avoid approving a sealed work area that has a hidden opening or leak in its protective boundary. Finding such damage first helps keep contamination out.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an isolator with an undetected boundary breach directly undermines containment, so relevance is very high. Severity is severe because a breach shipped into service means silent, ongoing exposure risk that may go unnoticed until harm occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and constant-pressure methods per ISO 10648 catch gross and moderate leaks reliably, yet undetected small breaches at acceptance remain a recognized residual risk. Acceptance leak testing adds hours to commissioning but is a scheduled, budgeted step that rarely stalls the stage. Interpreting borderline pressure-decay results still leans on experienced qualification engineers, so subtle breaches can slip past less-seasoned staff. Instrumented pressure-decay rigs and re-test cycles cost money but are minor against total isolator commissioning spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adverse isolation-condition drift remains unflagged until the aseptic zone is compromised
rows.plain: Detect harmful changes in the clean area’s protective conditions before they allow contamination. This gives people time to act before the area is no longer safe.
rows.imp_rat: Adverse drift unflagged until the zone is already compromised removes the chance for intervention; central to timely detection with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm thresholds catch sharp excursions well but slow drift below alarm limits frequently goes unflagged until compromise. Slow drift is often only recognized retrospectively during trend review rather than as it develops. Spotting gradual drift within noisy data requires experienced trend analysis that many sites lack. Late recognition allows drift to compromise multiple batches, escalating cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous residues escaping the isolator into adjacent controlled areas during disassembly or removal
rows.plain: Keep harmful leftover material contained while the enclosed unit is being taken apart or removed. This protects nearby work areas and the people in them.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue escaping into adjacent controlled areas during disassembly directly undermines the containment mission (relevance high); escape of potent APIs into surrounding areas risks worker exposure and facility contamination with serious health/compliance consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Transfer between rooms during disassembly is a known weak point where residues can migrate despite standard precautions. Moving components out through airlocks under containment adds handling time to disassembly. Preventing spread to adjacent controlled areas relies on trained crews maintaining bagging and transfer discipline. Wrapping, double-bagging and dedicated transfer routes consume consumables and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous compounds escaping from the enclosure into the surrounding cleanroom
rows.plain: Keep dangerous chemicals from escaping the enclosed area into the surrounding clean room. This protects people and keeps the room clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Preventing hazardous compounds from escaping into the cleanroom is the very definition of containment — the CFJ fails outright if this occurs. Severity is catastrophic: operator exposure to potent/toxic compounds and facility-wide contamination causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Mature negative-pressure isolator design with validated OEB containment routinely achieves sub-microgram escape levels, making leakage into the cleanroom uncommon. Continuous pressure and particle monitoring provide real-time containment status without slowing usage. Once validated and installed, maintaining negative-pressure containment demands little operator expertise beyond following SOPs. HEPA filtration, pressure cascades and monitoring add operating cost but are amortized across long service life.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing isolator containment while operating installed equipment
rows.plain: Keep the sealed enclosure intact while the built-in equipment is running. This prevents hazardous material from escaping or outside contamination from entering.
rows.imp_rat: Losing containment while operating installed equipment strikes at the isolator's core operational job during normal production. Severity is severe because continuous operation means exposure and contamination can escalate before detection.
rows.sat_rat: Dynamic equipment penetrations (shafts, seals, cabling) create containment vulnerabilities during operation that are managed but not fully eliminated. Installed equipment operation proceeds without added exposure delay under normal conditions. Operating in-isolator equipment through gloves demands dexterity and attention that can lapse. Sealed penetrations and equipment integration add engineering cost to the isolator.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of containment conditions remaining outside validated limits during processing
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the conditions needed to safely contain dangerous chemicals are outside their approved safe limits during processing. This reduces the chance of chemicals escaping.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged operation outside validated containment limits directly undermines the isolator's core job of protecting product and operator; the longer the excursion persists, the greater the odds of contaminated product reaching patients or operator exposure to potent compounds.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback control brings conditions back reliably for common upsets, but complex faults extend the out-of-limit duration unpredictably. Once an excursion is detected, automated controls restore validated conditions within a bounded interval, but the correction is not instantaneous. Restoring conditions is largely automated, though operator intervention for root causes still requires expertise. Prolonged out-of-limit time can force product holds or rejections, creating cost exposure that current controls only partly limit.

rows.source_job: Verify containment integrity of assembled isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a leak in a fixed enclosure joint or penetration remains undetected before first use
rows.plain: Find any leak in the enclosure's fixed connections or openings before the enclosure is used for the first time. This confirms that it can keep materials safely contained.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected leak in a fixed joint or penetration before first use defeats containment from the outset, so relevance is very high. Severity is severe — a latent leak means exposure and contamination risk goes unmitigated across all subsequent operations.
rows.sat_rat: Whole-enclosure pressure testing plus soap/tracer-gas techniques on penetrations catches most fixed-joint leaks, though a slow leak below detection sensitivity can persist to first use. Joint and penetration leak checks are integrated into the standard integrity test window without meaningful added delay. Locating which specific joint leaks after a failed decay test requires skilled fault-tracing that not all commissioning teams possess. Sealant, gaskets and rework on penetrations are inexpensive relative to the value of a compliant enclosure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a glove or sleeve breach during enclosed manipulation
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a glove or sleeve tears or develops a hole while someone works inside a closed enclosure. This helps keep the worker and the materials protected.
rows.imp_rat: A glove or sleeve breach during enclosed manipulation is the most direct failure of the isolator's containment barrier (relevance high) and causes both product contamination and operator exposure to hazardous material (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Physical glove and sleeve breaches during enclosed work remain a frequent maintenance and deviation trigger despite pressure-decay integrity testing. Manipulation continues at normal pace as breaches are usually detected only at scheduled tests. Avoiding glove/sleeve breach depends on careful handling and awareness of sharp or abrasive contact. Glove and sleeve replacement plus testing represent ongoing modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing filtration media with specifications incompatible with the isolator's containment requirements
rows.plain: Install air filters that provide the level of protection the enclosed work area needs. The wrong filters may not keep unwanted material contained.
rows.imp_rat: Installing filtration media incompatible with containment requirements directly defeats the isolator's core job of containing hazardous product; relevance is high because filtration integrity is central to containment, and severity is high because breached containment exposes operators to potent compounds and compromises product sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Part-number control, change-control paperwork and receiving QC catch most mismatches, yet look-alike media and substitution still produce occasional incompatible installs. Verifying filter specs against the isolator's containment requirements adds documentation time but is not a bottleneck. Matching HEPA/ULPA grade, seals and mounting to the isolator's classification requires a technician who understands the containment spec, so errors track experience. A wrong filter means scrapping media and repeating installation, a moderate but real material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the glove barrier is breached during aseptic manipulation
rows.plain: Avoid tearing or opening the glove barrier while handling materials in the germ-free area. A damaged barrier could let germs reach the product.
rows.imp_rat: A glove barrier breach during aseptic manipulation is a primary containment failure central to the isolator's protective job (relevance high); it simultaneously exposes the operator to hazardous material and compromises product sterility, a severe dual consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Glove barrier breaches during aseptic work are a well-known failure mode; integrity testing catches many but in-process undetected pinholes still occur. Aseptic glove manipulation is slow but does not directly delay the process. Aseptic technique through gloves is highly skill-dependent and error-prone even for trained operators. Glove integrity testing and sterile glove replacement are recurring consumable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of glove pinholes, tears, or degradation remaining undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure tiny holes, tears, or weakened areas in the gloves are not missed during the check. Damaged gloves could let germs pass through.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected glove pinholes or tears are among the most common real-world breach points directly contacting the operator, highly central to containment. Severity is very high given direct hand-level exposure to hazardous agents.
rows.sat_rat: Pinhole and micro-tear detection by visual inspection is notoriously unreliable, and even automated glove leak testers miss small defects, so escapes remain a recurring pain. Visual and physical glove inspection is quick per glove but tedious across many gloves at required frequency. Detecting pinholes and early degradation depends heavily on the inspector's diligence and eyesight, making outcomes inconsistent between individuals. Gloves are inexpensive to replace, and pressure-based glove testers are affordable where deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an affected batch is released before the contamination excursion is resolved
rows.plain: A group of products affected by contamination should not be released while the problem is still being investigated or fixed. This prevents possibly contaminated products from being sent out.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing an affected batch before the excursion is resolved puts contaminated product into distribution; catastrophic, irreversible patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine-until-cleared procedures are standard and generally reliable at preventing early release. Release timing pressure can push disposition before excursion resolution is complete. Holding release until resolution relies on quality discipline rather than deep skill. Premature release of an affected batch risks recall, a very high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into the isolator during glove and sleeve assembly replacement
rows.plain: Make it less likely that germs or particles enter the enclosed clean chamber while its flexible gloves and sleeves are replaced. This helps keep the product and work area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Glove/sleeve replacement is a known high-risk breach point; introducing microbial or particulate contamination during this operation directly defeats the aseptic barrier and can contaminate subsequent batches, a core failure mode for isolators.
rows.sat_rat: Established change-out ports and aseptic procedures limit ingress, but manual glove replacement remains a recognized recurring source of microbial and particulate contamination. Glove replacement typically requires a decontamination cycle before and requalification after, adding time. Aseptic glove-change technique to avoid introducing contamination is skill-intensive and error-prone if rushed. Replacement assemblies plus post-change decontamination are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that enclosure seams or seals permit surrounding air to enter the aseptic processing zone
rows.plain: Avoid allowing gaps or faulty seals in the enclosure to draw surrounding air into the protected processing area. Keeping outside air out helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Seam/seal leaks admitting surrounding air are a direct breach of the physical barrier — the core mechanism of isolation — with severe contamination stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified isolators with gasketed enclosures and automated leak tests reliably keep seam ingress extremely low across batches. Leak and integrity tests are periodic rather than continuous, so a developing seal breach may persist between test intervals. Setting up and interpreting pressure-decay integrity tests requires trained technicians but is well documented in standard procedures. Seal replacement and integrity testing are routine maintenance costs that are predictable and modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protective-control testing exposes the technician to a hazardous state
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that testing a safety feature puts the person doing the test in danger. The test should confirm the feature works without creating a hazardous situation.
rows.imp_rat: Protective-control testing that exposes a technician to a hazardous state is directly relevant to safe operation of the containment system; severity is maximal because it can cause acute personnel exposure to highly potent or toxic agents, an irreversible harm to a human.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO, permits and interlock design reduce exposure, but ad-hoc test setups and human shortcuts leave a meaningful residual hazard. Safe protective-control testing requires deliberate lockout/setup steps that lengthen the task. Deliberately driving a control toward its trip state without exposing the technician demands strong hazard awareness and procedure discipline, a real skill dependency. PPE, interlocks and safe-test rigs add modest cost.

rows.stk: Professional | End User / Safety Officer | One-time
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the defined containment classification understates the application hazard
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that the chosen level of protection is too low for the danger involved. The protection should be strong enough for the actual risks of the work.
rows.imp_rat: An understated containment classification means the isolator is mismatched to the true hazard, undermining the core job of matching containment to risk. Consequences are severe since operators may be exposed to a more dangerous agent than the equipment is rated for.
rows.sat_rat: Established OEB banding frameworks and containment-performance targets exist, but understatement still occurs when data on the compound is sparse or surrogate assumptions are used. Hazard classification typically happens during early risk assessment workshops that consume days but rarely bottleneck the preparation stage. Correctly mapping application hazards to a containment class (OEB/OEL banding) depends heavily on scarce toxicology and industrial-hygiene judgement that many labs lack in-house. Getting the classification wrong is cheap to write down but extremely expensive downstream, yet the classification exercise itself consumes little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of compromised isolator containment after the utility interruption
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the enclosed clean area remains unprotected after a loss of electricity or another needed service. Faster recovery helps keep the product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged compromised containment after a utility interruption leaves the barrier down during an unplanned event; severe because operator exposure and product loss accumulate with duration, and central to resilient containment.
rows.sat_rat: UPS and backup provisions help but the duration of compromised containment after a utility hit varies widely and is not tightly bounded. Restoring containment after a utility loss depends on utility recovery plus re-establishment sequences, which can take a prolonged interval. Recovery choreography needs experienced operators to bring systems back in the right order. Extended compromised periods risk product loss and rework, a meaningful cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a transfer-interface boundary breach during material entry into the processing zone
rows.plain: Make sure the connection used to bring materials into the processing area does not break its protective seal. This helps stop germs from entering with the materials.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer-interface breaches are a leading real-world ingress route; highly central to isolation with severe contamination consequences when they occur.
rows.sat_rat: Alpha-beta ports and validated airlocks work well, but interface breaches at material entry remain one of the more common contamination pathways. RTP and airlock transfers add handling steps but are a routine part of the run cadence. Correct RTP docking demands careful operator technique and attention, a recurring source of human error. Rapid transfer port hardware and validated transfer procedures carry meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a failed protective control incorrectly passes verification
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that a broken safety feature is incorrectly judged to be working. Testing must clearly identify failures before the equipment is used.
rows.imp_rat: A failed protective control that wrongly passes verification leaves a latent safety gap in the containment system; when relied upon during an actual event it fails to protect, exposing operators and product with no warning.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized functional test protocols catch most failures, but a control that fails in an undetected mode or a poorly designed test can still yield a false pass. Protective-control verification is a defined test step that adds routine time. Correctly verifying interlocks and alarms requires a qualified technician who understands failure modes to avoid false passes. Test tooling and qualified-tester time are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch conditions outside established sterility-assurance boundaries remain unrecognized before disposition
rows.plain: Make sure any batch conditions that could make the product unsafe are recognized before the batch is approved. This helps confirm that the process was able to keep the product free from harmful germs.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecognized out-of-boundary batch conditions before disposition risks releasing non-sterile product; final-gate error with catastrophic stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Batch review procedures catch most flagged conditions, but subtle boundary breaches can be rationalized away, leaving a real gap. Recognition before disposition depends on completing review within the batch-release timeline, usually adequate. Judging whether conditions fell outside sterility-assurance boundaries requires expert interpretation of aggregated data. Missing an out-of-boundary condition before release risks recall-level cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of microbial or particulate ingress pathways unresolved at the processing-zone boundary
rows.plain: Resolve as many openings or routes for germs and particles to enter the processing area as possible. This helps keep contamination from reaching the product.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved ingress pathways at the boundary directly weaken isolation; each open path is a severe contamination vector though this is a coverage/completeness framing.
rows.sat_rat: HACCP-style contamination risk assessments are mature practice, yet subtle pathways at dynamic interfaces still routinely go unresolved. Identifying every ingress pathway is a lengthy investigation but is treated as a scheduled design task rather than a bottleneck. Enumerating ingress pathways depends on deep contamination-control expertise and is highly error-prone when done by less-seasoned reviewers. Unresolved pathways surface late as costly retrofits, but structured reviews limit how often that happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operating status falsely indicates established containment during activation
rows.plain: The machine’s status should not falsely say that the product is safely enclosed when that protection has not really been established. Accurate status information helps prevent unsafe operation.
rows.imp_rat: A false 'containment established' indication during activation directly misleads operators into working in an unprotected state, tightly tied to the containment job. Severity is high due to unwitting exposure before real containment exists.
rows.sat_rat: Status indicators often infer containment from pressure alone, so false 'established' indications during activation still occur and standard practice only partially catches them. Pressure and airflow readings display quickly, so operators are not waiting to see status. Interpreting whether a green status truly reflects containment requires trained judgement, since sensors can read nominal while a seal is compromised. Adding independent verification sensors and continuous integrity monitoring raises cost beyond a single pressure gauge.

rows.source_job: Conduct final safety survey before handoff
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual chemical, biological, or radiological contamination remains undetected during the final survey
rows.plain: Make sure no harmful germs, chemicals, or radioactive material are missed during the final safety check. This helps confirm the area is safe before it is handed over.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected residual contamination during the final survey defeats the containment verification purpose; if the box is released with hidden contamination, downstream users face serious exposure, making both centrality and consequences high.
rows.sat_rat: Established survey protocols and calibrated detectors catch most contamination, but shadowed corners, porous materials, and mixed CBR hazards leave recurring blind spots where residues go undetected. Final surveys are time-intensive scans of every internal surface, and hidden geometries force repeat passes that stretch the schedule. Reliable detection of undetected residuals hinges on the surveyor's technique and choice of instrument, so results vary strongly with the health-physics or industrial-hygiene technician's expertise. Comprehensive multi-hazard surveying consumes instrument time, consumables, and labor, and false-negative rework adds unplanned cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the oven cycle starts with parameters that differ from approved batch requirements
rows.plain: Lower the chance that the oven starts with settings that do not match the approved requirements for the batch. This helps ensure the batch is processed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Starting a cycle with parameters differing from approved batch requirements produces non-conforming product from the outset, striking at the process's core validity (relevance high) and causing potentially discarded regulated batches (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-lockout on newer controllers helps, but many installed ovens rely on manual entry, so wrong-parameter starts still occur. Verifying parameters against the batch record before start adds a manual checking step to each run. Correct startup still leans on the operator reading the batch sheet and manually confirming each setpoint, an error-prone judgement task. A cycle started on wrong parameters wastes the whole batch and its energy and material, a costly rework event.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that routine interventions compromise isolation of the critical zone
rows.plain: Make sure ordinary work activities do not break the protection keeping the important clean area free from germs. This helps preserve product safety during handling.
rows.imp_rat: Routine interventions compromising isolation is a frequent real failure mode; central to maintaining the barrier during normal operation with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Routine interventions remain a leading contamination risk, and current evaluation only partially assures isolation is preserved. Intervention studies extend qualification schedules but are a defined activity. Assessing intervention impact on isolation depends on operator-technique realism and expert observation. Smoke studies and intervention testing add moderate cost within normal programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a rigid barrier panel, seam, or joint develops a through-breach during aseptic filling
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a solid protective panel, its joined edges, or its connection develops a hole during sterile filling. This helps keep outside contamination away from the product.
rows.imp_rat: A through-breach in a rigid barrier panel during filling is a direct loss of the physical isolation boundary with severe contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Through-breaches of rigid panels are rare, though seams, gaskets, and welds can degrade and are the practical weak points. Rigid panels require no operator time during filling and integrity is designed to persist across the run. Panel integrity is an engineered property, so the operator carries little skill burden here. Robust stainless and polymer construction is costly upfront but durable over service life.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching the isolator containment barrier during material transfer
rows.plain: Prevent the sealed protective enclosure from being opened or damaged while materials are moved through its openings. This helps keep harmful substances or germs from getting in or out.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching the containment barrier during material transfer is a frequent, high-exposure failure point central to the isolator's job. Severity is severe because transfers are routine and a breach exposes operators directly to hazardous material.
rows.sat_rat: Rapid transfer port technology provides well-established containment during transfer, though procedural error and improper docking still occasionally breach the barrier. Validated transfer procedures through RTPs or airlocks add cycle time but are routine. Correct alpha-beta port docking and airlock sequencing require trained operators who can err under pressure. Rapid transfer ports and validated transfer containers are costly consumables and hardware.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous residual materials during removal
rows.plain: Prevent the operator from coming into contact with dangerous leftover material while removing it. This helps protect the operator from harm.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to hazardous residuals during removal is central to the containment job's protective purpose. Severity is high — direct dermal/inhalation exposure to potent compounds can cause serious, potentially irreversible harm to personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Mature CIP/wipe-down protocols and airlock discipline substantially reduce exposure, but residual pockets and human slips still cause occasional incidents. Full decontamination and controlled removal add days to the disposal timeline but the schedule is generally accommodated. Safe residue removal depends heavily on trained, gowned operators following validated SOPs, so outcomes hinge on scarce expertise and vigilance. PPE, containment consumables and specialist labor make each removal expensive, though the spend is budgeted as routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile product, components, or critical surfaces become contaminated during the interruption and recovery
rows.plain: Keep the product, its parts, and important clean surfaces free of germs while the interrupted process is stopped and restarted. This protects product safety during recovery.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of sterile product or critical surfaces during interruption and recovery covers the vulnerable non-steady-state period central to sterility assurance (high relevance) with batch-integrity consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and interlocks help, but transitions and recoveries remain among the least deterministic phases, so contamination during recovery recurs. Recovery from an interruption can stretch across an extended period, prolonging exposure of sterile surfaces to potential contamination. Safe recovery relies on operators executing complex re-sanitization and line-clearance judgement calls that are hard to standardize. Compromised components and product during recovery often must be scrapped, driving material loss.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transient barrier failures during interventions or operating transitions occur outside evaluation windows
rows.plain: Make sure brief losses of protection during interventions or changes in operation are checked when they happen. This prevents short failures from being missed because they occurred outside the testing period.
rows.imp_rat: Transient barrier failures outside evaluation windows are unseen breaches during dynamic operation; relevant to true integrity with severe consequences if real.
rows.sat_rat: Transient barrier failures outside evaluation windows are a chronic blind spot poorly served by periodic testing. Capturing transient failures requires extended or continuous monitoring that lengthens evaluation. Detecting transient breaches during transitions demands vigilant expert observation that is easily missed. Continuous high-resolution monitoring to catch transients is costly and rarely fully deployed.

rows.source_job: Define and document material transfer protocols
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which specified decontamination dwell periods fall short of required exposure
rows.plain: Make sure materials are exposed to the cleaning treatment for nearly all of the required time, without falling short. This helps ensure harmful contamination is removed before materials are moved.
rows.imp_rat: Shortfalls in decontamination dwell time leave viable microbial or chemical residues, directly compromising the sterility assurance central to the isolator's job; consequences include contaminated batches and failed sterility with potential patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: PLC-controlled decontamination recipes enforce dwell setpoints consistently, so shortfalls against specified exposure are rare and alarmed. Dwell periods are pre-defined recipe parameters, so meeting them adds no unplanned time. Automated cycle control removes reliance on operator timing judgement for dwell exposure. Meeting dwell requirements consumes agent and cycle time but at negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the designated operating mode fails to establish the required pressure direction and airflow conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the selected operating setting creates the needed direction of pressure and movement of air. This helps keep contamination from entering the protected area.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to establish the required pressure direction and airflow is the fundamental mechanism by which the isolator maintains both sterility and containment (very high relevance), and its loss can breach containment or sterility catastrophically (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern isolators use interlocked automated control of pressure direction and airflow with alarms, so failure to establish required conditions is uncommon and usually flagged. Automated mode changeovers establish airflow and pressure conditions quickly on command. Automated control systems handle pressure/airflow setup with little operator judgement required. PLC control, sensors and validation add capital cost but not high running cost.

rows.source_job: Monitor temperature and pressure during operation
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a chamber temperature or pressure excursion goes unnoticed during the active cycle
rows.plain: Make sure a change in the chamber's heat or pressure is noticed during the active process. Early notice helps people respond before the process is affected.
rows.imp_rat: An unnoticed temperature or pressure excursion during the active cycle means process failure goes uncaught, defeating monitoring that is core to guaranteeing a valid cycle (relevance high) and letting compromised batches proceed (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Threshold alarms reliably flag most excursions, though unmonitored or poorly configured alarms let some pass unnoticed. Alarms and trend displays surface excursions in near-real time rather than requiring later discovery. Catching an excursion still often depends on an operator watching displays or being nearby, a vigilance burden that varies with staffing. Alarm and logging hardware is standard, and catching excursions early avoids costly spoiled batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual hazardous material during glove and sleeve assembly removal
rows.plain: Protect the technician from contact with dangerous material left inside the equipment when protective gloves and sleeves are removed. This reduces the risk of harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to residual hazardous material during glove/sleeve removal is a well-known high-risk task central to worker protection (high relevance) with direct toxic-exposure consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination-before-removal and careful technique reduce exposure, but residual hazardous material on worn gloves is a persistent variable that technique alone cannot fully eliminate. Glove/sleeve removal is preceded by decontamination cycles, adding scheduled time before handling. Safe glove change-out requires trained technique to fold contaminated surfaces inward and avoid touch-contamination. Replacement gloves, sleeves and PPE are frequent recurring consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile batch contamination while the operator diagnoses or resolves an alarm or deviation
rows.plain: Prevent the sterile batch from becoming contaminated while a worker investigates or fixes a warning or problem. This protects the product while the issue is being handled.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile batch contamination while diagnosing an alarm or deviation targets a high-risk intervention window central to maintaining sterility (high relevance), with batch-loss and patient-safety consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: First-air protection and closed-isolator design reduce risk, but any prolonged diagnostic activity inside a sterile zone still yields recurring contamination events. Diagnosing an alarm takes minutes to hours during which the sterile field remains exposed, extending the window of vulnerability. Protecting the batch while troubleshooting depends heavily on operator discipline and aseptic technique, a scarce and error-prone competency. A contaminated sterile batch is a very high-value loss, and current practice offers only partial protection against it during deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the required containment response remains inactive after activation
rows.plain: Make sure the required response to a breach starts fully and quickly after it is turned on. This helps limit the effects of a loss of containment.
rows.imp_rat: A required containment response that remains inactive after activation means protection is nominally engaged but ineffective, directly defeating the safety job (high relevance) with severe undetected-exposure consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Response actuation is usually completed as designed, but incomplete or partial activation after command remains a possible failure that verification only partly catches. Once initiated, the response reaches full effect within a designed short interval. Confirming the response fully activated requires minimal operator skill given status feedback. Redundant actuators and verification add modest cost over base design.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operator manipulation compromises glove or sleeve integrity during processing
rows.plain: Prevent workers from damaging the protective gloves or sleeves while handling materials. A tear or opening could let contamination reach the product.
rows.imp_rat: Operator manipulation compromising glove/sleeve integrity opens the barrier during processing; central common failure with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Even trained operators inadvertently stress or snag gloves during complex manipulations, making this a persistent human-factor gap. Careful manipulation slows interventions but the integrity loss itself is the concern, not time. Avoiding glove damage during manipulation depends almost entirely on operator dexterity and training. Damaged gloves and resulting rework impose moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of chamber temperature or vacuum outside specified limits during the timed hold
rows.plain: Keep the chamber's temperature and air pressure within the required range throughout the timed step. The longer they are outside that range, the less reliable the process may be.
rows.imp_rat: Holding chamber temperature and vacuum within specified limits during the timed hold IS the core job of a vacuum oven; time outside limits directly invalidates the process (relevance maximal), and out-of-spec holds can ruin batches, so severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback control and datalogging make in-band holds the routine outcome, with excursions being infrequent and logged rather than silent. PID controllers and vacuum regulators hold setpoints continuously through the timed hold, so out-of-limit durations are typically brief and self-correcting. Once a validated recipe is loaded, closed-loop control keeps temperature and vacuum within band with little operator finesse required. Modern controllers and vacuum pumps that maintain limits are standard equipment cost, not a recurring burden per cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the efficacy assessment fails to detect surviving microbial contamination after cycle completion
rows.plain: Make sure the check after cleaning and germ-killing is able to find any germs that survived. This helps confirm that the process actually made the area safe for use.
rows.imp_rat: Efficacy assessment failing to detect survivors after decon lets a non-sterile baseline pass; central to decon validation with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: BIs are a proven standard but their limited number and placement mean surviving contamination between indicators can escape detection. Biological-indicator incubation delays confirmation of efficacy by days. Reading and interpreting BIs and enumeration requires trained lab staff. BIs, media, and incubation are recurring but accepted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches started without confirmed integrity of the isolation boundary
rows.plain: Start fewer production runs before confirming that the barrier protecting the clean area is fully intact. This helps prevent outside contamination from entering.
rows.imp_rat: Starting batches without confirmed boundary integrity gambles the entire batch's sterility; central gatekeeping error with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pre-batch integrity tests with interlocks are common, but not all installations gate batch start on them, leaving a real recurring gap. Pre-batch integrity confirmation adds startup time but is a scheduled step rather than a delay-prone bottleneck. Confirming boundary integrity depends on operators following checklists and interpreting test results, introducing some human-reliance error. A batch started without confirmed integrity risks total batch loss, a high-cost failure the practice aims to prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of chamber locations that remain above the viable-contamination acceptance threshold after the initial cycle
rows.plain: After the first cleaning cycle, reduce the number of chamber areas where living germs are still above the allowed level. This helps make the chamber safe for the process.
rows.imp_rat: Chamber locations remaining above the viable-contamination threshold after the initial decontamination cycle directly defeats the isolator's core job of maintaining a sterile/contained environment (relevance high); undetected residual bioburden can contaminate product batches leading to recalls or patient harm (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Vaporized hydrogen peroxide cycles with BI mapping reliably achieve the acceptance threshold across most locations, with cold/shadowed spots being the well-known residual challenge. Bio-decontamination cycle development and settling take significant time, but the mapping runs on a defined qualification schedule. Selecting representative biological-indicator locations to prove worst-case kill still demands microbiological and cycle-development expertise. Biological indicators, media and repeated cycle runs add cost but are standard qualification expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of incoming transfers that carry contaminants into the sterile isolator chamber
rows.plain: Prevent incoming materials from carrying dirt or germs into the germ-free chamber. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming transfers carrying contaminants into the sterile chamber is one of the leading real-world routes of isolator contamination, central to the sterility job (relevance high); contaminated transfers compromise the entire chamber and product (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Surface bio-decontamination of incoming transfers is a mature validated practice, though shadowed surfaces and procedural lapses still let some contaminants enter. Incoming transfer decontamination cycles (VHP, sporicidal wipe) add setup time before entry. Loading and decontaminating incoming items follows procedures that require trained but not expert execution. Sporicidal agents, VHP consumables and transfer hardware carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intervention item carries contamination into the isolated zone
rows.plain: Make sure tools or other items brought in during the work do not carry germs into the protected area. This helps keep the area and its contents clean.
rows.imp_rat: An intervention item carrying contamination into the zone is a direct import vector during manipulation; central with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures exist but human-handled intervention items are a frequent and hard-to-control contamination route. Sanitizing intervention items before entry adds handling time. Ensuring items are properly treated relies on operator discipline and procedure. Sanitization materials for interventions are low but recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an access interface remains incompletely closed after an aseptic intervention
rows.plain: Make sure an opening is fully closed again after work is done in a germ-free area. This helps keep unwanted germs from entering the protected area.
rows.imp_rat: An access interface left incompletely closed after an intervention leaves an open ingress path; central to isolation with severe consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and closure sensors help, but many ports and doors still rely on manual confirmation, leaving recurring gaps. An incompletely closed interface is caught at the next status check, which may not be immediate. Correct closure depends heavily on operator diligence, a well-known human-error source. A missed closure can compromise a batch, incurring high downstream cost.

rows.source_job: Qualify isolator installation and operational performance
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that containment, airflow, pressure control, alarms, or interlocks fail qualification criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the enclosure keeps materials contained, moves air correctly, maintains the right pressure, gives warnings, and stops unsafe actions as required. This confirms that the enclosure is safe and works properly.
rows.imp_rat: Failure of containment, airflow, pressure, alarms, or interlocks at qualification strikes the fundamental engineered barriers the CFJ depends on. Severity is high because any of these failures can lead to undetected escape or exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Mature standards (ISO 14644, containment leak testing, alarm/interlock challenge testing) make pass/fail highly repeatable, so qualification failures are usually caught before release rather than escaping. Formal qualification (IQ/OQ/PQ and containment leak/pressure testing) is a well-scheduled but lengthy protocol-driven activity in every preparation program. Qualification against acceptance criteria follows standardized protocols and is often run by experienced commissioning specialists, reducing reliance on individual judgement. Requalification after a failed criterion is costly in rework and delay, but the testing itself is a budgeted, predictable expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the containment response fails to initiate after the operator command
rows.plain: Make sure the system starts containing a leak or other breach when the operator gives the command. This limits the spread of hazardous material.
rows.imp_rat: A containment response that fails to initiate after operator command leaves an active breach uncontrolled, central to protective function (high relevance) with severe exposure/release consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked control systems generally initiate the containment response reliably on command, though sensor or actuator faults occasionally cause a failure to start. Command-driven containment initiation is designed to trigger promptly with minimal delay. Operator command of the containment response is straightforward once trained, requiring little skill. Control interlocks and response hardware add moderate cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of viable microbial excursions above qualified limits in the critical zone during aseptic processing
rows.plain: Prevent harmful levels of living germs from appearing in the most sensitive work area during sterile processing. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Viable microbial excursions above qualified limits in the critical zone directly defeat isolation; consequences are batch loss and patient safety risk.
rows.sat_rat: Modern closed isolators with H2O2 bio-decontamination achieve very low and consistent viable counts, making excursions above qualified limits genuinely rare. Viable monitoring relies on incubation cycles that take days, so excursions are detected long after they occur rather than in real time. Interpreting microbial results and linking them to aseptic events demands scarce microbiologist judgement that varies between reviewers. A confirmed viable excursion can force batch rejection and investigation costs that are substantial but infrequent in a qualified isolator.

rows.source_job: Decontaminate the isolator prior to disposal
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous material escaping from the isolator into the surrounding facility during decontamination
rows.plain: Prevent harmful material from escaping the sealed enclosure into the building while it is being cleaned before disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous material escaping into the facility during decontamination is a direct containment breach undermining the core job. Severity is severe as it can contaminate the surrounding facility and expose multiple personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Because the isolator itself is designed to contain, decontaminating in-situ with intact seals is highly reliable at preventing facility escape. Containment-preserving decon adds time but is a predictable step within the shutdown window. Maintaining negative pressure and integrity during decon relies on skilled operators and functioning controls. Running the isolator's own containment during decon uses energy and monitoring resources at moderate ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing isolator containment during an extended high-throughput campaign
rows.plain: Keep the sealed enclosure secure during a long period of high-volume work, so harmful material does not escape or enter.
rows.imp_rat: Losing containment during an extended high-throughput campaign is central because sustained operation is where seals and interfaces degrade. Severity is high due to prolonged, potentially unnoticed exposure across a long production run.
rows.sat_rat: Prolonged high-throughput operation accumulates glove fatigue and seal degradation, and containment loss over an extended campaign is a recognized cumulative risk.
