rows.sat_rat: Sensor-verified aeration reliably drives residue below limits, though sensor placement gaps leave minor residual uncertainty. Aeration to clear decontaminant residue adds a defined hold before filling. Aeration is automated and monitored, requiring little operator judgement. Extended aeration consumes time and energy, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required readiness criteria with missing or conflicting evidence at the release decision
rows.plain: Reduce the number of required readiness checks that lack proof or have proof that conflicts when the release decision is made. This helps ensure the process is released only when its readiness is clearly supported.
rows.imp_rat: Readiness criteria with missing or conflicting evidence at release can allow an unready isolator into service (relevance/severity), a moderate gate-integrity concern.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record review usually surfaces complete evidence, but missing or conflicting criteria still appear at the release decision. Chasing missing readiness evidence at release delays the decision. Assembling complete readiness evidence depends on operator documentation discipline. Missing evidence forces investigation or reprocessing, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of shielding residue on isolated interior surfaces at cycle initiation
rows.plain: Reduce the leftover protective material on enclosed inner surfaces when the cleaning cycle starts. This helps the cycle begin with the surfaces in the expected condition.
rows.imp_rat: Shielding residue on interior surfaces at cycle initiation can protect microbes from decontamination (relevance), risking incomplete sterilization and contamination (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning procedures generally remove residues that would shield microbes, though occasional soiling reduces cycle efficacy. Pre-cycle cleaning to remove shielding residue adds fixed preparation time. Effective pre-cleaning depends on operator thoroughness. Cleaning agents and labor are routine costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of airflow disruption caused by operator movement around exposed sterile materials
rows.plain: Keep people moving around exposed germ-free materials from disturbing the clean airflow that protects them.
rows.imp_rat: Operator-movement airflow disruption around exposed sterile materials can drive particulate/microbial deposition (relevance), risking localized contamination (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Smoke studies and training reduce disruption, but operator movement remains an inherent variable source of turbulence. Deliberate movement is slower but the airflow disruption itself is the issue. Minimizing airflow disruption depends on operator awareness of unidirectional flow. Disruption-caused contamination carries investigation and loss cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical locations and processing intervals lacking cleanliness evidence
rows.plain: Make sure there is cleanliness evidence for every important location and stage of processing. This helps show that the work area stayed clean when it mattered.
rows.imp_rat: Critical locations lacking cleanliness evidence leaves blind spots in the assurance case (relevance) that could mask contamination (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans cover critical points but gaps in spatial and temporal coverage routinely leave locations without cleanliness evidence. Assembling cleanliness evidence across locations and intervals is delayed by incubation and lab throughput. Designing adequate sampling coverage requires microbiological expertise. Comprehensive sampling plans consume media and labor at real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the reported cleanliness state no longer represents the isolated zone at decision
rows.plain: The cleanliness result should still accurately describe the separated area when someone uses it to make a decision. This prevents people from relying on an outdated or misleading result.
rows.imp_rat: A reported cleanliness state no longer representing the zone at decision means release decisions rest on stale data (relevance), risking a wrong call (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Time-stamped records help but incubation delay creates real risk the reported state no longer represents the zone. Lag between sampling and decision means the reported state may be stale by disposition. Judging representativeness at decision requires quality expertise. Re-verification when currency lapses adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned zone access that invalidates established readiness
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected entry into a work area when the area has already been checked and declared ready. This helps prevent repeated checks and delays before the process starts.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned zone access that invalidates readiness reintroduces ingress risk and forces re-qualification (relevance/severity), a moderate operational and integrity concern.
rows.sat_rat: Access controls and interlocks limit unplanned entry, but it still occurs and reliably voids readiness when it does. Unplanned access forces re-decontamination, adding substantial delay. Deciding whether access invalidates readiness requires quality judgement. Re-establishing readiness wastes agent and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required sampling-point and operating-state combinations left unsampled
rows.plain: Make sure samples are collected from every required location under every required operating condition. This gives a complete picture of whether the environment is clean enough.
rows.imp_rat: Unsampled point/state combinations leave evaluation coverage gaps (relevance) that can hide ingress under specific conditions (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Practical sampling plans sample representative subsets, so some point-state combinations are routinely left unsampled. Covering all sampling-point/state combinations extends monitoring effort and turnaround. Planning full combinatorial coverage demands experienced program design. More sampling combinations mean more media and analyst time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an environmental sample is contaminated during collection or handling
rows.plain: Prevent an environmental sample from being contaminated while it is collected or handled. This helps ensure the result reflects the environment rather than outside contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating an environmental sample during collection produces false positives/negatives (relevance) that distort risk decisions (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic sampling technique reduces but does not eliminate false-positive contamination, a recurring monitoring frustration. Careful handling adds minor time to sample collection. Avoiding sample contamination during collection depends heavily on aseptic technique. False positives from contaminated samples drive costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of isolated-zone airflow disruption caused by sample collection
rows.plain: Collecting samples should not significantly disturb the clean, controlled airflow in the work area. This helps prevent the sampling process from causing contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Airflow disruption from sample collection can transiently compromise the protected zone (relevance) and skew or contaminate results (severity), a moderate concern.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed sampling ports and trained technique limit disruption, but manual sampling still perturbs airflow to some degree. Careful sampling is slower but disruption not duration is the loss. Minimizing airflow disruption during sampling depends on operator technique. Sampling supplies are low cost; disruption cost is indirect.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that collected samples acquire contamination unrelated to the isolated zone
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that samples pick up germs from outside the area being checked. Otherwise, the results may wrongly suggest that the isolated area was contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Samples acquiring contamination unrelated to the zone yield false positives (relevance) that trigger unwarranted action or mask true status (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Technique and controls limit but do not eliminate adventitious sample contamination. Careful handling adds minor time to sample collection. Avoiding extraneous contamination unrelated to the zone depends on analyst aseptic skill. False positives from extraneous contamination trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a brief contamination-relevant excursion is absent from the overseer's risk review
rows.plain: Make sure even a short event that could introduce contamination is included in the supervisor's risk review. This helps prevent a small warning from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: A brief excursion absent from the overseer's risk review means a real risk goes unassessed (relevance) and possibly unmitigated (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous data logging captures transients, but filtering and manual triage mean brief events are still sometimes dropped from review. Brief transient excursions can be missed entirely if not captured at the moment they occur. Recognizing that a brief excursion belongs in the risk review requires attentive expert triage. An omitted transient can hide a real risk, with potential large downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of isolator shutdowns caused by contamination events in the surrounding room
rows.plain: Have fewer shutdowns of the enclosed sterile work area because of contamination in the surrounding room. This helps production continue safely and with fewer interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Shutdowns from surrounding-room contamination affect availability and hint at boundary weakness (relevance); consequence is interruption rather than direct product contamination (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because isolators are designed to be independent of room conditions, room-driven shutdowns are relatively infrequent. Room contamination events prompt fairly quick precautionary shutdown decisions. Deciding whether room events warrant isolator shutdown needs contamination-control judgement. Unnecessary shutdowns from room events cause avoidable downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch evidence unavailable during sterility assurance review
rows.plain: Make sure all required batch records are available when reviewers check whether the process kept the product free from harmful germs. This gives them the evidence needed to make that judgment.
rows.imp_rat: Required batch evidence unavailable during sterility review undermines the release decision basis (relevance) and risks releasing unproven product (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records and automated data capture make most required evidence available, with occasional gaps. Missing evidence is discovered at review, delaying disposition. Assembling required evidence is a procedural task needing organization more than deep skill. Unavailable evidence can force batch rejection, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the current isolator state and the state represented by qualification evidence
rows.plain: The chamber’s current condition should match the condition shown in the evidence used to approve it. Otherwise, the earlier test results may no longer prove that it is ready to use.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between current isolator state and the state in qualification evidence means the assurance no longer applies (relevance), risking operation outside validated bounds (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Periodic requalification and change control catch drift, but silent configuration changes create recurring uncertainty about current-vs-qualified state. Confirming current isolator state matches qualification evidence is periodic and not a delay-dominated concern. Judging whether the current state still reflects qualification evidence needs experienced engineers aware of undocumented changes. State mismatch can trigger costly requalification but is not a direct material spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between qualification conditions and GMP operating conditions
rows.plain: The conditions used to approve the equipment should match the conditions under which it will actually operate. This supports confidence that the equipment is safe and suitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between qualification and GMP operating conditions means the isolator is validated against unrepresentative conditions (relevance), risking real-use failure (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Worst-case qualification design and change control catch many mismatches, yet drift between qualified and actual operating states recurs and is imperfectly monitored. Comparing qualification conditions to real GMP operating conditions is a periodic review task, not primarily time-bound. Detecting mismatch between qualified and operating conditions requires expert knowledge of both validation and daily operations, a scarce combination. Undetected mismatch can force revalidation, a significant cost, but the review effort itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in ingress-prevention results across equivalent qualification runs
rows.plain: Equivalent approval tests should produce similar results for preventing contamination from entering. This helps show that the prevention method works reliably rather than only in one test.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in ingress-prevention results across qualification runs signals unreliable barrier performance (relevance); consequences are inconsistent assurance (severity) but indirect.
rows.sat_rat: Replicate-run expectations exist but genuine reproducibility of ingress-prevention results across equivalent runs is frequently marginal and hard to demonstrate. Demonstrating consistency across equivalent runs requires repeated full-length runs, extending qualification timelines substantially. Interpreting run-to-run variation and distinguishing signal from noise demands statistical and process expertise that is unevenly available. Variation forcing additional confirmatory runs multiplies material and labor cost with limited mitigation today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncontrolled variation between repeated qualification conditions
rows.plain: Repeated approval tests should be carried out under the same controlled conditions. This makes it possible to tell whether differences in results reflect real performance rather than changes in how the tests were done.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled variation between qualification conditions reduces confidence in reproducible ingress control (relevance); consequence is weakened assurance (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automation and recipe control constrain drift for engineered parameters, but manual and environmental variation between repeats persists as a real recurring gap. Controlling and re-establishing identical conditions between runs adds setup and verification time each cycle. Holding conditions truly constant depends on disciplined operators and engineers whose vigilance varies in practice. Uncontrolled condition drift causes invalidated runs and repeats that waste resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required operating states and aseptic interventions omitted from repeated evaluation
rows.plain: Repeat testing under every important operating condition and during each important sterile-handling action. This shows that the process keeps working reliably, not just once.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting operating states and interventions from repeated evaluation leaves reproducibility blind spots (relevance) that could hide condition-specific ingress (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Intervention matrices exist but repeated coverage of all required states is commonly incomplete, leaving recurring gaps. Repeating evaluation across all required states and interventions markedly lengthens the qualification campaign. Deciding which states warrant repeated evaluation needs experienced judgement that is scarce and variably applied. Omitting states from repeated evaluation risks costly late failures, only partly offset by current matrices.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of invalid ingress results caused by contamination introduced during evaluation
rows.plain: Have fewer failed tests caused by germs or other unwanted material being introduced during the test. This helps show that the process can produce reliable results over time.
rows.imp_rat: Invalid ingress results caused by contamination introduced during evaluation produce false conclusions (relevance) that could mask real ingress (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Trained personnel and controlled sampling reduce false positives, and this is a well-drilled competency, making it moderately well served. Contamination introduced during evaluation triggers repeat runs that add substantial delay. Aseptic technique during evaluation depends on highly trained operators whose consistency directly governs false results. Invalid results from evaluation-induced contamination waste whole runs, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the rate of degradation in ingress-prevention results across qualification cycles
rows.plain: Keep the ability to block unwanted entry from getting worse from one testing round to the next. This shows that the process continues to work reliably over time.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation of ingress-prevention results across cycles signals declining barrier reliability over time (relevance), risking eventual undetected failure (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Continued-qualification and trending programs exist but degradation across qualification cycles is often caught late, leaving this modestly served. Detecting degradation across cycles requires trend accumulation over time, inherently slow to reveal. Recognizing gradual performance degradation trends needs experienced analytical oversight that is not always present. Undetected degradation leads to late-stage failures with high remediation cost the status quo poorly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical production states excluded from candidate evidence
rows.plain: Fewer important stages of production should be left out of the evidence used to judge whether a solution fits. This helps show whether it will work in all relevant situations.
rows.imp_rat: Excluding critical production states from candidate evidence weakens completeness of the assurance case (relevance) and can hide risk (severity), but is documentary.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence requests focus on nominal cases and often exclude worst-case production states. Gathering candidate evidence for production states is bounded by vendor response. Knowing which critical states to demand evidence for requires expert framing. Missing evidence risks selecting an unsuitable unit, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance thresholds for sterility objectives
rows.plain: Set clear limits for deciding whether the product is still free of germs, so people know exactly what passes and what fails.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous sterility acceptance thresholds can lead to inconsistent pass/fail calls (relevance) and questionable release decisions (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pharmacopeial sterility criteria are well defined, so ambiguity here is relatively low and well served. Setting sterility acceptance thresholds is a bounded drafting task. Defining unambiguous sterility thresholds relies on scarce microbiology and regulatory expertise. Ambiguous thresholds cause dispute and rework rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination-relevant lifecycle states excluded from evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Fewer important stages in a product’s life should be left out when deciding how contamination will be checked. This helps ensure risks are considered from start to finish.
rows.imp_rat: Excluding contamination-relevant lifecycle states from criteria leaves gaps in evaluation coverage (relevance) that could miss risk windows (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle thinking exists but transient states like startup and cleaning are frequently under-covered in criteria. Enumerating lifecycle states is scheduled analytical work. Identifying all contamination-relevant lifecycle states requires broad process-lifecycle expertise. Excluded states cause late-discovered gaps with rework cost.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in contamination challenge conditions across barrier evaluations
rows.plain: Keep the contamination test conditions the same each time barriers are evaluated. This makes the results easier to compare and trust.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in challenge conditions across barrier evaluations makes results less comparable (relevance), weakening confidence in barrier performance (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Challenge conditions vary across evaluations because protocols are not uniformly enforced, undermining comparability. Standardizing challenge conditions across evaluations adds coordination time. Achieving consistent challenge conditions requires disciplined protocol control. Standardization reduces waste but demands upfront resource investment.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which microbiological acceptance limits exceed levels justified by product and process risk
rows.plain: Keep cleanliness limits from being set much higher than the product and process risks actually require. This avoids unnecessary restrictions while still protecting the product.
rows.imp_rat: Microbiological limits exceeding risk-justified levels means passing criteria that permit unsafe contamination (relevance), allowing bad product through (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance limits sometimes exceed risk-justified levels because derivation is judgement-based and inconsistently rigorous. Justifying limits against product risk adds upfront analysis time. Deriving risk-justified limits requires specialized risk-assessment expertise. Overly loose limits waste little directly but risk costly escapes; setting them is cheap.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of routine interventions and material transfers lacking explicit contamination-control acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Set clear contamination-acceptance rules for more routine interventions and material transfers. This helps people know what is safe enough when handling materials or entering the process.
rows.imp_rat: Routine interventions lacking explicit contamination-control acceptance criteria leave common risk activities ungoverned (relevance), enabling inconsistent practice (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Many routine interventions and transfers still lack explicit contamination-control acceptance criteria, a common documentation gap. Defining acceptance criteria for each intervention and transfer is time-consuming. Writing meaningful criteria per intervention requires process and microbiological expertise. Criteria development is low-cost but often incompletely done.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that baseline and post-exposure microbial counts are not comparable
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that germ counts taken before and after treatment cannot be fairly compared. Comparable counts are needed to tell whether the treatment worked.
rows.imp_rat: Non-comparable baseline vs post-exposure counts undermine the validity of ingress conclusions (relevance) and could hide real ingress (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized baseline/post-exposure protocols make counts largely comparable, though method variability still introduces occasional non-comparability. Baseline and post-exposure sampling follow set schedules with modest time impact. Ensuring comparability requires controlled technique but follows established protocols. Paired sampling adds routine, budgeted cost.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in pass-fail criteria for the required microbial reduction
rows.plain: Set clear pass-or-fail rules for how much harmful microbial life the cleaning process must remove or kill.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous pass-fail criteria for required microbial reduction can allow inconsistent efficacy calls (relevance), risking acceptance of inadequate decontamination (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Microbial-reduction pass-fail criteria are generally clear and standardized, with only occasional ambiguity in edge cases. Pass-fail criteria for microbial reduction are defined upfront with little delay. Standard log-reduction targets reduce interpretive ambiguity for practitioners. Defining these criteria is low-cost and routine.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in linking particulate-control evidence to microbial-ingress conclusions
rows.plain: Evidence about controlling dust and particles should clearly support any conclusion about germs entering the area. This prevents conclusions about germ contamination from relying on evidence that does not directly support them.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty linking particulate evidence to microbial-ingress conclusions weakens inference validity (relevance); mistaken conclusions could pass risk (severity) but indirectly.
rows.sat_rat: The link between particulate control and microbial ingress is inherently uncertain, leaving conclusions weakly supported. Linking particulate to microbial evidence adds interpretive analysis time. Inferring microbial ingress from particulate data requires specialist judgement about surrogacy relationships. Particulate monitoring is cheap, but the interpretive linkage adds little direct cost.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that assessment conclusions rely on unverified contamination-risk assumptions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a safety review depends on guesses about the risk of germs that have not been checked. This makes the review more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Assessment conclusions resting on unverified contamination-risk assumptions can yield false assurance (relevance) and let real risk through (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conclusions often rest on unverified contamination-risk assumptions because verification is inconsistently enforced. Verifying assumptions adds analytical time to the assessment. Recognizing and testing unverified risk assumptions demands experienced critical judgement. Assumption verification adds modest analytical cost.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of distortion in risk prioritization from underweighting high-consequence contamination scenarios
rows.plain: Make sure risks involving serious contamination are not rated too low just because they may be less common.
rows.imp_rat: Underweighting high-consequence scenarios distorts risk prioritization (relevance), potentially leaving the worst hazards under-controlled (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Risk prioritization is often distorted by underweighting rare high-consequence contamination events, a persistent bias. Weighting high-consequence scenarios properly adds risk-analysis time. Avoiding underweighting of severe scenarios depends on expert risk-prioritization judgement. Risk-prioritization work is low direct cost.

rows.stk: EHS, facilities, and waste management teams
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual hazardous contamination remaining on isolator components presented for recycling
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous contamination on components sent to recycling matters for downstream worker safety, but this is an end-of-life disposal concern far removed from the isolator's core containment job during production, keeping relevance low.
rows.sat_rat: Component-level decon for recycling is inconsistently applied, so residual contamination on recycled parts is a recurring concern. Certifying components clean enough for recycling requires additional testing time. Deciding when parts are decontaminated for recycling relies on QA expertise. Testing plus potential rework or disposal of failing parts adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containment performance limits lacking measurable acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Containment performance limits lacking measurable acceptance criteria weakens verifiability, somewhat relevant to defining success; severity is low-moderate because it creates ambiguity rather than an operational failure.
rows.sat_rat: Vague or qualitative performance limits without numeric acceptance criteria remain common, undermining consistent verification. Defining measurable acceptance criteria for each performance limit adds specification time upfront. Translating containment performance into measurable acceptance criteria requires specialized test and metrology knowledge that is scarce. Setting criteria costs little but their absence causes expensive disputes at acceptance testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of adjacent isolator components damaged during repair
rows.imp_rat: Damaging adjacent components during repair is a rework/cost issue peripheral to the operational containment job (low relevance), though collateral damage can compromise a nearby boundary component (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no systematic barrier preventing incidental damage, so outcomes vary technician to technician. Careful disassembly to protect neighbors slows the job but is absorbed within normal maintenance windows. Avoiding collateral damage in the tight, sealed geometry of an isolator relies almost entirely on the technician's care and dexterity with few physical safeguards. Adjacent-component damage means extra spare parts and rework, a real but occasional expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a correctly functioning protective control incorrectly fails verification
rows.imp_rat: A correctly functioning control failing verification is a false-negative that wastes effort but does not endanger containment; both relevance and severity are low because the outcome is conservative rework rather than exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Defined test procedures and setpoint tolerances limit false fails, but marginal calibration and test-rig variability still produce them. A false verification failure forces retesting and troubleshooting, extending the visit. Interpreting borderline control responses needs a competent technician to avoid wrongly condemning a good control. Unnecessary component swaps waste parts and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified enclosure gas composition before use
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified gas composition before use affects readiness quality (relevance) but is typically verified before work begins, limiting downstream consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Analyzers and recirculation reliably establish target composition before use, with only occasional sensor-drift deviations. Achieving target gas composition before use requires purge time that delays start. Composition setup is largely automated and gauge-verified, low skill burden. Gas consumption to reach composition is a recurring operating expense.

rows.source_job: Purge enclosure to establish required atmosphere
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of ambient atmosphere remaining in poorly swept enclosure regions at purge completion
rows.imp_rat: Ambient atmosphere trapped in poorly swept regions at purge completion degrades inert-environment quality in localized zones (relevance); consequences are usually a rework or dead-spot issue rather than acute harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Oxygen/moisture monitoring at set points confirms bulk purge success, but stagnant pockets can retain ambient atmosphere undetected between measurement points. Purge cycles to reach target atmosphere can be lengthy, especially for large or geometrically complex enclosures. Judging adequate sweeping of dead zones relies on operator experience since flow distribution is not directly visible. Purge gas consumption is a notable recurring cost, and extended purging to clear pockets wastes more gas.

rows.source_job: Disconnect and depressurise gas supply lines
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual pressure remains in a gas line when its connection is opened
rows.imp_rat: Residual pressure in a gas line when opened is a maintenance safety detail peripheral to the core isolation function (relevance), though it can cause sudden release or injury when it happens (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard depressurize-and-verify procedures with gauges are well established, making trapped-pressure surprises uncommon though not impossible with blocked or unmonitored line segments. Verifying line depressurization via gauges and bleed steps is quick and adds little to the decommissioning timeline. Confirming zero residual pressure before breaking a connection is basic lockout practice that trained technicians execute routinely. Bleed valves and pressure gauges are cheap, so serving this need imposes negligible material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of degraded fans, duct obstructions, or leaking flow paths remaining undetected during servicing
rows.imp_rat: Degraded fans, duct obstructions, or leaking flow paths left undetected during servicing can erode containment airflow over time (moderate relevance) with gradual rather than immediate consequences (moderate severity), scoring in the middle range.
rows.sat_rat: Airflow and pressure readings catch major degradation, but gradual fan wear and concealed duct issues frequently remain undetected until performance drops noticeably. Airflow velocity and pressure checks are quick, but tracing hidden duct obstructions or flow-path leaks can extend servicing. Diagnosing degraded fans and internal flow-path leaks demands experienced airflow-savvy technicians and is easy to overlook. Anemometers and pressure gauges are standard tools, so measurement cost is low.

rows.source_job: Define and document material transfer protocols
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of foreseeable abnormal transfer conditions lacking documented response actions
rows.imp_rat: Missing documented response actions for abnormal transfer conditions raises risk during rare events, giving moderate severity, but relevance is limited since it is a procedural-documentation gap rather than a direct containment mechanism.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage of foreseeable abnormal transfer conditions is chronically incomplete, leaving operators to improvise when deviations occur. Documenting abnormal-condition responses is often deferred, so it rarely pressures the schedule until late. Foreseeing abnormal transfer conditions and specifying responses requires deep failure-mode expertise that few authors fully possess. Adding contingency documentation is inexpensive but frequently under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interventions omitted from the pharmaceutical isolator run record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting interventions from the run record is a documentation-completeness gap only loosely tied to the physical containment job (low relevance), with severity driven by audit/data-integrity exposure (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual or paper-based logging remains common, so interventions are routinely missed and the record's completeness is a recurring GMP weakness. Logging each intervention during a run interrupts workflow, and operators under time pressure often defer entries until later, when memory is imperfect. Complete intervention capture depends heavily on operator diligence and understanding of what constitutes a recordable event, a skill gap that persists. Omitted interventions surface at review or audit as deviations, driving costly investigations and potential batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a completed pharmaceutical isolator run record is unavailable for quality review
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable completed run record for quality review is a records-management reliability issue not central to the physical containment CFJ (low relevance), though missing records create compliance exposure (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document management and increasingly electronic records make availability fairly dependable, with residual loss risk mainly in hybrid paper workflows. Retrieving a completed record for review is usually quick where electronic batch systems exist, though paper archives can add retrieval delay. Archiving and retrieval follow defined document-control procedures requiring little specialized skill once in place. A lost record can force a batch hold or rework, but robust backup practices make this an infrequent cost event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood the operator is unable to actuate equipment controls through the sealed enclosure
rows.imp_rat: Inability to actuate controls through the sealed enclosure is an ergonomic/usability failure that impedes operation but rarely breaches sterility directly, moderate on both proxies. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: External HMIs and well-placed internal controls mean operators can almost always actuate equipment, though glove-mediated touchscreen use occasionally fails. Glove-port and half-suit access to controls adds only minor delay for reaching through the barrier. Actuating controls through gloves demands dexterity and practice, especially for small buttons or touchscreens. Failed actuation rarely causes material loss directly, so cost exposure is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that usable instrument readings are unavailable or unclear during processing
rows.imp_rat: Instrument readings being unavailable or unclear during processing hampers operator confidence and decisions, an ambiguity error partly overlapping monitoring reliability; consequences are usually a pause rather than a breach.
rows.sat_rat: Modern HMIs present readings consistently, with unclear or unavailable periods being uncommon and short-lived. Instrument readings are continuously displayed and only briefly unclear during faults, keeping unavailable duration short. Interpreting standard displays needs little skill, though ambiguous readings occasionally require judgement. Brief unclear readings seldom translate into material loss.

rows.source_job: Integrate process equipment within isolator enclosure
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the installed equipment makes required process manipulations unreachable through the glove ports
rows.imp_rat: Equipment making manipulations unreachable through glove ports is an ergonomic/usability defect somewhat peripheral to containment integrity (lower relevance), with modest severity as it slows work rather than breaching containment (lower severity).
rows.sat_rat: Ergonomic mock-ups and glove-port reach testing reliably catch most reach problems though awkward-but-reachable manipulations still slip through. Reach studies during preparation take modest time and rarely delay the stage. Verifying that all manipulations are reachable through glove ports leans on experienced operators and anthropometric judgement that not all teams have. Fixing unreachable equipment late is expensive, but mock-up checking is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an intact glove or sleeve assembly incorrectly failing the integrity test
rows.imp_rat: A false-fail on an intact glove integrity test causes unnecessary rework rather than a containment breach, so it is only modestly relevant; severity is low since the error errs on the conservative side and harms throughput, not safety.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay glove testers are mature and reasonably repeatable, but sensitivity settings and fit still cause occasional false rejects. A false glove failure triggers unnecessary re-tests and replacement, adding downtime. Automated glove-leak testers reduce operator judgement, though setup and mounting still require care. False failures waste good gloves and labor, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the candidate isolator configuration falls outside the scope of its certifications
rows.imp_rat: A configuration falling outside certification scope is a procurement/compliance mismatch discovered before or during qualification; relevance to the running containment job is indirect and the issue is typically correctable, so both proxies stay moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration-versus-scope mismatches are subtle and easily overlooked, so this remains a moderate recurring risk. Confirming the specific configuration falls within certification scope adds a focused review step. Interpreting whether a customized configuration stays within certification scope requires expert reading of standards and certificate annexes. The check is low-cost but a scope gap can void warranty and force recertification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in classifying observed seal wear or damage against maintenance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty classifying seal wear against maintenance criteria is a judgment-ambiguity error that mainly affects maintenance scheduling; it only indirectly threatens containment and is generally recoverable, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Absent quantitative wear standards, classification varies widely between technicians, leaving this a chronic ambiguity. Resolving classification uncertainty causes deliberation delays during inspection. Judging seal wear against maintenance criteria is inherently subjective and leans hard on individual experience. Uncertainty leads to conservative over-replacement or risky retention, both costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that monitoring-point placement disturbs airflow within the isolator chamber
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring-point placement disturbing chamber airflow is a design-detail error that can bias readings or create dead zones, but its effect on actual containment is subtle and correctable, keeping both proxies moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Smoke studies and validated placement practices generally confirm probes do not disturb airflow, making this a well-controlled and infrequent problem. Positioning monitoring points to avoid airflow disturbance is a design-time consideration adding no operational delay. Placing probes without disrupting unidirectional flow requires aerodynamic and contamination-control expertise, though guidance exists. Probe mounting adds negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of monitoring points that conflict with process equipment, material flow, or operator interventions
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring points conflicting with equipment or interventions is a layout/instability nuisance peripheral to the core containment function (lower relevance), with modest consequences typically resolved by repositioning (lower severity).
rows.sat_rat: 3D layout reviews help, but conflicts with actual operator interventions and material flow are frequently discovered only in use. Detecting monitoring-point conflicts during preparation adds review time but is planned for. Anticipating clashes between monitoring points, equipment, material flow, and interventions demands cross-disciplinary layout expertise that is scarce. Late relocation of a conflicting monitoring point can incur rework cost but is usually modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of contamination introduced to processed samples during chamber unloading
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during unloading can spoil processed samples, giving moderate relevance and severity, though it is a handling-stage error ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: With largely manual unloading and open-chamber exposure, contamination during unloading remains a frequent, poorly controlled problem. Careful clean unloading adds handling time, and contamination-driven rework costs more time later. Avoiding contamination during unloading relies almost entirely on operator technique, gloving, and clean-handling discipline. Contaminated samples may be scrapped, and cleanroom consumables add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of admitted process gas contaminated by moisture, oxygen, or particulates
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated admitted process gas (moisture, oxygen, particulates) can compromise processed material, giving it moderate relevance and severity, though it ranks below the higher-priority batch items.
rows.sat_rat: Gas filtration and purge lines control contamination well when maintained, but degraded filters or lines allow moisture and particulates through. Purging with clean gas and using filters/driers adds setup and line-conditioning time. Ensuring gas purity depends on the operator maintaining filters, driers, and gas grade correctly. High-purity gas, inline filters, and moisture traps are recurring consumable costs.

rows.source_job: Inspect vacuum system seals and plumbing
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of existing seal and vacuum-path defects remaining undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected seal and vacuum-path defects can degrade vacuum integrity, but this inspection-stage error is moderate in relevance and its consequences are typically caught before catastrophic failure.
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo inspection is largely manual and visual, so a meaningful fraction of incipient seal and path defects go undetected until they cause a leak. Thorough seal and vacuum-path inspection is time-consuming when done properly and is often abbreviated under schedule pressure. Detecting subtle seal degradation and hidden vacuum-path defects depends heavily on an experienced inspector's eye and technique. Visual inspection is cheap, but missed defects that cause later failures are expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of components dropped or damaged inside the processing zone during an intervention
rows.imp_rat: Dropped or damaged components during intervention cause localized loss (severity) but are tangential to boundary ingress prevention (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Drops and component damage occur with some regularity given restricted isolator ergonomics. Recovering from a dropped or damaged component consumes intervention time. Avoiding drops within the constrained glove-port workspace demands dexterity and care. Dropped and damaged components are direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product containers or closures damaged during operator manipulation
rows.imp_rat: Damaged containers/closures during manipulation risk product loss and potential seal breach (severity) but are peripheral to zone isolation (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Container and closure damage during manipulation occurs occasionally, controlled but not eliminated by technique. Damaged containers must be removed and replaced, costing line time. Gentle manipulation to avoid damaging containers is a routine operator skill. Damaged containers and closures are direct scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical interior surfaces are damaged during disruption response
rows.imp_rat: Damage to interior surfaces during disruption response can create contamination niches (severity) but is a secondary concern to the primary isolation job (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Interior surface damage during disruption response is infrequent but a real risk under time pressure. Damage to interior surfaces during response forces additional repair or requalification time. Avoiding damage to critical surfaces during urgent response demands careful, skilled handling. Surface damage can require costly repair or component replacement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among microbial, particulate, and barrier-integrity indications during aseptic filling
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among microbial, particulate, and barrier indications complicates a correct in-process call (severity); an interpretation issue removed from prevention (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Disagreement among indication types occurs and is not systematically reconciled, leaving ambiguous contamination pictures. Reconciling disagreeing indications adds assessment delay. Resolving conflicts among microbial, particulate, and integrity data requires strong analytical skill. Investigation and potential holds from conflicts drive cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination observations assigned to the wrong isolator zone or filling interval
rows.imp_rat: Assigning observations to the wrong zone or interval misdirects investigation and disposition (severity); a data-attribution error removed from prevention (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Observations are mostly attributed correctly, but zone or interval mislabeling occurs, muddying investigations. Misattributed observations require rework to reassign, adding time. Correct zone and interval assignment relies on operator recordkeeping care. Misattribution can trigger unnecessary investigations, a cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the barrier-breach extent is underestimated during response assessment
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating breach extent during response can leave contaminated product undetected (severity); an assessment-judgment error downstream of detection (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Breach extent is often underestimated because contamination spread is not directly measurable, a poorly-served need. Assessing breach extent quickly is hard and adds investigation time. Estimating true breach extent demands significant investigative judgement and experience. Underestimation causes downstream losses far exceeding assessment cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of change in recoverable sample content between collection and evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Change in recoverable sample content between collection and evaluation can bias microbial counts (severity), an analytical validity issue somewhat removed from ingress prevention (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Validated hold times and transport controls keep sample content change reasonably bounded. Prompt transport limits change but hold times add scheduling constraints. Proper sample handling and hold conditions need lab discipline. Controlled transport and storage carry modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting contamination indicators preventing a clear isolated-zone risk judgment
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting contamination indicators blocking a clear risk judgment can delay or misdirect response (severity); an interpretation problem removed from prevention (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting indicators between viable, nonviable and physical data are common and hard to reconcile, undermining clear judgements. Reconciling conflicting indicators extends the time to reach a judgement. Resolving conflicting contamination signals demands strong analytical and microbiological expertise. Indecision stalls disposition, tying up product and capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from a contamination-relevant isolator change to its inclusion in the overseer's risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: Delay in reflecting a contamination-relevant change in risk assessment leaves decisions based on stale information (severity), an oversight-timeliness gap (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Change-control links changes to reassessment reliably in principle, but timeliness of updating the living risk assessment is inconsistent. The lag from an isolator change to its inclusion in risk assessment depends on periodic review cadence, often days or weeks. Judging relevance for inclusion needs experienced assessment but is procedurally defined. Delayed inclusion lets unassessed risk persist, raising exposure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that contamination-relevant deviations remain unresolved before informing the isolated-zone risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contamination-relevant deviations delaying the risk assessment leave hazards unaddressed longer (severity); an oversight-timeliness concern (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation backlogs are common, so contamination-relevant items frequently linger before informing the assessment. Deviations often remain open for extended periods before feeding the risk assessment, a real duration problem. Prioritizing which deviations to resolve first needs quality judgement. Prolonged unresolved deviations delay risk-informed decisions, adding holding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unresolved conflict among monitoring results, batch records, and personnel accounts
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflict among monitoring, records, and accounts hampers a correct contamination judgment (severity); an investigative-clarity gap removed from prevention (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: There is no systematic reconciliation tool, so conflicts among sources routinely remain partly unresolved. Resolving conflicts among data sources is a deliberative task, not primarily time-bound. Reconciling monitoring data, batch records and personnel accounts requires experienced investigative judgement. Unresolved conflict can lead to wrong disposition and its associated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity about which sterility assurance threshold applies to a batch
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity over which sterility threshold applies to a batch can produce an incorrect release decision (severity); a criteria-clarity issue somewhat removed from the boundary (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Product specifications usually define the applicable threshold clearly, so ambiguity is uncommon. Threshold ambiguity is resolved during review, not a delay issue. Knowing which sterility-assurance threshold applies requires regulatory and product knowledge. Applying the wrong threshold risks either waste or unsafe release, both costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in contamination-control limits, escalation triggers, and decision accountabilities
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in limits, escalation triggers, and accountabilities can delay or misdirect response (severity); a governance-clarity gap somewhat removed from the boundary (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Established contamination-control SOPs and alert/action limits cover routine cases, but escalation-decision ownership remains inconsistently defined, producing recurring gaps. Escalation triggers when documented allow quick response, but ambiguity forces time-consuming clarification cycles. Defining unambiguous limits and accountabilities leans on experienced quality leaders and is error-prone when delegated to less senior staff. Unclear accountabilities lead to duplicated or missed effort, a modest resource cost rather than a direct spend.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial ingress after exposure is misattributed to inadequate decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing post-exposure ingress to inadequate decontamination distorts root-cause understanding (severity) but is an analytical, not preventive, error (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Post-exposure contamination is frequently misattributed because current methods cannot cleanly separate the causes. Distinguishing decontamination failure from ingress prolongs investigation time. Correct attribution demands expert forensic microbiology that is scarce. Additional testing to disambiguate causes adds cost, applied inconsistently.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regenerated catalyst fails to restore specified moisture and oxygen conditions
rows.imp_rat: Regenerated catalyst failing to restore moisture/oxygen conditions is a subsystem reliability issue (relevance) with recoverable consequences since conditions can be re-checked (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Post-regeneration moisture and oxygen readings confirm success in most cases, though degraded catalyst can under-perform without immediate obvious signs. Catalyst regeneration is a scheduled, hours-long cycle whose duration is well understood and planned around. Running and confirming a successful regeneration is largely automated, requiring modest operator oversight. A failed regeneration wastes the cycle's energy and delays return to service, but catalyst reuse still saves replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of process residue and particulate contamination remaining on interior chamber surfaces after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residue and particulate left after cleaning directly threatens the sterile/contained interior (relevance) and can compromise product batches (severity), but this batch's ranking caps importance at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Manual cleaning with visual and swab verification generally works but leaves recurring residue in corners and hard-to-reach zones. Interior cleaning and residue removal is a lengthy manual wipe-down process that dominates the maintenance window. Achieving residue-free surfaces depends on technique and thoroughness, with real operator-to-operator variation. Cleaning agents and labour are the main cost, and inadequate cleaning triggers costly re-cleans or contamination events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between calibrated atmosphere-sensor readings and approved reference values
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between calibrated atmosphere-sensor readings and reference values affects atmosphere trust (relevance) but is managed within calibration routines with limited immediate harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established multi-point calibration against approved references keeps sensor deviation small and well controlled in most cases. Sensor calibration against a reference is a defined procedure completed within a routine service window. Calibrating oxygen and moisture sensors follows standard steps that trained staff execute consistently. Reference gases and standards cost something, but calibration is inexpensive versus the errors it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contamination remaining in the gas circulation lines after purging
rows.imp_rat: Contamination remaining in gas circulation lines after purging affects atmosphere quality (relevance) but is a contained maintenance step with detectable effects (severity); bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Purge-to-setpoint practice usually clears lines, but dead legs and adsorbed contaminants can leave residual traces below the analyzer's reach. Purging gas circulation lines to acceptable levels is a time-dependent flush that can be lengthy to reach targets. Judging adequate purge endpoint relies on operator reading of analyzers, a moderate skill demand. Extended purging consumes inert gas, and residual contamination can force repeat purges that waste more gas.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of shortfall in restored moisture-removal and oxygen-removal capacity after regeneration
rows.imp_rat: Shortfall in restored moisture/oxygen removal capacity after regeneration is a gradual performance degradation (relevance) that is monitorable and correctable before harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Atmosphere pull-down checks reveal gross shortfalls, but partial capacity loss can persist within tolerance and degrade later performance. Verifying restored removal capacity requires a stabilization period that lengthens return-to-service. Judging whether capacity is truly restored versus marginally short takes experienced interpretation of pull-down trends. Shortfall means premature catalyst replacement or repeat cycles, adding avoidable material and energy cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of gas flow or pressure from specified limits after circulation-equipment service
rows.imp_rat: Gas flow/pressure deviation after circulation-equipment service is a post-maintenance verification concern (relevance) that is typically caught during recommissioning (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Direct gauge readings against setpoints make deviations readily detectable and correctable, keeping this fairly reliably served. Flow and pressure verification after circulation service is a quick instrument-read step with little delay. Adjusting to specified flow and pressure is routine for trained technicians using onboard gauges. Out-of-spec operation wastes gas and energy, but the check and adjustment themselves are low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering the sterile product zone during feed-through maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering the sterile product zone is a narrow maintenance-specific event (relevance) but contamination consequences can be severe for product safety (severity); held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Correct lubricant type and controlled quantity usually prevent ingress, though over-application incidents still recur. Careful lubricant application at feed-throughs adds only minor time to the service task. Preventing lubricant migration into the sterile zone hinges on the technician's precision and restraint, a skill-dependent finesse. Lubricant is cheap, but contamination of the product zone can force expensive cleaning and re-qualification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an energy or material connection remains active when physical decommissioning begins
rows.imp_rat: An active energy or material connection during decommissioning is peripheral to routine isolation operation (relevance) but can cause serious safety harm when it occurs (severity); still bounded by the 3.5 importance ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and isolation-verification practice makes a live connection at decommissioning start a rare event, so this is one of the better-served needs on the list. Lockout/tagout isolation adds modest, planned time before physical decommissioning begins. LOTO of energy and material connections is a standardized, trained competency present in virtually every industrial decommissioning crew. Locks, tags, and verification effort are inexpensive relative to the incident cost they prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory or safety requirements omitted from the compliance review
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulatory requirements from a compliance review is an administrative documentation task (low relevance to the physical isolation job) but can carry compliance exposure when missed (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature regulatory checklists and URS templates cover most common requirements, yet novel or overlapping regulations are periodically omitted. Compliance reviews follow established checklists that speed the assessment, though comprehensive review of all applicable standards still consumes weeks. Complete regulatory mapping (GMP, ATEX, containment, ergonomics) depends on experienced QA/regulatory staff whose coverage varies by organization. Missed requirements surface late and drive costly rework, but structured reviews are relatively inexpensive to run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical isolation pathways lacking test evidence representative of intended fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Critical pathways lacking representative test evidence is a documentation/qualification gap rather than an active operational error (relevance); it raises compliance and assurance risk but does not itself breach containment (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard FAT often uses surrogate or idealized conditions rather than actual process representative ones, so critical isolation pathways frequently lack test evidence matching real intended use. Factory acceptance testing for isolation pathways is scheduled but representative fill-finish condition testing adds significant lead time that acquisition timelines rarely accommodate. Designing test protocols that faithfully replicate intended fill-finish conditions requires scarce specialist containment engineers whose judgement varies between projects. Representative testing is expensive and often value-engineered out or deferred to post-installation, leaving gaps at acquisition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between calibrated monitoring-sensor readings and approved reference values
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between calibrated monitoring-sensor readings and reference values affects measurement trust but is a maintenance/QA concern rather than core operation (relevance), and consequences are contained if caught in calibration cycles (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceable calibration against approved references is a mature, tightly controlled practice, so deviations between readings and reference values are uncommon and well bounded. Sensor calibration against references is a routine, scheduled procedure with predictable duration. Calibration follows documented procedures and traceable standards, reducing reliance on rare expertise. Reference standards and calibration services carry cost, but the practice is well amortised and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an enclosure utility is connected to an incorrect source or operating range
rows.imp_rat: Connecting an enclosure utility to an incorrect source is an occasional setup error (relevance) but can damage equipment or create hazards when it happens (severity), so it stays low-relevance, moderate-severity under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Labelling, keyed connectors and P&ID checks make mis-connection fairly rare, though occasional range or source errors still slip through under time pressure. Utility connection and verification is a quick check within normal preparation activities. Correct source and range matching relies on installers reading labels and specs, a modest but real attention demand. Verification consumes negligible material, though a wrong connection can damage equipment expensively.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of enclosed-equipment measurements outside tolerance because of airflow or vibration
rows.imp_rat: Enclosed-equipment measurements drifting out of tolerance from airflow or vibration is a narrow instrument-condition issue (relevance) with contained, detectable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Enclosed-equipment measurements are intermittently thrown out of tolerance by airflow and vibration that are hard to fully control. Airflow and vibration effects rarely add time but can force remeasurement. Diagnosing airflow/vibration-induced measurement error requires expert troubleshooting. Vibration isolation and airflow management add moderate equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of processed-output packages with incomplete seals before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete seals on output packages relate to downstream transfer rather than the enclosure's core isolation job (relevance), though they risk sterility loss when they occur (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated sealers with checks catch most defects, but incomplete seals still slip through inspection intermittently. Seal verification of output packages adds a checking step before transfer. Detecting incomplete seals depends on operator inspection or sealer settings, a moderate skill point. Rejected or resealed packages waste material and product.

rows.source_job: Cycle transfer airlock through isolation sequence
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of airlock cycles failing to reach specified evacuation or refill endpoints
rows.imp_rat: Airlock cycles failing to reach evacuation/refill endpoints degrade transfer integrity central to isolation (relevance), though a failed cycle is typically detected and re-run with limited harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated endpoint detection makes most cycles succeed, but seal wear and marginal setpoints still cause recurring cycle failures. Failed airlock cycles force re-cycling that directly wastes transfer time. Airlock operation is automated and requires little skill once set up. Re-cycles waste inert gas and vacuum energy, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between displayed atmosphere readings and actual chamber conditions during use
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between displayed and actual atmosphere readings undermines operator trust in the isolation chamber's controlled environment (relevance), and false readings could allow undetected contamination or unsafe conditions (severity); capped by batch ceiling at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Sensors drift between calibrations, so displayed readings can diverge from true chamber conditions with only periodic verification. Reading displayed values is instantaneous, adding no time. Interpreting sensor accuracy and drift requires some trained judgement. Calibrated sensors and periodic verification carry recurring calibration cost.

rows.source_job: Control gas composition circulating through enclosure
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of gas-composition nonuniformity across enclosure work zones
rows.imp_rat: Gas-composition nonuniformity across work zones affects process consistency and is a refinement of environment quality (relevance); consequences are localized process variation rather than acute failure (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Single-point sensors often miss dead zones, so composition nonuniformity across the enclosure is a real and under-monitored gap. Achieving uniformity across zones takes recirculation time but little active effort. Uniformity depends on enclosure design rather than operator skill. Better circulation blowers and baffling raise cost modestly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from enclosure alarm activation until the operator initiates corrective action
rows.imp_rat: Delay from alarm to operator corrective action is an operational response-time issue (relevance) that matters mainly in escalation cases (severity), keeping it modest under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms are generally effective at prompting action, though alarm fatigue and unclear priorities can delay correct response. The need is response latency, and audible/visual alarms prompt reasonably prompt operator action. Recognizing and responding to alarms is trained but depends on operator attentiveness. Alarm systems are standard and inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recipient rejects the isolator because its contamination status or decontamination history is unresolved
rows.imp_rat: A recipient rejecting the isolator over unresolved decontamination history is a transfer/logistics disruption; it is peripheral to the operational containment job and the consequence is a delayed handoff rather than a safety breach.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete or disputed decontamination records still cause recipients to reject units, making this an unreliable handoff. Assembling decon history and status for the recipient adds administrative lead time. Producing acceptable contamination documentation requires QA and regulatory literacy. Rejection means repeat decon and re-shipping, a costly rework loop.

rows.source_job: Assess residual value of the isolator
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about residual contamination that could restrict resale, donation, parts recovery, or recycling
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about residual contamination restricting resale, donation, or recycling is an asset-disposition concern at end of life, largely disconnected from the active containment job, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Poorly documented residual status frequently blocks resale, donation and parts recovery, a persistent unresolved gap. Clarifying residual status for resale or recycling adds investigative delay. Assessing what residues could restrict downstream use needs specialist toxicology and materials judgement. Uncertainty forces conservative scrapping of otherwise recoverable value, wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in test conditions underlying candidate isolator containment claims
rows.imp_rat: Variation in test conditions behind vendor containment claims affects the reliability of specification comparison during selection; it is a procurement-evaluation error peripheral to the operating job with limited direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Test conditions underlying vendor containment claims vary widely and are rarely normalized, leaving buyers comparing inconsistent data. Scrutinizing the test conditions behind containment claims is slow and often deprioritized under schedule pressure. Detecting non-standard or favorable test conditions requires deep containment-testing expertise that few buyers have. The analysis is cheap, but accepting claims from inconsistent test conditions risks costly performance shortfalls.

rows.source_job: Define and document material transfer protocols
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material, transfer-direction, or port scenarios with ambiguous instructions
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous instructions for material/transfer/port scenarios is a documentation-clarity concern with lower relevance to the physical containment job, and severity is modest since ambiguities are usually clarified in practice (lower severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard SOP formats reduce ambiguity, yet direction- and port-specific scenarios remain a recurring source of confusion. Clarifying ambiguous instructions during preparation adds review time but no bottleneck. Writing unambiguous material, direction, and port instructions demands careful technical-writing skill that is inconsistently applied. Rewriting ambiguous instructions costs little beyond authoring effort.

rows.stk: Receiving/logistics staff and procurement or quality personnel
rows.source_job: Unpack, inspect, and verify delivered isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of contamination present on the delivered isolator after unpacking
rows.imp_rat: Contamination on the delivered isolator after unpacking is caught by installation cleaning and qualification, making it peripheral with moderate, recoverable consequences. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming clean-and-inspect practice reduces delivered contamination, but variability in shipping and packaging means residual contamination is commonly encountered. Unpacking, wipe-down and inspection of a delivered isolator is a defined but time-consuming receiving step. Effective decontamination wipe-down of a delivered unit follows standard procedures needing modest skill. Wiping agents and labour for incoming cleaning are low-cost routine expenses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading decontaminant outside the isolator approved specifications
rows.imp_rat: Loading decontaminant outside approved specs could impair the decon cycle and leave contamination (moderate severity), but it is a procedural preparation step peripheral to containment structure (low relevance). Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Approved-material lists and procurement controls make wrong-decontaminant loading uncommon, though human substitution errors still occasionally occur without a hard interlock. Verifying decontaminant against approved specs is a brief check before loading. It relies on the operator reading labels and following the approved material list, which is trainable but attention-dependent. Correct consumables are a normal supply cost with little waste when procedures are followed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual contamination exceeds acceptance limits when the isolator is cleared for disposal
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination exceeding limits at disposal poses real exposure risk (moderate severity), but disposal clearance is a peripheral end-of-life task relative to the isolator's operational containment job (low relevance). Held at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Swab-and-test clearance is standard practice but sampling only spot-checks surfaces, so exceedances can pass undetected and results vary. Clearance sampling and lab turnaround delay the disposal decision by days. Setting and interpreting acceptance limits and swab results requires qualified toxicology/EHS judgement. Analytical testing and any re-cleaning on a failed result add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified acceptance tests producing results outside agreed functional or containment criteria
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance tests outside criteria signal potential containment gaps (moderate relevance), yet failing tests are caught in qualification before use, limiting real-world consequence (modest severity). Capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature, standardized FAT/SAT protocols with predefined acceptance criteria are a widely deployed practice that reliably catches out-of-spec results before shipment or handover. Acceptance testing (FAT/SAT) follows scheduled protocols but reruns of out-of-criteria tests extend timelines significantly. Executing and interpreting containment and functional acceptance tests requires trained validation personnel and supplier specialists. Failed acceptance tests trigger rework and retest costs, but structured FAT/SAT protocols contain most of this expense.

rows.stk: Enterprise | Quality / Regulatory Specialist | As needed
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable safety, containment, and regulatory requirements omitted from the compliance assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting applicable safety/containment/regulatory requirements from the compliance assessment is largely an administrative/documentation defect (lower relevance to the functional job) though it can create compliance exposure if a real gap is missed (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established compliance checklists and regulatory frameworks cover the common requirements well, but coverage still depends on the assessor's diligence so omissions recur. Assembling the applicable regulatory and containment requirement set is a time-consuming manual literature and standards search across cGMP, EU Annex 1, and OEB references. Correctly scoping which safety, containment, and regulatory requirements apply demands specialist regulatory and containment expertise that few individuals fully possess. Omitted requirements surface as costly post-award compliance gaps, though the assessment work itself carries limited direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of utility lines connected to incorrect isolator ports
rows.imp_rat: Utility lines on wrong ports could impair function or safety (some severity), but this is a one-time installation setup error, peripheral to ongoing containment (low relevance). Held below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling, keyed fittings, and commissioning checklists make incorrect connections reliably caught, though ambiguous ports still cause occasional errors. Connecting utility lines is a defined installation step verified quickly against P&IDs. Correct port connection relies on fitter reading labels and drawings, with keyed/color-coded connectors reducing skill dependence. A misconnection caught early costs little; an undetected one can damage equipment, but rework cost is generally low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator monitoring instruments remaining outside protocol tolerance after adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring instruments remaining outside protocol tolerance after adjustment degrades measurement confidence (moderate-low relevance) and could mask a real excursion (moderate severity), but it is a calibration-maintenance issue, scoring lower.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable calibration and documented tolerance checks make out-of-tolerance instruments reliably caught and corrected before use. Instrument calibration and adjustment against reference standards is a routine, quickly scheduled task during preparation. Calibration follows documented procedures with traceable references, requiring only standard metrology competence. Recalibration consumes modest technician time and reference-standard use, a low recurring cost.

rows.source_job: Install and configure isolator control software
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of site-specific operating parameters entered outside approved values
