rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying surface residue or reflections as panel cracks causes false rejects during inspection, moderately relevant; the consequence is unnecessary rework, not containment loss. Capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: False-positive misclassifications remain common because typical practice offers no objective discrimination aid, forcing subjective calls that vary technician to technician. A careful visual inspection under proper lighting takes only minutes, so time is not the binding constraint here. Distinguishing a genuine crack from a surface reflection, water spot, or residue depends heavily on the individual technician's trained eye and experience, which is exactly the scarce judgement that fails today. The status quo relies on cheap handheld lights and cleaning rags rather than costly imaging, so material cost is low even though false calls waste effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting an incompatible glove or sleeve assembly for the isolator
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an incompatible glove/sleeve assembly directly undermines containment integrity, so relevance is moderate; but the error is usually caught before use, limiting severity. Capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Part-number specifications and approved supplier lists constrain selection, though mismatches still occur with substitutions or multi-model fleets. Selecting a compatible glove is a quick step once the correct part is identified. Choosing the right glove/sleeve for the isolator port and chemistry depends on the technician knowing the correct specification. An incompatible glove that fails or is discarded wastes material, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of glove and sleeve assemblies omitted from periodic integrity testing
rows.imp_rat: Omitting glove/sleeve assemblies from integrity testing can leave a breach path, so it touches containment, but as a scheduling-coverage gap its relevance and severity are held at the low ceiling for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Positioned inventories and test logs make omitting a glove uncommon, though ad hoc glove additions occasionally escape the test schedule. Testing every glove and sleeve individually takes time but is a defined periodic task. Glove integrity testers are largely automated, so completeness depends more on inventory tracking than deep skill. A skipped glove that later fails causes contamination cost, but the testing itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of integrity results assigned to the wrong glove or sleeve assembly
rows.imp_rat: Assigning integrity results to the wrong glove/sleeve could mask a real breach, giving moderate relevance and severity; traceability checks reduce the impact. Held at ceiling per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized port-numbering and structured test forms catch most mix-ups, though manual transcription still causes occasional wrong-assembly attributions. Labeling and logging each glove/sleeve result is quick when the process is followed. Correct association requires only basic diligence and position mapping, not specialized expertise. Mislabeled results occasionally trigger costly rework or unnecessary replacements but this is not the primary loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of enclosure penetrations left open or improperly sealed during the test
rows.imp_rat: Penetrations left open during the test undermines the validity of the containment test; relevance is moderate and severity somewhat higher because it can mask a real containment breach, though caught in the test phase.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-test setup checklists and the test's own pressure-hold result quickly expose an unsealed penetration, making undetected omissions uncommon. Verifying all penetrations are sealed before a pressure test adds only brief setup time. Sealing and confirming penetrations is a straightforward checklist task requiring modest skill. An open penetration invalidates the test and forces a repeat, a limited cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of component assemblies with missing, reversed, or misaligned parts
rows.imp_rat: Component assemblies with missing, reversed, or misaligned parts affects assembly quality, only moderately central to containment (low-moderate relevance); such defects cause rework or malfunction but are usually caught in inspection (moderate severity), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: In-process visual checks catch many defects, but constrained manipulation still allows misalignment errors through. Correcting misassembled components adds inspection and rework time within the run. Correct assembly through gloves in restricted space demands operator precision and attention. Reversed or missing parts can scrap the assembly, a moderate material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of product or components being dropped or spilled inside the isolator workspace
rows.imp_rat: Dropping or spilling product inside the workspace is a handling waste issue tangential to the containment barrier itself (low-moderate relevance); a spill causes loss and cleanup but is contained within the isolator (moderate severity), capped at the batch limit.
rows.sat_rat: Glove-port work is inherently clumsy and drops remain a frequent, only partly mitigated occurrence. A dropped item inside the workspace requires retrieval and possible cleanup, interrupting the run. Avoiding drops during constrained glove-based manipulation is heavily dependent on operator dexterity. Spilled product or components mean material loss and potential cleaning downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unmet enclosure prerequisites block operating-mode activation before production
rows.imp_rat: Unmet prerequisites blocking mode activation is actually a protective interlock behaving as intended, so it is low relevance and mostly a workflow delay; severity is minor because the block prevents unsafe operation.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock logic reliably enforces prerequisites, and this is largely a beneficial safeguard rather than a failure mode, so it is well handled. Prerequisite interlocks may briefly delay mode activation until conditions are met. The system enforces prerequisites automatically, so operators need little judgement to comply. Blocked activation prevents costly bad starts, so the interlock is generally protective rather than wasteful.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual pharmaceutical material left in inaccessible isolator areas before retirement
rows.imp_rat: Residual pharmaceutical material in inaccessible areas before retirement is an end-of-life decommissioning concern, largely peripheral to the operational CFJ though it carries handling/exposure risk. Low.
rows.sat_rat: Inaccessible cavities, filters and dead-legs are a chronic problem where residue routinely remains despite standard cleaning. Extra effort to reach dead-legs and hidden voids lengthens pre-retirement cleaning. Locating and clearing inaccessible areas relies heavily on expert knowledge of the specific isolator design. Partial dismantling to access hidden zones adds significant labor and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of released isolator components being mixed with components still requiring controlled handling
rows.imp_rat: Mixing released components with those still requiring controlled handling has some contamination-risk relevance, but as a handling-segregation slip within a broader process its scores stay under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling and physical segregation are ad hoc during disposal, so cleared and uncleared components getting commingled is a persistent, poorly guarded failure mode. Sorting released versus still-controlled components requires holding parts in staging areas, which slows the flow of decommissioning work. Correctly distinguishing cleared from uncleared parts depends on trained quality personnel interpreting clearance records rather than any automated gate. Mislabeling forces re-decontamination or re-disposal as hazardous waste, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator components assigned to a group inconsistent with their verified contamination status
rows.imp_rat: Assigning components to a group inconsistent with their contamination status is a classification error with contamination implications, moderately relevant; usually caught in review, so severity is bounded. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Status-to-group matching is largely manual and error-prone with no closed-loop verification, so inconsistent assignment recurs. Reconciling each component against its verified contamination status is time-consuming when status data is scattered across logs. Assigning components to the right group demands expert interpretation of decontamination certificates and material history. Wrong grouping triggers costly rework or unnecessary hazardous-waste routing.

rows.source_job: Verify containment integrity of assembled isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that penetrations or utility connections are configured differently from the specified first-use configuration during testing
rows.imp_rat: Penetrations configured differently from the first-use spec during testing is relevant to commissioning fidelity but peripheral to ongoing containment; severity is limited since it occurs in a controlled test phase before production use.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration checklists help, but test-time deviations from the specified first-use setup still slip through without rigorous change control. Verifying first-use configuration during testing is a defined checklist step with little delay. Confirming penetrations match the specified first-use configuration requires attentive comparison against design specs. Correcting configuration drift late is moderately costly but usually caught cheaply.

rows.source_job: Complete installation and operational qualification documentation
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that IQ or OQ test evidence is invalidated by unverified instrument calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Invalidated IQ/OQ evidence from unverified calibration undermines qualification credibility, but it is a documentation-integrity issue caught in review rather than a containment failure; both relevance and severity are capped low per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration-tracking and pre-test verification are mature GMP practice that make invalidated IQ/OQ evidence uncommon. Checking calibration status before IQ/OQ is a quick documentation step. Verifying calibration currency needs procedural discipline rather than scarce expertise. A calibration-status check is cheap versus repeating invalidated qualification.

rows.source_job: Integrate process equipment within isolator enclosure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of equipment interfaces being incompatible with enclosure utility and process connections
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible equipment interfaces with utility/process connections affects integration but is a design/commissioning concern peripheral to active containment; severity moderate as it is discovered before operational use.
rows.sat_rat: Interface specification and FAT reviews catch most incompatibilities, though mismatches between multi-vendor connections still recur. Checking interface compatibility during preparation is planned but can add coordination delay. Reconciling enclosure utility and process interfaces across vendors requires integration expertise that is unevenly available. Late interface adapters or rework can be moderately costly.

rows.source_job: Calibrate monitoring sensors against traceable references
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator monitoring sensors remaining outside specified tolerance after adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Sensors remaining out of tolerance after adjustment mirrors the calibration-completeness concern and touches monitoring reliability; severity moderate since out-of-tolerance readings could mislead but are typically re-verified.
rows.sat_rat: Standard adjust-and-verify loops leave few sensors out of tolerance, though some drift-prone sensors recur. Sensor adjustment is a bounded task within the calibration schedule. Bringing sensors into tolerance requires metrology skill that is generally well established. Adjustment and re-verification cost is routine and modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical deviations observed during testing that remain unresolved before shipment release
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved critical deviations before shipment release could allow a nonconforming isolator out, giving some relevance, but the deviations are already identified, keeping severity within the low batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Formal deviation-management and release gates are mature, so most critical deviations are caught and closed, though some slip toward release under pressure. Resolving critical deviations before release often becomes the schedule-critical path near shipment. Root-cause resolution of containment deviations needs experienced quality and engineering judgement. Unresolved deviations can force costly holds, rework, or expedited fixes to meet ship dates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the isolator is damaged during transport or rigging
rows.imp_rat: Damage during transport or rigging affects the delivered unit's integrity, moderately relevant; consequences are repair/replacement delays rather than in-use failure. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vibration-isolated crating and experienced rigging make transport damage uncommon, though rough handling incidents still occur. Proper crating and rigging planning add setup time but prevent slower damage-recovery cycles. Specialized riggers and packaging engineers routinely handle sensitive isolators competently. Robust packaging and qualified rigging carry real cost but are standard budgeted protections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous electrical energy while connecting the isolator
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous electrical energy is only tangentially related to the containment CFJ, but severity is elevated because electrical shock can cause serious injury; the ceiling still applies given low relevance to containment.
rows.sat_rat: Mature electrical-safety regulation and LOTO discipline make hazardous-energy exposure during connection rare. Lockout/tagout and safe-connection steps add minor time to the hookup. Electrical safety practice is well codified so competent technicians follow it with standard training. LOTO procedures cost essentially nothing beyond routine practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting the isolator to supply characteristics outside its electrical requirements
rows.imp_rat: Connecting to out-of-spec electrical supply is peripheral to the containment CFJ; severity is moderate since it can damage equipment or trip protection but rarely breaches containment directly.
rows.sat_rat: Standard nameplate-versus-supply verification makes connecting to out-of-spec supply characteristics quite rare. Verifying supply characteristics is a quick pre-energisation check. Reading nameplate ratings against supply specs needs only basic electrical competence. A verification step costs little and prevents costly equipment damage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrect conductor terminations at the isolator power interface
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect conductor terminations at the power interface is an electrical-install defect peripheral to containment function; severity is moderate as it can cause equipment faults but is caught during electrical commissioning.
rows.sat_rat: Labelled terminals, wiring diagrams, and point-to-point checks make incorrect terminations uncommon in qualified hands. Terminating conductors correctly is routine wiring work with predictable duration. Correct terminations depend on qualified electricians, and competent trade skill is generally available. Re-termination is cheap relative to downstream faults.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator pressure, flow, and particle-monitoring instruments that remain outside tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Instruments remaining out of tolerance after calibration weakens confidence in pressure/flow/particle monitoring, but the error is caught by the calibration record; relevance moderate and severity limited by detection at calibration.
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration procedures reliably bring most instruments into tolerance, with only occasional stragglers needing rework. Calibration of pressure, flow, and particle instruments is a defined scheduled activity. Instrument calibration relies on trained metrology technicians whose skill is standardised. Calibration labour and reference standards cost is routine and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using expired or untraceable references to calibrate the isolator instruments
rows.imp_rat: Using expired or untraceable references to calibrate instruments risks inaccurate isolator readings, yet this is a metrology-hygiene error; both proxies stay low under the batch importance ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: This closely overlaps needs - is well controlled by traceability programs, yet occasional lapses still produce invalid calibrations. Confirming reference validity before use is a brief step embedded in the calibration procedure and rarely a bottleneck. Recognizing an untraceable reference is basic metrology competence covered in standard training. Re-doing calibration performed against a bad reference wastes labor and instrument time, a real but infrequent expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering facility space, utility, or structural constraints after candidate selection
rows.imp_rat: Discovering facility constraints after candidate selection causes costly rework, but it is a project-planning error rather than a containment failure; both proxies held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Site walkdowns catch most constraints, but late discovery of hidden utility or structural limits still happens with some regularity. Facility surveys and constraint checks add lead time but are a normal pre-selection activity. Identifying space, utility, and structural constraints requires facilities and site-engineering knowledge that is usually available in-house. Early surveys are cheap compared to the rework of discovering constraints after selection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the formal order and the authorized vacuum-oven configuration
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between the formal order and authorized configuration are an administrative/procurement error with limited centrality to the oven actually processing samples (relevance low); consequences are usually caught and correctable rather than catastrophic (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature PO templates, spec attachments and quality sign-offs make order-to-configuration discrepancies uncommon, though transcription and revision-control errors still occasionally occur. Cross-checking the purchase order against the authorized configuration is a quick clerical step within standard procurement workflows. Reconciling order line items to an approved spec is low-skill administrative work most buyers handle competently. Order-versus-spec mismatches are usually caught before or at receipt, limiting the cost of wrong-configuration deliveries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of designated operators completing training with uncorrected cycle-setup or monitoring errors
rows.imp_rat: Operators certified with uncorrected setup or monitoring errors is a training-quality gap that can propagate mistakes, giving modest relevance; consequences are individual errors that supervision usually catches (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal SOPs and sign-off checklists catch many errors, but training verification is often informal so operators still complete qualification with uncorrected habits. Training programs take real time to deliver and to re-run when errors surface during qualification. Reducing uncorrected setup and monitoring errors depends on the quality of trainers and hands-on practice, which varies widely between sites. Operator training is a modest but recurring labor cost, especially with turnover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of obstruction to uniform heat exposure caused by sample spacing or placement
rows.imp_rat: Obstruction to uniform heat exposure from sample spacing touches process outcome quality, but it is operator technique that is easily adjusted (low relevance); poor spacing yields uneven results rather than catastrophic failure (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is little standardized guidance for load placement, so uneven heating from crowding or poor positioning remains a common, largely operator-dependent problem. Arranging samples with adequate spacing adds little time once a loading pattern is known. Achieving uniform heat exposure depends heavily on operator know-how about spacing and tray placement rather than a codified method. Racks and spacers are cheap, so cost is not the constraining factor here.

rows.source_job: Train operators in containment operating procedures
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained operators who cannot correctly demonstrate critical containment and aseptic procedures
rows.imp_rat: Trained operators unable to demonstrate critical containment/aseptic procedures does bear on containment competence (moderate relevance) and could risk contamination (elevated severity), but the batch ranking caps its combined importance at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured qualification programs improve pass rates, but a meaningful share of operators still cannot reliably demonstrate critical procedures. Requalifying operators who fail demonstration extends the training timeline before go-live. Correct aseptic and containment technique is a genuinely hard skill to instill, and competency gaps remain the central challenge. Re-training and additional media/gowning consumables add cost when operators fail assessment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of door seal, gasket, and transfer port seal surfaces omitted from each periodic inspection
rows.imp_rat: Omitting seal surfaces from periodic inspection is relevant to sustaining containment integrity but is a maintenance-completeness issue; severity moderate since a missed seal could later leak but overlaps with routine checks.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists structure the inspection but manual visual coverage means some seal surfaces are still occasionally skipped. Inspecting every seal and port surface is time-consuming but part of the scheduled PM. Complete coverage of every gasket and transfer-port seal relies on inspector diligence and familiarity with all seal locations. A missed seal defect can cause a containment breach requiring expensive intervention, though the inspection itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of vacuum-oven chamber surfaces retaining sample residue after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Sample residue retained on chamber surfaces after cleaning creates cross-contamination potential, giving modest relevance, but it is a housekeeping error typically detected on inspection and correctable, keeping severity moderate under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Residue removal is verified visually at best, so a fraction of sample residue commonly remains especially in corners and ports. Wiping down chamber surfaces is a routine, bounded cleaning step. Surface cleaning requires only basic technique and product knowledge. Cleaning agents and wipes are inexpensive consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of twisting or uneven seating of the replacement gasket in the door groove
rows.imp_rat: A twisted or unevenly seated gasket can degrade vacuum seal integrity, which is somewhat relevant to the job, but this is a maintenance-time error caught during pump-down (moderate severity, low relevance under the batch ceiling).
rows.sat_rat: Correct seating is confirmed only by feel and a subsequent leak check, so uneven or twisted installs still occur and are caught inconsistently. Seating a door gasket correctly is quick once done right but redoing a twisted one adds time. Achieving even, untwisted gasket seating in the groove is a hands-on knack that varies strongly with technician experience. Gaskets are cheap, so a redo mainly costs labor rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of condensate or contaminants remaining in the vacuum trap after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Condensate or contaminants left in the vacuum trap after cleaning can carry over into subsequent cycles, giving it some process relevance, but it is a maintenance-hygiene error normally caught (low relevance, moderate severity capped by the batch ceiling).
rows.sat_rat: Manual cleaning leaves variable residual condensate and contaminants since completeness is judged by eye rather than a defined endpoint. Cleaning the vacuum trap is a modest routine task with predictable duration. Trap cleaning is straightforward and needs little specialized skill. Solvents and disposal are low cost for this task.

rows.source_job: Inspect heating shelves and shelf supports
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of damaged heating-shelf or support areas remaining undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected damaged heating-shelf or support areas is a maintenance-inspection gap of modest relevance to the core job, with moderate severity since damage tends to be gradual and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual inspection of heating shelves and supports leaves a notable share of subtle damage undetected until it worsens. Inspecting all shelf and support areas thoroughly takes time that is often compressed in practice. Spotting early shelf or support damage relies on the inspector's experience and diligence. Inspection is inexpensive, but an undetected damaged support that fails later is costly.

rows.source_job: Verify temperature uniformity across the chamber
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified chamber positions lacking reference-sensor coverage
rows.imp_rat: Chamber positions lacking reference-sensor coverage is a monitoring-completeness gap with limited direct impact on the core job and modest consequences, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor coverage of all specified positions is frequently incomplete, leaving unmonitored chamber zones and uncertain uniformity. Positioning reference sensors at every specified chamber location is a lengthy setup step often trimmed short. Knowing which positions require coverage and placing sensors correctly demands mapping expertise not all techs have. Additional sensors and channels add equipment cost that limits how many positions get covered.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual contamination omitted from the vacuum-oven final condition record
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination omitted from the final condition record affects traceability but is peripheral to executing the core drying/curing job (low relevance); an omitted record can mislead later handling, giving moderate but contained severity.
rows.sat_rat: Final-condition records are often ad hoc without standardized checklists, so contamination details are inconsistently and incompletely captured. Documenting final contamination condition adds recordkeeping time to the decommissioning process. Complete condition records depend on the assessor knowing what must be captured, which is expertise- and diligence-dependent. Omitted records are cheap to skip short-term but create costly liability and rework later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous materials recovered from the vacuum oven receive an incorrect waste classification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect hazardous-waste classification is peripheral to the oven's core processing job (low relevance) but carries elevated severity due to compliance and safety consequences when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Established EHS classification procedures and manifests exist, but ambiguous mixed residues still produce recurring misclassification. Waste classification requires paperwork and sometimes lab analysis that can delay the disposal timeline. Correct classification hinges on regulatory expertise in hazardous-waste codes, a scarce and error-prone judgement. Misclassification risks fines and expensive incorrect disposal routing, making errors costly.

rows.stk: Purchaser, procurement specialist, or laboratory manager evaluating total cost of ownership
rows.source_job: Evaluate supplier support and service capabilities
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier service response exceeds the sterile fill-finish facility outage window
rows.imp_rat: Supplier service response exceeding a fill-finish outage window is a service-logistics timing error peripheral to the oven's function itself (low relevance); when it occurs it prolongs downtime, a real but bounded consequence within this low-ranked batch (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Even where SLAs exist, actual response consistently varies with parts, travel and staffing, so the risk of exceeding the outage window is poorly controlled. Supplier response times are governed by SLAs and technician availability that often exceed a sterile facility's tight outage window, and expediting is unreliable. Negotiating and enforcing a response-time SLA that fits sterile operations requires procurement leverage and vendor-management skill that many buyers lack. Guaranteed rapid-response contracts and spares holding are expensive, and unplanned overruns cause costly lost sterile production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required replacement barrier component is unavailable after a breach
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability of a replacement barrier component after a breach extends downtime (low relevance to prevention); severity moderate as it prolongs exposure risk. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Spare-parts inventories usually ensure replacement availability, though the right component is occasionally out of stock after a breach. Having spares on hand avoids long delays after a breach. Stocking correct replacements requires modest inventory planning skill. Maintaining spare barrier components ties up inventory cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing the contamination excursion to an incorrect source
rows.imp_rat: Attributing an excursion to the wrong source misdirects corrective action (low relevance to prevention); moderate severity if recurrence follows. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause attribution of contamination is inherently uncertain and frequently wrong, a chronically poorly-served need. Source misattribution prolongs investigations for weeks. Correctly attributing an excursion source demands rare investigative and microbiological expertise. Wrong-source conclusions cause ineffective, costly corrective actions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that physical evidence from the contamination excursion is altered before collection
rows.imp_rat: Physical evidence altered before collection impairs root-cause (low relevance to prevention); moderate severity if it misleads corrective action. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Investigation procedures aim to preserve evidence but response actions can alter conditions before capture. Securing excursion evidence quickly competes with response urgency. Preserving physical evidence needs investigative discipline under pressure. Lost evidence prolongs costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a collected sample is altered or accessed without attribution before evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Sample altered or accessed without attribution is a data-integrity error (low relevance); severity moderate under audit. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Chain-of-custody and access controls make unattributed sample alteration uncommon in regulated environments. Custody controls add negligible time. Maintaining attributable custody requires procedural discipline. Custody systems carry modest overhead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that contamination-control evidence remains defensible across inspected batches
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that evidence stays defensible across inspected batches is an audit-assurance need (low relevance); moderate severity under inspection. Below ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations or corrective actions that undermine the control-strategy approval basis
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations undermining the approval basis is a closeout-integrity gap (low relevance); moderate severity if approval is unsound. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Approval checklists flag open deviations reasonably well, though linkage to approval validity is inconsistently assessed. Tracking open deviations against approval is a review step, not primarily timing. Judging which open items undermine the approval basis needs experienced QA judgement. Approving on a weak basis risks costly downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between a relevant isolator change or deviation and its incorporation into the risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: Delay incorporating a change or deviation into the risk assessment leaves the assessment stale (low relevance to live containment); moderate severity if risk shifts. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control workflows push updates but backlogs and manual handoffs cause recurring lag. This need is explicitly about shortening the lag between a change/deviation and its incorporation into risk assessment. Timely incorporation depends on coordination more than deep skill, though awareness of relevance matters. Delay ties up product and staff attention, a modest resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination-control excursions lacking documented investigation, impact assessment, and disposition
rows.imp_rat: Excursions lacking documented investigation/disposition is a compliance-completeness error (low relevance); severity moderate under GMP scrutiny. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-management systems capture most excursions, though completeness of documented investigation and disposition still lapses. Documenting investigation and disposition for every excursion is time-consuming but the failure is missing records. Thorough investigation and disposition need trained investigators, a moderate skill demand. Undocumented excursions cause regulatory penalties and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification records containing unresolved data-integrity concerns
rows.imp_rat: Qualification records with unresolved data-integrity concerns is a records-defensibility error (low relevance); moderate severity under audit. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity controls and reviews have matured substantially, giving fairly reliable but not complete coverage. Resolving data-integrity concerns extends record review but the error is unresolved concerns. Detecting and resolving data-integrity issues needs specialized QA and audit skill. Data-integrity findings are extremely costly in regulatory terms.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved qualification deviations undermining the validity of isolation-function evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved qualification deviations undermining evidence validity is an integrity gap (low relevance); moderate severity if evidence is unsound. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification deviation management is standard, but validity impact is sometimes under-assessed. Closing qualification deviations extends timelines but the failure is unresolved deviations. Assessing whether deviations undermine evidence validity needs validation expertise. Unresolved deviations force requalification and product holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory or GMP requirements omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitted regulatory/GMP requirements from criteria is a completeness error (low relevance); severity moderate under compliance exposure. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory checklists and QA review capture most requirements, but updates and site-specific rules are sometimes omitted. Building complete criteria takes review time but the error is omission, not delay. Ensuring all applicable regulatory/GMP requirements are captured needs regulatory expertise and current knowledge. Missing requirements cause audit findings and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed qualification runs excluded without documented justification
rows.imp_rat: Qualification runs excluded without justification is a data-integrity/documentation error (low relevance); severity moderate if it masks a failure. Held below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation and exclusion-justification procedures are standard GMP practice and broadly enforced, so this is fairly well served. Documenting justification for excluding runs is a quick step relative to the run itself. Judging whether an exclusion is defensible requires quality oversight and regulatory awareness of limited availability. Undocumented exclusions risk audit findings and re-runs with meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed that an unidentified ingress failure mode remains at qualification sign-off
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed about an unidentified failure mode at sign-off is an emotional/assurance need (low relevance); severity moderate if a real gap underlies it. Ceiling-bound.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of isolator compliance claims unsupported by available objective evidence
rows.imp_rat: Compliance claims unsupported by available evidence is a defensibility gap (low relevance); severity moderate under audit scrutiny. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence-linking practice exists but a fraction of claims routinely remain unsupported. Substantiating compliance claims with evidence is documentation effort within schedule. Judging whether evidence supports each claim requires quality/regulatory expertise. Unsupported claims cause costly audit findings and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that line-layout changes invalidate compliance assumptions embedded in isolator requirements
rows.imp_rat: Line-layout changes invalidating compliance assumptions is a rework/planning error peripheral to live isolation (low relevance); consequences are re-verification effort (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Embedded compliance assumptions are frequently undocumented, so change impact on them is unreliably tracked. Reassessing compliance assumptions after changes is scheduled change-control work. Recognizing invalidated compliance assumptions needs experienced regulatory judgement. Missed invalidations cause costly compliance failures late.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that integrated isolator loads exceed floor or structural support limits
rows.imp_rat: Integrated loads exceeding structural limits is a physical-integration defect (low relevance to ingress); moderate severity for facility safety. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Structural load calculation is a mature, highly reliable engineering discipline. Structural load checks are standard engineering done within schedule. Load assessment uses routine structural-engineering competence that is readily available. Exceeding structural limits causes very costly reinforcement rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of egress, emergency-access, or fire-protection clearances violated by the integrated layout
rows.imp_rat: Violated egress/fire clearances is a safety-code layout defect (low relevance to ingress); severity moderate for personnel safety. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Building-code review and inspection make life-safety clearance compliance highly reliable. Checking egress and fire clearances is a defined code-compliance task on schedule. Life-safety clearance checks rely on established code knowledge widely available. Violations force costly rework and jeopardize occupancy approval.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of barrier-verification anomalies unresolved before isolator specification approval
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved barrier-verification anomalies at spec approval is a closeout-integrity gap (low relevance) with moderate severity if a real anomaly persists. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Formal deviation/anomaly closure before approval is standard practice and generally enforced. Resolving verification anomalies before approval can delay sign-off but is expected. Diagnosing barrier-verification anomalies requires specialized troubleshooting expertise. Unresolved anomalies risk expensive post-approval failures.

rows.stk: Industrial Microbiologist / Contamination Control Subject-Matter Expert
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator compliance claims unsupported by traceable qualification evidence
rows.imp_rat: Compliance claims unsupported by traceable evidence is a documentation/defensibility error (low relevance); severity moderate under audit. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance claims are frequently unsupported by fully traceable evidence, a recurring gap in vendor documentation. Linking claims to qualification evidence adds documentation review time. Verifying claim support requires QA and validation expertise. Traceability review is labor cost rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the source of a blower, valve, feed-through, or antechamber fault
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying a blower/valve/feed-through fault source complicates repair efficiency but is a troubleshooting/rework error; the isolation job can often continue while diagnosis is refined, so scores stay bounded.
rows.sat_rat: Onboard alarms and pressure/oxygen sensors flag that something is wrong but rarely localize the root cause, so misidentification remains common. Diagnosing which subsystem is faulting can drag out because symptoms overlap and technicians often swap parts iteratively to isolate the culprit. Correctly pinpointing a blower, valve, feed-through, or antechamber fault leans heavily on an experienced glove box technician's judgement, and such expertise is scarce and unevenly distributed across sites. Misdiagnosis wastes money on unnecessary replacement parts and repeat service visits, though individual components are not catastrophically expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated control parameters altered or erased by the firmware update
rows.imp_rat: Firmware updates erasing validated control parameters could disturb operation, but this is a maintenance-event error usually caught by revalidation; its centrality and consequences stay bounded under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Backup-and-restore practice around updates protects most settings, but silent parameter changes still occasionally slip through post-update checks. Verifying parameters after a firmware update adds a review step that modestly extends the update process. Confirming validated parameters survived an update requires knowledgeable review of controller settings. Lost parameters force reconfiguration and re-validation, an avoidable cost that pre-update backups mitigate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required control parameters, setpoints, alarm thresholds, and system settings omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting control parameters from a backup risks slower recovery but does not affect live isolation; the error is a data-hygiene concern with contained consequences under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checklist-driven backups capture most settings, but undocumented or newly added parameters are periodically omitted. Capturing a full configuration backup is a brief step once procedures are in place. Knowing every required parameter and setting to capture depends on documented checklists and staff familiarity. An incomplete backup risks costly reconstruction later, while the backup effort itself is negligible.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that viewing-panel crazing or mechanical damage remains undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected viewing-panel crazing could eventually compromise visibility or integrity, but this inspection-detection error is a downstream monitoring concern; its consequences are typically caught before failure, keeping relevance and severity modest within the 2.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on subjective visual checks means subtle crazing frequently goes unnoticed until it becomes obvious. Visual inspection of viewing panels is quick but easily deprioritized when it competes with other tasks. Detecting early crazing or fine mechanical damage depends heavily on inspector attention and experience. A missed panel defect that later fails is costly, while the inspection itself costs almost nothing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the provider rejects the returned glove box because contamination clearance is inadequate
rows.imp_rat: Provider rejection over inadequate contamination clearance affects the return/decommissioning workflow, peripheral to the box's core isolation job; consequences are rework delays rather than containment failure, so both proxies stay low under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where clearance criteria are shared up front acceptance is usually smooth, but mismatched interpretations of adequacy still cause recurring rejections at the provider gate. A provider rejection sends the unit back through re-decontamination and re-survey, causing significant schedule loss. Meeting the receiving provider's clearance expectations requires understanding their acceptance criteria and documentation, a skill many disposers underestimate. Rejected returns incur repeat transport, storage, and rework costs that are entirely avoidable when clearance is done right the first time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate judged suitable does not fit the allocated space or access path
rows.imp_rat: A candidate not fitting the allocated space or access path is a pre-purchase sizing error peripheral to run-time containment (low relevance); it forces reselection or rework before use, a moderate-low consequence, kept under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional and access-path checks are mature and usually reliable, though rigging and door-clearance surprises occasionally occur. Verifying fit and access path against dimensions is a quick, well-defined check. Comparing candidate dimensions to space and access routes needs basic drafting and logistics skill that is commonly available. The check is very low-cost versus the expense of a unit that cannot be installed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reference claims about containment performance cannot be authenticated
rows.imp_rat: Inability to authenticate reference containment-performance claims is largely an administrative/verification concern, peripheral to the day-to-day containment job; severity is modest as it affects trust in documentation rather than immediate containment.
rows.sat_rat: Reference claims are hard to authenticate independently and often taken at face value, so unverifiable claims regularly persist. Contacting references and authenticating claims takes time and is often abbreviated. Authenticating containment-performance references requires knowing what to ask and how to interpret answers, a specialized skill. Reference verification is low-cost but frequently skipped due to effort rather than money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the quotation is based on incomplete or inconsistent glove box configuration requirements
rows.imp_rat: A quotation based on incomplete configuration requirements is a pre-sale administrative rework item, largely peripheral to the operating isolation job (relevance) with commercial rather than safety consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: URS templates help, but requirements frequently remain ambiguous or inconsistent at quotation, so this is a recurring pain only partially mitigated. Requirement gathering iterations extend the quotation cycle when configuration inputs arrive incomplete. Translating process needs into a complete, consistent configuration specification requires experienced users and vendor application engineers who are not always engaged early. Quotes based on incomplete requirements cause scope changes and price escalation later in the acquisition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the critical manipulation area below the required illumination level
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient illumination of the manipulation area affects task quality and ergonomics but not containment integrity; consequences are workability issues, keeping relevance and severity modest.
rows.sat_rat: Built-in lighting reliably meets illumination levels in most modern glove boxes, with shadowing at extremes as the minor residual issue. Lighting is always-on and adds no operational time. Adequate lighting removes reliance on operator visual strain or skill. LED task lighting is cheap and standard on modern enclosures.

rows.source_job: Inspect enclosed work through viewing window
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the critical work area obscured by condensation, residue, glare, contents, or structural features
rows.imp_rat: Obscured critical work area (condensation, glare) hampers visibility during use but does not directly breach isolation; consequences are usability annoyances more than containment loss, so scores stay under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Condensation, glare, and clutter recurrently obscure the work area despite standard mitigations, degrading visual control. Wiping condensation or repositioning to see adds minor time interruptions. Working around obscured views relies on operator adaptation and careful technique. Anti-fog coatings and heated panels add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required calibration records or approved protocol prerequisites are unavailable when requalification testing begins
rows.imp_rat: Missing calibration records or protocol prerequisites at requalification start delays work; relevance is indirect to the containment job and severity is low as it is a reschedulable prerequisite gap.
rows.sat_rat: Document management systems help, but fragmented records still frequently leave prerequisites missing at the moment testing should begin. Chasing missing calibration records or protocol approvals at test start causes significant delay. Assembling the correct prerequisite documentation depends on QA coordination rather than technical skill. A halted requalification for want of paperwork wastes scheduled resources, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of requalification records invalidated by protocol deviations or incomplete evidence
rows.imp_rat: Requalification records invalidated by protocol deviations is an administrative rework issue only loosely connected to the containment CFJ (low relevance); consequences are repeated paperwork and delay rather than contamination (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Protocol deviations and incomplete evidence remain a chronic source of invalidated records despite review steps. Records invalidated by deviations force re-testing that consumes substantial extra time. Producing deviation-free, complete evidence demands experienced execution and thorough documentation discipline. Invalidated requalification records mean repeated test campaigns, a significant recurring cost.

rows.source_job: Adapt work technique to isolator geometry
rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of required manipulations that cannot be completed within operator reach limits
rows.imp_rat: Manipulations beyond operator reach hurts usability/ergonomics (moderate relevance) but rarely causes containment failure and is a design-fit annoyance (low severity). Bounded below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed gloveport placement leaves a persistent fraction of manipulations at or beyond reach limits with only crude workarounds today. Reach limitations force awkward repositioning that slows work, but the core problem is tasks that simply cannot be reached. Working around fixed gloveport geometry demands practiced ergonomic technique and improvisation. Ergonomic redesign, half-suits or additional ports are costly and rarely retrofitted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of pressure-sensor readings remaining outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Pressure sensors still out of tolerance after calibration is a metrology quality issue (some relevance to monitoring) but readily re-calibrated with minor consequence (low severity). Well within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable calibration to documented tolerances is a mature practice that keeps most sensors in spec, with only occasional drift or hysteresis leaving readings marginally out. Pressure-sensor calibration against a reference is a quick, routine bench or in-situ task. Calibration follows a defined procedure, requiring a competent metrology technician but little rare expertise. Reference standards and calibration service are standard budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of residual items left inside the isolator chamber before decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Residual items left before decontamination can shadow surfaces from the cycle (some relevance) but is a checklist oversight usually caught and re-run (low severity). Within the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-decon inspection checklists exist and catch most items, but overlooked objects in obscured areas still happen occasionally. A physical walk-down to remove leftover items is quick when a checklist is used. Catching every residual item inside the chamber depends on operator attention and thorough inspection rather than any tool. Removal of leftover items costs little beyond disposal of those items.

rows.stk: Receiving/logistics staff and procurement or quality personnel
rows.source_job: Unpack, inspect, and verify delivered isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a mismatch between the delivered and ordered isolator configurations remains unidentified before installation
rows.imp_rat: An unidentified mismatch between delivered and ordered configuration (relevance) is a one-time procurement/installation verification issue, peripheral to routine containment operation; severity is contained because it is caught during commissioning validation. Ceiling-limited.
rows.sat_rat: FAT and receiving checks catch most mismatches, but subtle configuration deviations from the order still occasionally go unidentified. Verifying delivered configuration against the purchase order is a bounded receiving activity. Detecting configuration mismatches requires someone who understands the ordered spec well enough to spot subtle differences. An unnoticed configuration mismatch drives expensive late rework or rejection, so verification pays off.

rows.stk: Receiving/logistics staff and procurement or quality personnel
rows.source_job: Unpack, inspect, and verify delivered isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that missing required isolator components or documents remain unidentified before installation
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified missing components or documents before installation is mainly a readiness/logistics error, moderately relevant to commissioning; consequences are schedule slips, not containment breach. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list and document-checklist practices catch most shortfalls, though incomplete manifests still let some omissions slip through. Checking deliverables against packing lists is a routine receiving task that adds little time. Reconciling components and documents against a manifest requires attention but not scarce skill. Finding missing parts late causes costly delays, so the inexpensive check has high value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of withdrawn samples assigned to the wrong batch or sampling point
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning withdrawn samples to the wrong batch or sampling point is a sampling-traceability error tangential to the isolator's containment CFJ; severity is moderate given potential mix-ups but not catastrophic to containment.
rows.sat_rat: Manual labeling and sample-log practices leave a persistent mixup rate that barcode/LIMS integration only partially closes. Labeling and logging sample-to-batch assignment adds minor time at each withdrawal point. Correct assignment depends on operator care in labeling and tracking multiple sampling points, an attention-heavy task. A misassigned sample can invalidate test results and force resampling or batch reassessment, incurring notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of withdrawn samples that are not representative of the process material at the designated sampling point
rows.imp_rat: Non-representative samples affect quality-testing accuracy rather than the isolator's containment job; severity is moderate as bad data could pass defective batches but is tangential to containment.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling representativeness inside constrained isolator workspaces is hard to guarantee and remains a recurring source of questionable data. Obtaining a representative sample through glove ports can require careful timing and technique that slows sampling. Representative withdrawal at the designated point demands considerable operator technique and process understanding, a real skill dependency. Non-representative samples yield misleading data and can trigger unnecessary or missed rejections, an expensive quality risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of product or component transfer blockages during sustained high-volume operation
rows.imp_rat: Transfer blockages during high-volume operation reduce productivity but do not compromise containment; relevance is modest and severity is low as blockages are recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer systems are not fully optimized for sustained high-volume flow, so blockage frequency stays a chronic operational gap. Repeated blockages during high-volume runs create cumulative stoppages that erode throughput. Sustained smooth transfer relies on operator technique and load management more than on the equipment. Frequent blockages under high volume drive lost output and labor for clearing, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of blockage at the contained discharge interface during process waste removal
rows.imp_rat: Blockage at the contained discharge interface during waste removal slows throughput but is an operational delay; its relevance to the containment job is secondary and severity low as it is clearable.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge interfaces such as continuous liners clog unpredictably, and current designs offer limited built-in anti-blockage mitigation. Blockages at the contained discharge interface directly stall waste removal and can idle the whole operation until cleared. Clearing a contained discharge blockage through glove ports without breaching containment demands significant manual finesse. Downtime and potential batch impact from a stuck discharge carry real productivity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of workspace detail obscured by condensation or residue on viewing panels
rows.imp_rat: Condensation obscuring viewing panels reduces visibility and precision (moderate relevance to usability) but rarely causes containment loss and is a transient nuisance (low severity). Bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Anti-condensation measures work reasonably well but residue and fogging still periodically obscure the workspace, a recurring but manageable issue. Waiting for panels to clear or stopping to clean them interrupts the workflow. Managing condensation via technique and cleaning schedules is straightforward and low-skill. Anti-fog coatings, heated panels and cleaning consumables add modest ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of collisions between operators' hands or tools inside the isolator
rows.imp_rat: Hand or tool collisions inside the isolator hamper task efficiency (moderate relevance to usability) but seldom breach containment and are absorbed operationally (low severity). Under the 3.5 limit.
rows.sat_rat: Confined interiors make hand and tool collisions a frequent recurring nuisance with little engineered mitigation. Collisions are momentary but the cramped shared workspace slows coordinated multi-operator tasks. Avoiding hand and tool collisions depends on operator coordination and spatial awareness in a tight enclosure with limited controls to help. Larger isolators reduce collisions but at significant footprint and capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reference standards have expired status or incomplete traceability records
rows.imp_rat: Expired status or incomplete traceability records for reference standards is a records-integrity error; relevance to the containment job is peripheral and severity capped at the low batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Metrology management systems flag expiring standards and maintain traceability, so expired or untraceable standards seldom reach the test. Checking reference standard expiry and traceability is a quick pre-test administrative step. Verifying standards status is a routine metrology task requiring little specialized skill. Using an expired standard invalidates results and wastes the whole test, but calibration lab controls make this rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required validated settings, recipes, user configurations, and audit records omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omission of settings, recipes, user configs, or audit records from backup is a backup-completeness defect peripheral to containment (low relevance); missing records complicate recovery and compliance (moderate-low severity), under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backup scope is often incomplete, so required settings or audit records are frequently found missing on restore. Reconstructing omitted settings and audit records after restore adds considerable time. Knowing which recipes, user configs, and audit records must be included requires system-specific expertise. Omitted settings mean rework and possible revalidation, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that backed-up isolator configuration data is corrupted when restoration is required
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted configuration backup on restoration is a data-recovery risk peripheral to physical containment (low relevance); it delays recovery and could force manual reconfiguration (moderate-low severity), capped at the batch limit.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are commonly made but restore-testing is infrequent, so corruption often goes undiscovered until it matters. Discovering a corrupt backup only at restoration time causes major delay when the system is already down. Verifying backup integrity requires IT and controls knowledge that is inconsistently applied. A corrupt backup can force full reconfiguration and revalidation, an expensive rare event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the isolator configuration backup is inaccessible when restoration is required
rows.imp_rat: An inaccessible configuration backup when restoration is required is a recoverability concern tangential to the containment CFJ (low relevance); it can extend downtime but does not cause immediate containment failure (moderate-low severity), capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Backups exist but access location, media, and permissions are not always verified, so accessibility occasionally fails when needed. An inaccessible backup at restoration time causes severe delay while alternatives are sought. Locating and accessing the right backup relies on IT process knowledge that varies by site. Inability to restore extends downtime and may force costly rebuild, an infrequent but heavy loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of measurement error caused by enclosure temperature drift during the test
rows.imp_rat: Measurement error from enclosure temperature drift affects test accuracy, moderately relevant to qualification; consequences are re-testing rather than product harm, so severity is low. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental stabilization periods and temperature-compensated instruments keep drift error small in most controlled test conditions. Allowing enclosure temperature to stabilize before testing adds waiting time but is standard practice. Recognizing and controlling for temperature drift requires metrology awareness during the test. Drift-induced measurement error may force a retest, a limited occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of enclosure tasks being started before prerequisite operator tasks are complete
rows.imp_rat: Starting enclosure tasks before prerequisite operator tasks complete (relevance) is a workflow-sequencing lapse that interlocks usually prevent; severity is low-to-moderate since such errors are typically recoverable procedural slips. Below the batch importance threshold.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and batch-record steps enforce some sequencing, but many prerequisite dependencies still rely on procedural compliance and are occasionally violated. Starting enclosure tasks prematurely triggers rework or aborts that cost time to unwind. Sequencing correctly depends on operator training and attention to procedure order rather than enforced logic. Out-of-sequence starts can spoil material or force re-cleaning, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of encountering conflicting setpoint requirements for the active operation
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting setpoint requirements is a specification-ambiguity issue tangential to containment execution; severity moderate as unresolved conflicts could misconfigure conditions but are typically caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and parameter interlocks in modern control systems catch many conflicts, though hybrid and manual setups still surface them. Resolving conflicting setpoints mid-operation halts work until parameters are reconciled. Detecting and reconciling contradictory recipe/setpoint requirements demands experienced process knowledge. Setpoint conflicts rarely destroy material outright but can cause scrapped sub-runs when unnoticed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory quality, containment, acceptance, or liability obligations omitted from the executed contract
rows.imp_rat: Omitted contractual obligations are administrative/liability errors peripheral to the physical containment job, so relevance is low; severity can be meaningful in disputes but does not affect operation. Ceiling-limited.
rows.sat_rat: Mature URS-to-contract mapping and QA checklists catch most mandatory obligations, but consistency gaps mean some still slip through. Legal and quality contract review adds time but is a routine part of the acquisition cycle for GMP equipment. Capturing all containment, acceptance, and liability obligations depends on experienced quality and contracts staff who are unevenly available across buyers. Omitted obligations translate into uncovered liability and expensive later disputes, though standard templates limit gross omissions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that factory acceptance test results are invalid because measurement instruments lack current calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Invalid FAT results due to uncalibrated instruments are a pre-delivery quality concern; relevance is modest since containment job itself is unaffected, and severity is low because errors are typically re-tested before acceptance.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration-certificate checks before FAT are routine practice, so invalid FAT results from out-of-calibration instruments are infrequent. Confirming instrument calibration before FAT is a brief pre-test verification. Checking measurement-instrument calibration status is a standard procedural task requiring modest skill. Verification is inexpensive against the cost of repeating a FAT.

rows.source_job: Connect utilities and ducting to isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between facility ducting or utility lines and corresponding isolator ports
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between facility ducting and isolator ports triggers rework and fit-up effort but does not directly defeat containment; relevance is moderate and severity low under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Facility-to-equipment interface misalignment is a persistent commissioning problem because facility and vendor drawings often diverge, so it recurs often. Reconciling facility ducting and utility lines to isolator ports frequently causes field delays while mismatches are reworked. Coordinating multi-trade interfaces requires experienced field engineers reading combined MEP and equipment drawings. Field modification of ducting or ports to close alignment gaps is expensive rework.

rows.source_job: Connect utilities and ducting to isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required utility feed is unavailable at the isolator connection point during commissioning
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable utility feed at commissioning is a scheduling/readiness delay peripheral to the containment CFJ; severity is modest since it delays startup rather than compromising containment.
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning readiness checklists help, but utility feeds unavailable at the connection point remain a common commissioning-day surprise. Confirming utility availability at connection points is a scheduled commissioning check. Verifying utility readiness requires facility coordination rather than deep specialist skill. A missing feed at commissioning causes costly standby delays but avoidance is cheap.

rows.source_job: Verify containment integrity of assembled isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required test instrumentation lacks valid calibration when containment testing is scheduled
rows.imp_rat: Lacking valid calibration when containment testing is scheduled mainly causes delay rather than containment loss; relevance to the core job is indirect and severity is low since it is a reschedulable administrative snag.
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration-management systems make a lapsed instrument at test time uncommon, though manual coordination gaps do still let it happen. Calibration due-date tracking via CMMS or calibration schedules usually flags instruments in time, though last-minute scheduling still causes occasional scrambles. Metrology and QA staff routinely manage calibration cycles, so little unusual expertise is required beyond following established SOPs. Recalibrating or renting a substitute instrument at short notice adds modest cost, but this is a well-budgeted routine expense.

rows.source_job: Complete installation and operational qualification documentation
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of IQ or OQ checks that fail predefined acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: IQ/OQ checks failing acceptance criteria are expected findings surfaced by design; relevance is moderate and severity low since failures are meant to be caught and corrected before use.
rows.sat_rat: Even with pre-qualification checks, a nontrivial fraction of IQ/OQ items fail acceptance criteria on first pass, making this a recurring pain. Failed IQ/OQ checks trigger investigation and retest cycles that extend the qualification timeline significantly. Anticipating and preventing failures requires seasoned validation engineers whose judgement is in short supply. Each failed check consumes retest labor, consumables, and sometimes vendor callbacks, adding meaningful cost.

rows.source_job: Complete installation and operational qualification documentation
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the installed isolator configuration and the drawings or specifications referenced by the qualification protocols
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between installed configuration and qualification drawings create documentation ambiguity but are usually reconciled in protocol review; relevance is moderate and severity low relative to containment integrity.
rows.sat_rat: As-built drift from design is a chronic source of qualification findings despite red-line and change-control practices, so discrepancies recur regularly. Walk-down comparisons of installed configuration against drawings are time-consuming and often compressed into tight commissioning windows. Detecting drawing-vs-as-built discrepancies demands experienced engineers who can read P&IDs and spot subtle deviations. Late-discovered discrepancies force rework and change orders that are expensive to correct on site.

rows.source_job: Integrate process equipment within isolator enclosure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process equipment colliding with enclosure features or blocking door closure during installation
rows.imp_rat: Collision of process equipment with enclosure features or blocking door closure during installation is a one-time setup defect, only peripherally tied to the CFJ of ongoing containment (low relevance); consequences are typically caught and remedied during commissioning (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Clash detection and layout review reduce collisions, but interference and blocked doors still surface where coordination was incomplete. Collisions or door-closure blockages discovered during install stall work while clearances are resolved. Anticipating clearance conflicts benefits from experienced planners doing 3D/clash review, a skill not always applied. Moving equipment or modifying enclosures to restore clearance is costly late in installation.

rows.source_job: Configure user access and role assignments
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an isolator action cannot be attributed to a specific authorized individual
rows.imp_rat: Inability to attribute an isolator action to a specific individual is a data-integrity/audit concern peripheral to physical containment (low relevance); it can create compliance exposure but not immediate product or personnel harm (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: During Preparation, attribution controls are being set up rather than proven, so gaps in role/account provisioning still leave some actions unattributable. Attributing actions via login/audit trails is instantaneous once access-control systems are configured. Operating within a badge or login attribution scheme requires little added skill from users. Access-control and audit-trail systems have upfront cost but low marginal cost per action.

rows.source_job: Calibrate monitoring sensors against traceable references
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a reference standard has expired or unverifiable traceability during isolator sensor calibration
rows.imp_rat: An expired or untraceable reference standard during sensor calibration can bias readings, but this ambiguity is peripheral to the containment job and severity is limited as it is typically caught during calibration checks.
rows.sat_rat: Formal reference-standard control programs keep most references valid and traceable, though expiry surprises still occur when logs are not tightly maintained. Verifying reference-standard validity is a quick documentary check when traceability records are organized, but chasing certificates can delay setup. Metrology technicians know how to read NIST-traceable certificates, so the judgement required is standard rather than scarce. Maintaining certified reference standards carries recurring recertification cost that is normally planned for.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operating-state checks that remain inconclusive during recovery
rows.imp_rat: Inconclusive operating-state checks during recovery (relevance) delay restart but rarely cause direct containment failure; severity is limited because a check that remains inconclusive typically triggers a conservative hold rather than a release. Below the batch importance threshold.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor coverage and self-diagnostics leave gaps, so some operating-state checks remain inconclusive and force conservative repeat actions. Inconclusive state checks stall recovery while operators re-run or manually verify conditions. Interpreting ambiguous sensor readings during recovery demands seasoned judgement not evenly distributed across shifts. Re-verification consumes labor and sometimes wasted material when a check cannot confirm a safe state.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate isolator configurations with missing sterile fill-finish suitability information
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile fill-finish suitability information on candidate configurations is a selection-phase data gap; it affects early planning more than containment performance, so both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor data on fill-finish suitability is frequently incomplete, so candidates with missing information are a recurring problem. Gathering suitability information from vendors on each configuration can be slow and iterative. Recognizing which sterile fill-finish suitability information is missing requires aseptic-process familiarity. Information gathering is largely a labor cost with little material expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material and personnel flow conflicts missed during layout evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Material and personnel flow conflicts missed during layout evaluation is a planning-stage defect peripheral to the containment barrier itself (low relevance); missed conflicts create workflow inefficiency or rework (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual layout reviews miss subtle personnel and material flow conflicts fairly often, making this a recurring gap. Flow analysis across material and personnel paths is time-consuming and often compressed. Detecting flow conflicts requires experienced process-flow and cleanroom-layout designers whose availability is limited. Layout evaluation is cheap relative to the operational cost of crossed flows discovered in operation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operator reach and maintenance-access limitations missed before purchase
rows.imp_rat: Operator reach and maintenance-access limitations missed before purchase is a procurement-stage oversight tangential to run-time containment (low relevance); consequences are ergonomic and maintenance difficulties, moderate-low, kept under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice relies on drawing reviews and vendor claims rather than mock-ups, so reach and access limitations are routinely missed until hardware arrives. Ergonomic and maintenance-access reviews are typically deferred until factory acceptance or install, so the time to catch problems balloons well past purchase. Spotting reach-envelope and maintenance-access limits pre-purchase demands scarce human-factors and containment-engineering judgement that most acquisition teams lack. Missed access limitations surface as costly rework or retrofits after commitment rather than as line-item costs during acquisition.

rows.stk: Laboratory manager, quality/compliance officer, data custodian
rows.source_job: Export data and archive lifecycle records
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required process and configuration records missing from the vacuum-oven export
rows.imp_rat: Missing process and configuration records in the export is a documentation-completeness error, largely administrative and not central to the oven doing its job (low relevance); gaps hamper audits but are recoverable (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Records are typically scattered across systems and paper files, so export packages routinely omit some required documents. Compiling process and configuration records for export is a slow, manual document-gathering effort. Knowing which records are required and where they reside demands institutional knowledge that is easily lost at end-of-life. Missing records rarely cost money directly but can force expensive reconstruction or block compliant transfer.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required vacuum-oven service and calibration activities omitted from the agreement
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required service and calibration activities from the agreement is a contracting-document error peripheral to daily oven operation (low relevance); missing coverage could eventually allow drift, giving modest severity.
