rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage by which the total installed capital requirement is underestimated
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating total installed capital is a financial-estimation error unrelated to ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is funding shortfall (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Total installed requirements are consistently underestimated because indirect costs get overlooked. Building a full installed-cost requirement takes effort early in the cycle. Comprehensive total-installed-cost estimation is a specialist competency that is scarce. Underestimation of total installed cost directly produces funding shortfalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that final installed capital cost exceeds the estimate used for funding approval
rows.imp_rat: Final installed cost exceeding the funding estimate is a cost-control error unrelated to ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is budget overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Estimates are unreliable because installation contingencies are habitually underweighted. Testing installed-cost estimates against funding approval takes time but is done during authorization. Forecasting installed capital accurately needs seasoned estimating skill often in short supply. Installed cost exceeding the funding estimate is a common and material overspend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of formal requirements lacking measurable acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Formal requirements lacking measurable acceptance criteria is a specification-quality error indirectly tied to isolation (low relevance); consequence is ambiguous acceptance (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Many requirements remain qualitative, so pass/fail is contested and failures recur. Attaching measurable criteria to every requirement lengthens specification drafting. Writing measurable acceptance criteria requires metrology and validation skill that is unevenly present. Unmeasurable requirements create disputes and rework costs at acceptance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of facility, utility, process, and material-flow interfaces omitted from the procurement specification
rows.imp_rat: Omitted interfaces in the procurement spec is a completeness/rework error (low relevance) with modest consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Interface omissions are a recurring failure because no complete interface register is consistently maintained. Mapping facility, utility, and material-flow interfaces requires lengthy coordination across engineering disciplines. Identifying every interface demands multidisciplinary design knowledge that a single buyer cannot supply. Missed interfaces generate expensive site rework and supplier claims after award.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the specified operating envelope and anticipated sterile fill-finish demand
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between operating envelope and anticipated demand is a specification/capacity error remote from ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is fit shortfall (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Demand-to-envelope mismatches recur because forecasts and specifications are loosely coupled. Matching operating envelope to demand requires forecasting work early in specification. Sizing capacity against sterile fill-finish demand needs process and market forecasting expertise. Envelope mismatch causes either wasted capacity or costly future expansion.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of additional capital required for unplanned modifications needed to achieve qualification
rows.imp_rat: Additional capital for unplanned modifications to achieve qualification is a rework/cost error indirectly tied to isolation (low relevance); consequence is budget overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: These modifications recur often enough that unplanned qualification spend is a predictable but poorly-budgeted event. Qualification-driven modifications surface late, adding both time and cost pressure. Anticipating qualification-forcing modifications demands deep aseptic and validation expertise rarely held by procurement. Unplanned modifications to achieve qualification are a classic source of extra capital demand.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital is unavailable when the isolator investment must be funded
rows.imp_rat: Capital being unavailable when the isolator investment must be funded is a financing error, not a containment mechanism (low relevance); it delays procurement rather than causing ingress (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Funds are typically earmarked once a project is approved, but liquidity shocks and competing priorities still leave a real chance capital is not free exactly when the isolator needs it. Capital release timing follows the established budget cycle, which usually aligns funds with committed projects but can stall when cash is reprioritized mid-cycle. Treasury and finance staff routinely manage capital availability, so the required expertise is generally present in the organization. The status quo does not add cost so much as risk locking up capital that competing demands may claim.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the isolator financial case fails required investment thresholds
rows.imp_rat: The financial case failing investment thresholds is a business-case error unrelated to physical containment (low relevance); consequence is project non-approval (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard NPV/IRR gating catches most weak cases, though compliance-driven isolators sometimes struggle to clear purely financial thresholds. Financial case preparation fits standard capital-appraisal timelines when templates are used. Finance partners routinely test cases against hurdle rates, so the analytical skill is generally available. A case failing thresholds means the isolator spend is deferred, but the appraisal itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the funding requirement is understated through omitted integration, validation, or lifecycle costs
rows.imp_rat: Understated funding from omitted integration/lifecycle costs is a financial-estimation error not central to ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is budget overrun (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Understatement is a common, well-known failure because upfront estimates lean on vendor quotes that exclude significant owner-side costs. Building a fully-loaded estimate takes time that pressured capital-request cycles rarely allow. Capturing integration, validation and lifecycle costs demands cross-functional engineering and quality knowledge the purchase executor typically lacks alone. Omitted integration, validation and lifecycle costs chronically understate the true funding requirement for isolators.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that funding authorization arrives after the required isolator investment start milestone
rows.imp_rat: Funding authorization arriving after the investment start milestone is a schedule/financing error, not a containment lever (low relevance); it delays the project rather than causing ingress (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approval slippage past a start milestone is a recurring, only partly mitigated problem given multi-stakeholder sign-off dependencies. Authorization commonly lags project timelines because approval gates are tied to fixed committee calendars rather than milestone urgency. Navigating the approval sequence quickly requires seasoned procurement judgement about when and how to escalate. Late authorization can force rushed premium purchasing, adding avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that the supporting evidence justifies the isolator capital commitment
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that evidence justifies the capital commitment is a business-case decision error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is investment hesitation (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviations from approved handling or transfer procedures during the demonstration
rows.imp_rat: Deviations from handling procedures during a demonstration are a rehearsal/compliance-observation error before real use; consequences are contained and correctable, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Mature written SOPs, sign-offs and supervised demonstrations make procedural conformance a well-established practice, though occasional deviations still slip through without independent verification. Following the approved procedure step-by-step during a demonstration is well-paced and rarely the bottleneck, though correcting deviations mid-run costs some time. Adhering to approved handling and transfer procedures leans on trained operators, but documented SOPs and written procedures reduce the reliance on individual expertise. Procedural deviations in a demonstration rarely waste costly material, so the cost exposure is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that preventive maintenance remains overdue before a qualified service appointment occurs
rows.imp_rat: Preventive maintenance staying overdue delays servicing and can erode reliability, yet this scheduling delay ranks below the prior batch; relevance and severity are kept at 2.0 as it is a service-timing issue rather than an immediate containment breach.
rows.sat_rat: Calendar reminders and service-contract schedules exist and work reasonably well, though enforcement gaps let some PM slip. PM overdue duration depends on scheduling and technician availability, and backlogs routinely push appointments past their due dates. Booking and performing scheduled PM is a routine task requiring little specialized judgement, so skill is not the limiting factor. Deferred PM defers immediate spend but risks costlier failures later, a tradeoff facilities often accept under budget pressure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance and service events omitted from the isolation glove box history
rows.imp_rat: Omitting maintenance events from the isolation history weakens the service record, but this recordkeeping error is only loosely tied to achieving containment (low relevance) and its consequences are documentary; capped at 2.0 by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Maintenance history is typically kept in paper logs, spreadsheets or memory, so omissions are frequent and the record is chronically incomplete. Logging an event takes little time, but events still get skipped when work is done ad hoc or under time pressure. Recording a service event needs no special skill, yet it depends on the discipline and diligence of whoever performed the work. Missing history entries cost little directly but erode warranty claims and inflate downstream troubleshooting spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required qualification, maintenance, and decontamination records missing from the transfer package
rows.imp_rat: Missing qualification/maintenance records in a transfer package is a documentation-handoff error, recoverable and peripheral to active isolation function, warranting low scores.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle records for glove boxes are often incomplete or siloed, so transfer packages routinely arrive with missing maintenance and decon documentation. Compiling the qualification, maintenance, and decon history into a transfer package is a slow, backward-looking search through fragmented files. Determining the complete required record set for transfer demands specialist knowledge of the receiving party's needs that disposal staff rarely hold. Gaps trigger delayed transfers, re-testing, or re-qualification costs to fill missing documentation.

rows.source_job: Export controller data and configuration records
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required monitoring, calibration, audit, and configuration records omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting monitoring/calibration records from an export is a documentation-transfer error, recoverable and administrative, remote from the core isolation job, warranting minimal scores.
rows.sat_rat: These records are dispersed across instruments, audit files, and configuration logs with no unified export, so omissions from the export are a persistent problem. Extracting monitoring, calibration, audit, and configuration records for export is slow when they live in disparate logs and control systems. Knowing the full export set and pulling it from multiple systems requires familiarity with each subsystem's records that few individuals possess. Incomplete exports cause rework and potential compliance penalties, adding avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required glove box decommissioning records omitted from the compliant archive
rows.imp_rat: Omitted decommissioning records are an archival/compliance error at end-of-life, peripheral to active isolation performance and generally recoverable, so relevance and severity are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Archiving practice is inconsistent and largely manual, so required decommissioning records are frequently omitted, making this among the worse-served needs. Assembling the compliant archive at end of life is a slow reconstruction of records scattered across the unit's history. Knowing which decommissioning records are mandatory and where they belong requires records-management and regulatory expertise often lacking on disposal teams. Chasing missing records and reconstructing them, or paying for non-compliance findings, is a recurring hidden cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the purchase commitment and the approved configuration and commercial terms
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between purchase commitment and approved terms are a commercial/administrative error, correctable and remote from the isolation job, so both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Mature purchasing controls and contract review practices reliably align commitments to approved terms, with rare exceptions. Purchase commitment reviews are integrated into standard approval sign-off with minimal added delay. Verifying commercial terms and configuration against approval is a defined procurement competency covered by standard roles. Commercial discrepancies caught before commitment prevent significant financial exposure, and the review itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required budgetary, technical, safety, or procurement elements missing from the approval package
rows.imp_rat: Missing budgetary, technical, safety, or procurement elements from the approval package causes approval delays, yet this documentation-completeness error is capped at 2.0 as it ranks lower than prior-batch items.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized capital approval templates make missing elements relatively uncommon, with occasional gaps in specialized safety content. Approval package assembly follows templates that streamline compiling required elements. Compiling budgetary, technical, safety and procurement content requires coordination but is a defined procurement competency. Incomplete approval packages cause approval delays and rework, but the compilation itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the ordered glove box configuration and the approved configuration
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between ordered and approved configuration are a procurement-alignment error, administrative and correctable pre-delivery, only tangential to the isolation CFJ; both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Formal order-vs-approved-configuration checks are well established in procurement, so discrepancies are usually caught, with occasional slips. Configuration review against the order adds a checkpoint but is routine and fast within procurement workflows. Comparing ordered versus approved configuration is a structured clerical task requiring moderate attention rather than deep expertise. Discrepancies caught at ordering are cheap to fix, and standard PO review avoids costly downstream errors.

rows.source_job: Verify site space and utility compatibility
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required site dimensions or utility parameters left unverified before order approval
rows.imp_rat: Unverified site dimensions or utility parameters before order approval can cause fit or hookup issues, but the ranking caps this below the prior batch; relevance and severity are held low as this is a pre-order administrative check rather than core containment function.
rows.sat_rat: Site verification checklists exist but coordination gaps leave utility or clearance parameters unverified before approval more often than acceptable. Site surveys and utility verification take time and are often compressed against order-approval deadlines. Verifying dimensions and utility parameters requires facilities engineers coordinating with process teams, a coordination that frequently lapses. Unverified site parameters cause expensive field modifications and delivery delays discovered too late.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the shipment exceeds confirmed transport-route or site-access limits
rows.imp_rat: A shipment exceeding transport-route or site-access limits can stall delivery and installation, but this logistics delay ranks below the prior batch; relevance and severity are held to 2.0 per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard transport and access surveys reliably catch most constraints, though last-mile or door-clearance surprises occasionally occur. Transport and site-access checks are routine logistics tasks completed within normal planning windows. Route surveys and rigging assessments are performed by logistics specialists using standard methods. Missed access limits cause expensive re-crating or crane rework, but assessments are relatively low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking chemical incompatibility between glove box materials and process materials or cleaning agents
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking chemical incompatibility between glove box materials and process/cleaning agents could degrade containment integrity, but per the batch ceiling relevance and severity are held to 2.0 as it ranks below prior items in the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility charts and vendor datasheets help, but the assessment relies heavily on expert judgement and is frequently incomplete for cleaning-agent interactions. Chemical compatibility assessment adds review time but is not the primary bottleneck in acquisition. Judging material compatibility with process chemicals and cleaning agents demands specialized materials and chemistry expertise that is scarce in typical procurement teams. Overlooked incompatibility leads to costly premature material degradation or contamination remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configuration discrepancies between the delivered glove box and the approved purchase order
rows.imp_rat: Configuration discrepancies between delivered box and purchase order are a receiving/verification error, correctable pre-use and peripheral to isolation performance, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Goods-receipt inspection is standard practice and catches most discrepancies, though subtle configuration deviations sometimes pass unnoticed. Incoming inspection against the PO is scheduled at delivery but can bottleneck if inspection resources are limited. Verifying delivered configuration against the PO requires trained inspectors familiar with glove box specifications. Discrepancies found at delivery are costly to remediate once equipment is on site, though inspection itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required operating and alarm-response tasks omitted from operator training
rows.imp_rat: Omitting operating and alarm-response tasks from operator training could impair safe use, but under the batch ceiling relevance and severity are held to 2.0 since it ranks below every item already scored.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor-provided training and SOPs cover core tasks, but alarm-response and edge-case tasks are inconsistently included, leaving recurring gaps. Operator training is scheduled around commissioning and rarely delays acquisition itself. Building complete operating and alarm-response training depends on experienced trainers who understand the specific glove box's failure modes. Incomplete training risks operational errors but training programs are modest in cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between the enclosure position and required site interfaces or clearances
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment with site interfaces or clearances is an installation-fit error resolved during commissioning, only indirectly related to ongoing isolation; both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional layout, survey and shop-drawing checks make interface alignment generally consistent, with only occasional clearance surprises from site variance. Positioning and alignment against site interfaces is a defined installation task with predictable duration. Achieving alignment relies on riggers and surveyors using standard measurement tools rather than scarce expertise. Alignment tooling is inexpensive, though correcting misalignment after fixing is costly.

rows.source_job: Document enclosure configuration and installation record
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the installation record and the as-installed enclosure configuration
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between installation record and as-installed configuration are a documentation-accuracy error, correctable and administrative, only loosely tied to the CFJ, so both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: As-built reconciliation practices exist but manual record updating is error-prone and frequently lags reality, so record-versus-installed discrepancies remain a recurring pain. Reconciling installation records against the as-built condition is tedious documentation work that is often deferred. Accurate red-lining requires diligent record-keeping discipline that is inconsistently applied across crews. Documentation itself is cheap, but discrepancies discovered later trigger costly investigation and rework.

rows.stk: Lab technician, facility manager, or receiving personnel
rows.source_job: Unpack and inspect enclosure on receipt
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transit damage remains undetected when receipt inspection is completed
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage at receipt is a logistics/inspection lapse only loosely tied to the operational isolation job; the error is usually reversible via claims or repair, so both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Shock indicators and checklists catch obvious damage, yet hidden internal or latent damage routinely escapes visual receipt inspection. Receipt inspection is a standard, relatively fast gate on delivery. Detecting subtle or concealed transit damage depends on the inspector's diligence and experience. Inspection cost is low, but undetected damage found later drives costly rework and delay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of handling errors remaining uncorrected during glove-box rehearsal
rows.imp_rat: Uncorrected handling errors during rehearsal are a training-phase issue that precedes real operation; their consequences are caught before deployment, keeping relevance and severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and supervised dry-runs catch many mistakes, but there is no systematic capture of every uncorrected handling error, so recurring gaps remain. Rehearsal runs can be repeated to catch handling errors, but re-running dry-runs adds time and errors caught late still force schedule slips. Detecting uncorrected handling errors during rehearsal depends heavily on an experienced observer or trainer whose vigilance and judgement are scarce, so slips routinely go unnoticed until later. Rehearsal in a mock or non-active box is relatively cheap, so undetected handling errors carry little immediate material cost at this stage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a software update causes isolator control functions to malfunction
rows.imp_rat: A software update malfunctioning control functions could disrupt containment operation (some relevance) with potentially serious consequences (some severity), but the batch ranking places it below higher items, so both proxies are held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and post-update functional testing catch many issues, but untested edge cases and vendor firmware regressions still cause occasional malfunctions. Applying updates and revalidating control functions can consume significant downtime. Assessing update impact on control logic needs specialized controls/validation expertise that is often scarce. Revalidation effort and potential vendor support add nontrivial cost per update.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preventive maintenance is scheduled after the isolator compliance deadline
rows.imp_rat: Scheduling preventive maintenance after a compliance deadline creates a compliance-timing gap (some severity) but is a scheduling error somewhat removed from real-time containment (moderate relevance); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated due-date reminders and compliance calendars make missed PM deadlines uncommon where a CMMS is in place, though resource conflicts occasionally cause slippage. Scheduling software and calibration-due tracking reliably trigger PM before deadlines in most facilities. Setting up and maintaining the schedule needs a planner but little specialized skill once configured. CMMS scheduling tools are widely owned, so ongoing cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of internal moisture causing corrosion or microbial growth during extended storage
rows.imp_rat: Internal moisture causing corrosion or microbial growth in storage can degrade a stored isolator (some relevance and severity), but it affects idle units and is ranked below the prior batch, so both proxies are held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Standard storage practices reduce moisture problems, but extended or improvised storage without monitoring still occasionally allows corrosion or microbial growth to go unnoticed. Applying desiccants or purge before storage is a quick preparatory step. Knowing correct storage prep is fairly routine but not always followed for infrequently stored units. Desiccants and dry-purge are cheap relative to corrosion damage they prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of contaminated isolator waste assigned an incorrect regulatory classification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect regulatory classification of contaminated waste carries compliance exposure (some severity) but is a downstream administrative step relative to the core containment job (moderate relevance); held at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Waste-profiling procedures and licensed haulers reduce errors, but shifting regulations and ambiguous contaminant profiles still produce misclassifications. Classifying waste per regulation adds documentation time before shipment. Regulatory classification demands specialist waste-compliance knowledge that many disposal teams lack in-house. Misclassification triggers fines and rework, and conservative over-classification wastes money on premium disposal.

rows.stk: Professional | Environmental Health and Safety Specialist | One-time
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous isolator components assigned to an incorrect disposal pathway
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning hazardous components to a disposal pathway is a downstream waste-handling error with regulatory consequences, but it sits well below active containment functions in centrality (relevance) and is capped in severity by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Waste-segregation SOPs and manifests guide sorting, but component-level judgement errors and ambiguous mixed materials cause recurring misassignments. Segregating components to correct disposal pathways adds sorting time to the process. Correct pathway assignment requires knowledge of both contamination status and material/regulatory rules, a scarce combined expertise. Misrouting to hazardous vs standard streams has large cost swings, so errors are financially painful.

rows.stk: Professional | Facilities Engineer / Administrator | As needed
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving an isolator that conflicts with required operating and service clearances
rows.imp_rat: Approving an isolator that conflicts with operating and service clearances is a planning-stage procurement error; it is only indirectly related to the core containment job (moderate relevance) and consequences are installation rework rather than containment breach (moderate severity). Capped at 2.0 per batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: 3D layout models and clearance drawings are common practice, but clearance conflicts still slip through when service and operator access needs are underspecified. Checking operating and service clearances against layouts is a manageable step within the normal design review cycle. Verifying clearances requires facility engineering judgement about maintenance access and ergonomics that is not fully standardized. Approving an isolator that violates service clearances forces expensive relocation or facility modification later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required certificates, test reports, and validation documents omitted from the supplier package
rows.imp_rat: Omitted certificates and validation documents is an administrative/paperwork gap (low relevance to physical containment) with easily remediated consequence (low severity). Clearly a minor item under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation deliverable lists in purchase specifications are standard practice, yet supplier packages still arrive incomplete often enough that follow-up chasing is routine. Chasing missing certificates and validation documents from suppliers routinely adds delay at the tail end of acquisition. Knowing exactly which certificates, test reports, and validation documents are required takes moderate documentation and QA familiarity rather than deep expertise. Missing documentation rarely costs money directly but can delay commissioning that ties up capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of trainee contact with non-aseptic surfaces during manipulation practice
rows.imp_rat: Trainee contact with non-aseptic surfaces during practice is a training-context contamination error; it relates to aseptic technique (some relevance) but occurs in practice not production (bounded severity), and is capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: With no forgiving practice environment beyond supervision, trainee contact errors remain common and poorly mitigated by current practice. Training runs take time regardless, so duration is not the central concern. Trainee aseptic manipulation practice is precisely where inexperience causes frequent non-aseptic surface contact, and mentoring only partly controls it. Dedicated training isolators or media-fill practice consume real resources and consumables.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer interruption caused by material lodged in the transfer port
rows.imp_rat: Material lodged in the transfer port interrupting transfer is a minor operational nuisance; both relevance and severity are low since the core containment function persists during a brief interruption.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer ports (RTP/alpha-beta or split-butterfly) jam intermittently and remain a common, imperfectly mitigated interruption source. Material lodged in the transfer port halts transfers and the clearing effort extends interruption directly. Dislodging jammed material through gloves without contamination requires practiced dexterity. Transfer interruptions delay throughput and can risk in-process material, a tangible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of material lost to residual hold-up when processing smaller batches
rows.imp_rat: Material lost to hold-up on small batches is chiefly a yield/economics error, only weakly central to the containment job, and its consequences are financial rather than safety-critical. Low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-up losses are fairly predictable but hard to eliminate, so yield consistency for small batches remains a recurring gap. Small-batch hold-up losses mainly cost yield rather than time, though extra recovery flushes add some duration. Minimizing hold-up depends on skilled setup of transfer geometry and operator finesse in recovering product from dead legs. For high-value APIs, residual hold-up in an isolator sized for larger volumes is a direct and painful material loss that scales badly for small batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same alarm recurring after the operator returns the isolator to operation
rows.imp_rat: A recurring alarm after return-to-operation is a rework/nuisance error, only weakly central to the containment job, with mild operational consequences. Low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Without robust root-cause fixes, the same alarm often returns after a reset, a well-known reliability frustration only partly addressed by alarm rationalization. Recurring alarms repeatedly halt the run, compounding lost time each cycle. Distinguishing a nuisance alarm from a real fault so it does not recur requires diagnostic skill many operators lack. Repeat alarms trigger repeated investigations and documentation effort, a recurring labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of withdrawing less sample material than required for scheduled testing
rows.imp_rat: Withdrawing insufficient sample volume is a minor sampling-workflow error largely peripheral to containment; consequences are usually just a repeat draw, so severity is low.
rows.sat_rat: Defined sampling SOPs and pre-set volumes reduce shortfalls, but manual withdrawal still produces occasional undersized samples. Drawing an insufficient volume means a return trip to resample, adding cycle time. Judging adequate sample quantity through gloves relies on operator familiarity with test requirements. Short samples waste material and testing slots and may delay release, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting crack or clouding classifications among technicians applying the inspection criteria
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting crack/clouding classifications among technicians is a criteria-consistency error, marginally relevant to containment; consequences are inspection disputes, not failures. Low, within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Even with written acceptance criteria, subjective grading of cracks and clouding produces inconsistent classifications between technicians, so reproducibility is weak. Reaching a classification is fast; the disagreement itself does not add meaningful delay. Consistency hinges on how uniformly technicians interpret written criteria, and interpretation still varies widely across individuals. Resolving conflicting calls consumes some re-inspection labor but little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of validated configuration change absent from the latest backup
rows.imp_rat: Validated configuration change absent from the latest backup is a records-currency gap only loosely tied to the containment CFJ (low relevance); it means a stale restore requiring minor rework (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Backup cadence often lags configuration changes, so a gap between latest validated state and last backup persists routinely. Recovering configuration changes missing from the last backup extends restoration time. Ensuring every validated change is captured relies on disciplined change-control practice by staff. Missing recent changes forces manual reconfiguration and revalidation, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of materials or tools dropped inside the enclosure during glove-port manipulation
rows.imp_rat: Materials or tools dropped during glove-port manipulation is a minor handling defect with little bearing on the containment CFJ (low relevance); the item stays inside the enclosure so consequences are minor cleanup or retrieval (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The ergonomic constraints of glove ports make dropped tools a persistent, hard-to-eliminate event. Recovering dropped tools through gloves consumes time and may require workspace reorganization. Dropping during glove-port manipulation is fundamentally a manual-dexterity limitation that training only partly overcomes. Dropped tools rarely cause direct material loss but can force cleaning or re-decontamination cycles.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of isolator deviation from the specified level tolerance at installation
rows.imp_rat: Level tolerance deviation at installation is a mechanical setup error with limited bearing on containment function; both relevance and severity are low.
rows.sat_rat: Precision leveling to specified tolerance is a mature, repeatable practice, so out-of-level installs are relatively rare. Leveling with shims and precision levels is a routine, fairly quick installation task. Achieving level tolerance is well within the standard competence of installation crews using laser levels. Leveling materials and labor are minor costs relative to the overall installation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of isolator deviation from the specified installation coordinates and orientation
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified installation coordinates and orientation is largely a placement/aesthetics issue; relevance to containment and severity are both minor, well within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard installation surveying keeps placement within tolerance in most cases, with occasional deviations from congested field conditions. Positioning to specified coordinates uses surveying and layout tools that are efficient once utilities are marked. Rigging crews and millwrights are practiced at placing equipment to layout drawings with standard instruments. Repositioning a heavy isolator after misplacement is costly, but pre-marked layouts usually prevent that.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the documented monitoring layout and the intended locations and configurations
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between documented and intended monitoring layout is a documentation-rework error peripheral to physical containment, with mild downstream consequences. Low, within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation-vs-intent mismatches for monitoring layout occur periodically despite drawing controls, so gaps persist but are usually catchable. Reconciling documented monitoring layout against intended locations is a documentary review that rarely dominates the schedule. Verifying monitoring placement needs contamination-control expertise to confirm points match risk assessment intent. Relocating monitoring points after documentation error is a minor cost relative to the project.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of anxiety while awaiting evidence needed for a containment-or-escalation decision
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while awaiting evidence for a containment-or-escalation decision is an emotional state remote from the isolation mechanism (low relevance); consequence is personal stress (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing confidence while others await the isolator-intervention decision
rows.imp_rat: Losing confidence while others await an intervention decision is an emotional state peripheral to the containment job (low relevance); the consequence is personal hesitation (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing confidence when unable to state sterility status while the team awaits direction
rows.imp_rat: Losing confidence when unable to state sterility status is an emotional state largely peripheral to the containment job (low relevance); consequences are personal hesitation, easily absorbed (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when a release decision is requested before cleanliness is clear
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when a release decision is requested before cleanliness is clear is an emotional error peripheral to ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is personal stress (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure as excursion indications intensify before the response team
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure as excursion indications intensify is an emotional-response error peripheral to physical isolation (low relevance); consequence is personal stress (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of self-doubt when monitoring indications and response-team reports conflict
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt when monitoring and response-team reports conflict is an emotional-alignment error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is personal hesitation (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain a documented contamination-control deviation decision
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to explain a deviation decision is an emotional-defensibility error remote from physical isolation (low relevance); consequence is personal discomfort (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of candidate-evaluation delays caused by unapproved qualification criteria
rows.imp_rat: Candidate-evaluation delays from unapproved criteria is a process-timeline error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is schedule slip (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows exist but bottleneck unpredictably, so evaluation delays from unapproved criteria remain a common, poorly-controlled problem. Delays from unapproved criteria are a direct, frequently-felt schedule loss with weak mitigation today. Getting criteria approved promptly depends on reviewer availability and negotiation skill rather than a systematic process. Idle equipment and staff during approval delays carry real opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty caused by conflicting reviewer interpretations of the same qualification evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting reviewer interpretations of the same evidence is a review-alignment error indirectly related to the isolation job (low relevance); consequence is decision delay (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when decision-makers question the evidence supporting the isolation recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when the recommendation's evidence is questioned is an emotional-defensibility error peripheral to containment (low relevance); consequence is personal discomfort (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty when defending how qualification criteria support acceptance decisions
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty defending how qualification criteria support acceptance is a defensibility concern remote from physical containment (low relevance); consequence is argumentative exposure (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when an acceptance decision cannot be traced to supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when an acceptance decision cannot be traced is an emotional-traceability error peripheral to the containment job (low relevance); consequence is personal discomfort (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes while qualification-evidence gaps remain unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety episodes while evidence gaps remain is an emotional-timing error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is personal stress (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of post-review aseptic requirement revisions from which the specifier is excluded
rows.imp_rat: The specifier excluded from post-review revisions is a role/governance error remote from the physical job (low relevance); consequence is process exclusion (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of regulatory and quality review beyond the specification approval date
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory/quality review extending beyond the approval date is a schedule error remote from ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is delay only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reviews reliably conclude but timelines are frequently exceeded. Regulatory and quality review overruns are a direct and common cause of approval delay. Reviewer expertise affects speed but the bottleneck is process throughput, not the role's skill. Extended review delays project spend recovery, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain the contamination-control rationale for an isolator requirement
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to explain a requirement's rationale is an emotional-defensibility error peripheral to containment (low relevance); consequence is personal discomfort (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in cost responsibility for scope boundaries, site interfaces, qualification support, and facility work
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in cost responsibility across scope/interfaces is a commercial-clarity error unrelated to the containment mechanism (low relevance); consequences are budget disputes (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cost boundaries are frequently left implicit, so disputes over who pays recur predictably. Resolving cost-responsibility ambiguity prolongs commercial negotiation. Allocating cost for interfaces and qualification support requires commercial and technical judgement combined. Ambiguous cost responsibility is a direct driver of unbudgeted spend when disputed items land on the buyer.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of expected isolator ownership cost omitted from lifecycle analysis
rows.imp_rat: Ownership cost omitted from lifecycle analysis is a budgeting error remote from ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is cost underestimate (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Ownership-cost omission is chronic because no standard captures all recurring cost elements. Compiling ownership-cost data takes effort that is commonly skipped under deadlines. Estimating full ownership cost needs maintenance and consumables expertise seldom integrated. Omitted ownership costs understate spend and surface as unbudgeted operating expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in accountability for site interfaces, delivery damage, installation readiness, and acceptance failures
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in accountability for site interfaces and acceptance failures is a commercial-governance error remote from containment (low relevance); consequences are dispute resolution (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Accountability gaps recur because responsibility for edge-case failures is rarely fully mapped upfront. Assigning accountability for damage and readiness failures adds contractual negotiation time. Structuring accountability clauses needs legal and logistics expertise beyond typical procurement staff. Unassigned accountability leaves the buyer absorbing costs of installation and acceptance failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of isolator-system scope changes requiring financial-case revision during authorization review
rows.imp_rat: Scope changes requiring financial-case revision is a project-governance error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is rework/delay (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Late scope changes recur because scope is often not frozen before authorization. Scope changes during authorization force financial-case revision loops that delay approval. Stabilizing scope before review requires disciplined upfront definition skill. Rework of the financial case consumes analyst effort and can shift approved amounts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of isolator-system lifecycle costs omitted from the financial case
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle costs omitted from the financial case is a budgeting error remote from ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is cost underestimate (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle costs are routinely omitted because the financial case focuses on acquisition price. Building a full lifecycle cost view early is time-consuming and often deferred. Lifecycle costing for isolators requires specialized total-cost-of-ownership skill rarely held by buyers. Omitted lifecycle costs are a direct and frequent understatement of true cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among quality, production, engineering, safety, and financial requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among functional requirements is a requirements-alignment error remote from ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is decision friction (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement conflicts recur because no single owner reconciles them systematically before award. Reconciling conflicting stakeholder requirements consumes significant coordination time. Mediating quality, production, and finance conflicts needs cross-functional facilitation skill. Unresolved conflicts cause rework and suboptimal specifications that raise cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplier and purchaser responsibilities lacking clear scope boundaries
rows.imp_rat: Unclear supplier/purchaser scope boundaries is a commercial-governance error remote from physical containment (low relevance); consequences are contractual friction, easily corrected (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Responsibility gaps recur because standard scope-split matrices are inconsistently applied. Defining scope boundaries adds negotiation rounds but is a familiar part of contracting. Drawing clean supplier/purchaser responsibility lines relies on experienced contract drafters who are not always available. Unclear boundaries lead to double-charging or gap-charging that inflates cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in projected lifecycle cash flows from incomplete cost, demand, and benefit assumptions
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in projected lifecycle cash flows is a financial-forecasting error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is planning inaccuracy (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Projected cash flows commonly carry unstated assumption risk, so uncertainty reduction is only partially achieved today. Robust cash-flow forecasting takes iterations that compressed approval windows discourage. Sound demand, benefit and cost assumptions require cross-functional forecasting expertise not concentrated in the buyer role. Weak assumptions propagate into over- or under-funding decisions with real financial consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the financial value attributed to reduced microbial and particulate ingress
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in financial value of reduced ingress is a business-case valuation error remote from the physical job (low relevance); consequence is weak justification (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: This benefit is intrinsically uncertain and rarely quantified with confidence, leaving a persistent gap. Valuing risk reduction from lower ingress requires analysis that lengthens case preparation. Attaching financial value to reduced microbial and particulate ingress needs specialized risk-quantification skill that is scarce. Misvalued contamination benefits can drive over- or under-investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of facility integration and utility expenditure missing from the isolator lifecycle estimate
rows.imp_rat: Facility integration/utility expenditure missing from the lifecycle estimate is a budgeting error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is cost underestimate (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Facility integration surprises are a common source of estimate error for isolator installations. Gathering facility and utility integration costs needs early engineering studies that slow the case. Accurately scoping utility and facility integration requires facilities engineering expertise beyond the buyer. Integration and utility costs are frequently omitted, understating true project cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of qualification and compliance expenditure missing from the isolator lifecycle estimate
rows.imp_rat: Qualification/compliance expenditure missing from the lifecycle estimate is a budgeting error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is cost underestimate (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Missing compliance costs is a consistent pattern, making the estimate unreliable without QA involvement. Compiling full qualification and compliance costs extends estimating effort. Estimating GMP qualification and compliance spend requires validation specialists whose input is often obtained late. Qualification and compliance expenditure is routinely underrepresented in early lifecycle estimates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of startup disruption and production loss omitted from the isolator lifecycle estimate
rows.imp_rat: Startup disruption/production loss omitted from the estimate is a budgeting error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is cost underestimate (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because it depends on uncertain implementation impacts, this cost is inconsistently captured. Estimating startup disruption requires production modelling that adds lead time. Quantifying startup and production loss demands manufacturing and scheduling expertise outside procurement. Startup disruption and production loss are frequently left out of lifecycle estimates entirely.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lifecycle cost uncertainty absent from the financial case before capital commitment
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle cost uncertainty absent from the financial case is a budgeting error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is forecast inaccuracy (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cases are frequently committed with material lifecycle uncertainty left implicit, making this a recurring gap. Adding lifecycle cost detail lengthens case preparation beyond typical schedules. Quantifying lifecycle uncertainty needs specialist cost-modelling skill that is scarce in most procurement teams. Unquantified lifecycle uncertainty routinely surfaces as unbudgeted spend later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination-control and compliance benefits lack decision-grade evidence
rows.imp_rat: Benefits lacking decision-grade evidence is a business-case error remote from ingress prevention (low relevance); consequence is weak justification (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Compliance and contamination benefits are notoriously hard to quantify, so decision-grade evidence is often lacking. Assembling decision-grade contamination-control evidence takes time not built into fast approval cycles. Translating contamination-control benefits into defensible financial evidence needs rare quality-economics skill. Weak benefit evidence can misprice the investment and waste capital on the wrong scope.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the isolator funding request follows an approval path lacking the required authority
rows.imp_rat: A funding request on an approval path lacking authority is a procedural-governance error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is rework/delay (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented DOA frameworks make wrong-authority routing relatively uncommon, though large or novel spends occasionally get misrouted. Approval routing is defined by delegation-of-authority policy, so the path is usually known upfront. Procurement staff are generally familiar with authority matrices and route requests accordingly. Following the correct authority path has negligible direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that the financial case includes the full isolator and facility-integration scope
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that the case includes full integration scope is a budgeting-completeness error remote from containment (low relevance); consequence is estimate gap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between registered service entitlements and the purchased agreement
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between registered service entitlements and the purchased agreement is a contractual bookkeeping error, peripheral to the containment CFJ (low relevance); consequences are commercial disputes, kept within the 2.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor portals and contract paperwork provide a reference, but registration errors and mismatches persist and often surface only at service time. Reconciling entitlements against the purchased agreement is a periodic clerical check that adds modest delay when disputes arise. Verifying entitlements requires only administrative attention to contract documents, not technical expertise. Discrepancies can mean paying for services already covered or losing entitled coverage, a real but bounded financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of charges incurred after glove box service contracts should have ended
rows.imp_rat: Charges incurred after service contracts should have ended is a billing waste with low relevance to containment; severity is modest financial leakage, held at or below 2.0 by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Contract end dates are documented and accounts-payable checks flag most stale charges, though manual oversight leaves occasional gaps. Catching post-expiry charges depends on invoice review cycles, so overbilling can persist for a billing period or two before detection. Spotting charges after contract end is a straightforward procurement review needing no specialized skill. The error IS wasted spend on lapsed contracts, and while procurement audits catch much of it, some leakage recurs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of stored user access data remaining in the control system after erasure
rows.imp_rat: Residual user access data after erasure is a data-hygiene concern peripheral to the glove box's containment CFJ (low relevance); severity is modest as it risks minor privacy exposure rather than containment failure, and the batch ceiling holds it at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a standard verified erasure step for control-system access data at disposal, so stored user data very often remains after nominal decommissioning. Purging stored access data is a quick action once addressed but is frequently deferred or skipped at disposal, so it lingers. Reliably wiping user access data from a control system needs IT/security knowledge that disposal crews typically do not apply. Data-erasure effort is minor, but residual access data creates security and privacy liability cost if the unit leaves with it intact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required isolator lifecycle records missing from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Missing lifecycle records is a documentation-archive gap that does not affect the isolator's live containment job (low relevance); it creates audit exposure rather than direct harm (modest severity). Capped per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Document-management practices exist and are enforced by GMP, so archive completeness is moderately reliable though gaps from long service histories persist. Compiling lifecycle records for the archive is time-consuming when data is spread across systems. Knowing which lifecycle records are required draws on quality and validation expertise generally available in pharma. Missing archive records mainly create compliance risk rather than direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of archived records linked to the wrong isolator or lifecycle event
rows.imp_rat: Mislinking archived records to the wrong isolator or lifecycle event is an administrative documentation error tangential to actual containment performance (low relevance); it can cause audit confusion but is correctable (low-moderate severity). Held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple similar isolators, serial-number reuse and inconsistent naming across systems mean wrong-asset links are a recurring, poorly-caught defect in archives. Correctly linking archived records to the right isolator is not slow per se, but tracing a mislink after the fact consumes significant time. Accurate indexing depends on document controllers who understand the equipment and lifecycle taxonomy, a judgement-heavy task. Mislinked records rarely cost money directly until an audit or dispute forces costly reconstruction.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that isolator records are destroyed before the required retention period ends
rows.imp_rat: Premature destruction of isolator records before retention ends is a records-compliance risk peripheral to physical containment (low relevance); it can expose the site to regulatory findings but does not affect operational safety (low-moderate severity). Bounded by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated pharma record-retention practice is mature and audited, so premature destruction is uncommon though not impossible with hybrid paper/electronic archives. Retention holds are typically automated in document systems so timing enforcement is not a delay driver. Applying correct GxP retention schedules requires records/QA expertise, but rules are well codified and widely understood. Premature destruction risks regulatory penalties, but retention storage itself is inexpensive so the safe default is over-retention.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in the isolator retirement status communicated to quality, regulatory, and facility stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent retirement-status communication among stakeholders is an end-of-life administrative error peripheral to the operating containment job (very low relevance); it risks minor governance confusion (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Without a single source of truth, stakeholders often hold differing views of retirement status, making inconsistency a frequent failure. Communicating retirement status across quality, regulatory and facility groups involves sequential sign-offs that slow closeout. Coordinating a consistent status message across stakeholders requires someone who understands each function's requirements. Inconsistent status messaging causes rework and duplicated effort rather than large direct cost.

rows.source_job: Assess residual value of the isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of decontamination, removal, transportation, and disposal costs omitted from the residual value assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting decontamination/disposal costs from a residual value assessment is a financial-accounting error with negligible bearing on the isolator actually containing product (very low relevance) and only mild fiscal consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard costing template for isolator disposal, so omitted cost elements are a frequent and predictable error. Building a full residual-value assessment takes analytical time often compressed at project end. Estimating decontamination, removal, transport and disposal costs demands specialized cost-engineering judgement rarely assembled. Omitting these costs systematically overstates residual value and produces budget shortfalls.

rows.stk: EHS, facilities, and waste management teams
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of eligible isolator components being routed to recovery facilities that lack required qualifications
rows.imp_rat: Routing eligible components to unqualified recovery facilities concerns end-of-life disposal logistics far from the core containment job (low relevance); while an unqualified recipient could mishandle residue, this is a downstream and infrequent consequence (modest severity). Capped per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor qualification and audit practices are mature in pharma, so misrouting to unqualified facilities is relatively well controlled though not foolproof. Vetting recovery facility qualifications takes procurement time but fits within normal lead times. Confirming a recipient's qualifications requires regulatory and vendor-audit knowledge that quality functions generally hold. Using an unqualified facility can trigger fines and rework costs far exceeding vetting effort.

rows.stk: EHS, facilities, and waste management teams
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of eligible isolator components rejected because required decontamination, material, or custody records are incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting eligible components over incomplete decontamination/custody records is a records-completeness administrative issue only tangential to running containment (low relevance), and the consequence is minor rework or delay rather than harm (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Records are frequently incomplete at disposal, so eligible components being rejected for documentation gaps is a common failure. Chasing missing decontamination, material or custody records delays recovery routing. Assembling complete records requires someone who knows exactly what recovery facilities demand, a niche competence. Rejected components become waste, forfeiting recovery value and adding disposal cost.

rows.source_job: Return isolator through applicable return program
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of isolator damage during removal, loading, or transit to the approved recipient
rows.imp_rat: Isolator damage during removal or transit to an approved recipient relates to decommissioning handling rather than the core containment job (low relevance); damage could be costly but is a one-off logistics consequence (modest severity). Held below batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Industrial equipment transport practices make in-transit damage uncommon when standard securement is applied. Protective loading and securement add handling time but are standard in transport planning. Proper crating, loading and lashing rely on established rigging and freight-handling skills. Packaging materials and specialized transport add cost but prevent larger damage losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent resolving missing or conflicting supplier information needed to compare candidate isolators
rows.imp_rat: Time resolving missing/conflicting supplier info during candidate comparison is a procurement-selection delay, peripheral to containment (very low relevance) and recoverable (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because supplier submissions are incomplete and noncomparable, comparison stalls on data resolution repeatedly. Resolving missing or conflicting supplier information is a well-known time sink during candidate comparison. Efficiently chasing and reconciling supplier data gaps depends on experienced buyers and evaluators. The effort is largely labor time rather than direct purchase cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approval package is rejected because required evidence is missing
rows.imp_rat: Approval-package rejection for missing evidence is a documentation-submission error peripheral to physical containment performance (low relevance); it causes resubmission delay, easily absorbed (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized business-case and stage-gate checklists make missing-evidence rejections uncommon in mature organizations. Approval packages follow a defined governance calendar, so rejections mostly cause predictable rework rounds. Assembling the required evidence is guided by established capital-approval templates that reduce reliance on individual judgement. Rejected packages waste staff effort and delay but rarely add large direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting acceptance criteria preventing authorization of the selected isolator purchase
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance criteria blocking purchase authorization is a procurement-decision error unrelated to operating containment (low relevance); it delays a purchase, an absorbable consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are often defined in silos with no single authority, so conflicts recur and block clean authorization. Resolving conflicting acceptance criteria before authorization can stall the approval decision for weeks. Reconciling quality, engineering, and procurement acceptance criteria requires cross-functional expertise that is hard to align. Conflicting criteria delay authorization more than they add direct cost, though delay carries opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of containment requirements revised after supplier engagement because project assumptions were not confirmed
rows.imp_rat: Containment requirements revised after supplier engagement due to unconfirmed assumptions is a scoping-rework error in procurement (low relevance) whose consequence is proposal rework, not operational failure (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Unconfirmed containment assumptions frequently surface after engagement, triggering requirement revisions. Revising containment requirements mid-engagement resets supplier work and delays the process. Confirming project assumptions on containment (OEB, airflow) upfront requires scarce toxicology and containment expertise. Late containment changes drive redesign and re-pricing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between occurrence of a transit delay and notification of the procurement specialist
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a transit delay and notifying the procurement specialist is a communication-timing error remote from the glove box's containment function (low relevance); severity is limited to schedule impact, capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Track-and-trace and freight-forwarder alerts reliably surface delays quickly, though notification to the specific specialist can still lag for some carriers. Modern shipment tracking and carrier notifications substantially shorten the gap between a transit delay and specialist awareness. Monitoring tracking systems is a low-skill routine task within procurement operations. Faster delay notification lets teams mitigate downstream cost, and tracking tools are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipment discrepancy reports lacking evidence needed to substantiate the claim
rows.imp_rat: Shipment discrepancy reports lacking substantiating evidence is a claims-documentation rework issue peripheral to containment (low relevance); its consequence is unrecoverable claims, kept at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Receiving procedures exist but discrepancy reports frequently lack the photographic or condition evidence needed to substantiate claims, so this is weakly served. Assembling discrepancy report evidence at receipt takes time under delivery-day pressure. Documenting substantiating evidence for shipment claims requires trained receiving staff who know what proof carriers and vendors demand. Unsubstantiated claims are rejected, forcing the buyer to absorb transit damage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate containment claims lacking comparable supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Containment claims lacking comparable supporting evidence is an evidence/marketing ambiguity with low relevance to physically running the glove box; severity is limited to weakened claim credibility, and the ceiling holds it at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors provide containment data of variable rigor, and buyers often cannot secure comparable evidence for many claims, leaving this poorly served. Gathering comparable containment evidence extends the technical evaluation phase. Assessing whether containment claims are backed by comparable evidence requires containment specialists who can interpret SMEPAC and OEB data. Accepting unsupported claims risks costly performance shortfalls and re-procurement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of gathered references based on applications not comparable to intended sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: References based on non-comparable applications is a reference-quality ambiguity distant from the operational CFJ (low relevance); its consequence is misleading evaluation input, capped within the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: References are commonly collected but their comparability to sterile fill-finish conditions is inconsistently vetted, undermining their value. Filtering references for comparability adds effort to the evaluation but not major delay. Judging whether a reference application is truly comparable to intended sterile fill-finish use requires nuanced expert judgement often lacking. Relying on non-comparable references risks poor selection and downstream cost, though gathering them is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed maintenance, calibration, and requalification activities omitted from the isolator log
rows.imp_rat: Omitting completed maintenance/calibration activities from the log is a documentation error affecting audit traceability, low centrality to active containment (low relevance) with modest consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Paper and CMMS logging is standard, yet manual entry means activities are still periodically omitted or recorded inconsistently, a recurring audit gap. Log entry is quick but adds administrative overhead at the end of each activity. Recording completed work correctly requires diligence rather than expertise, though it is easy to defer or forget. Logging costs only staff time, but omissions can trigger costly compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled sterile fill-finish disruption caused by requalification overruns
rows.imp_rat: Requalification overruns disrupting fill-finish touch on schedule but are peripheral to the isolator's containment CFJ (low relevance); consequence is delay, not breach, and the ranking caps it (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Failed or ambiguous test results trigger re-tests and investigations, so requalification duration is inherently variable and hard to guarantee. Requalification (leak/pressure decay, HEPA integrity, glove testing, cleaning validation) is time-consuming and overruns routinely push sterile activities later than planned. Requalification depends on specialist QA/validation and metrology staff whose scarcity and manual test execution frequently cause the overruns. Test consumables, contract specialists and idle line time make overruns expensive, and status quo scheduling leaves little slack.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of isolator unavailability that disrupts scheduled sterile fill-finish activities
rows.imp_rat: Isolator unavailability disrupting scheduled fill-finish is more central to throughput, but relative to prior batch items it ranks lower and must be capped (low relevance here); consequence is production delay, not containment breach (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because a single isolator is often a bottleneck with no redundancy, any hiccup reliably disrupts the fill schedule. Isolator downtime bleeds directly into scheduled fill-finish slots and, despite planned windows, unplanned faults and requalification steps frequently extend unavailability into production time. Coordinating maintenance to protect campaigns relies on skilled planners and multi-disciplinary teams whose availability is a recurring constraint. Lost sterile production time is extraordinarily costly, so sites invest heavily to compress downtime, but the buffer is thin.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required update records and validation evidence remaining incomplete after installation
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete update and validation records after installation is a documentation-completeness defect adjacent to qualification (modest relevance) with correctable consequences (low severity). Constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and IQ/OQ packages define required evidence, but records frequently remain partially incomplete after installation, so the outcome is inconsistent. Compiling update and validation evidence can lag well after installation, but the completion delay is secondary to the completeness gap. Knowing exactly which validation evidence is required draws on QA/validation expertise. Incomplete evidence risks costly re-execution of validation activities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the repair is delayed by unavailable compatible replacement components
rows.imp_rat: Repair delays from unavailable compatible parts affect uptime but are peripheral to the core containment job itself (low relevance); consequences are schedule cost rather than contamination breach (modest severity). Ranked below prior batch, so capped.
rows.sat_rat: Substituting a non-identical part risks containment integrity, so teams wait for the qualified component rather than improvise, making delays chronic. Long OEM lead times for compatible glove ports, gaskets, HEPA filters and proprietary parts routinely delay repairs for weeks, and status quo spares-holding rarely covers every failure mode. Sourcing correct compatible components demands experienced technicians who know the isolator's revision history and cross-reference approved equivalents, a scarce and error-prone judgement. Holding buffer inventory of expensive proprietary spares ties up capital, so most sites under-stock and eat the delay cost when a part is unavailable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of isolator maintenance, calibration, and repair activities omitted from the history
rows.imp_rat: Activities omitted from the maintenance/calibration/repair history is a records-completeness error secondary to containment (low relevance); it produces traceability gaps that are correctable (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual logbooks and paper work orders leave recurring gaps because activities are sometimes performed but not recorded, so history completeness is inconsistent. Recording each completed activity takes little time when done at the point of work. Logging requires only routine documentation discipline, not specialized skill. Missing history entries risk audit findings and rework but incur little direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the isolator remains unavailable while awaiting a required replacement component
rows.imp_rat: Isolator unavailability awaiting a replacement component is a spares-lead-time delay adjacent to uptime but not the core function (low relevance), causing production slip rather than harm (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Because critical spares are frequently not stocked and OEM lead times are long, component-driven downtime is unpredictable and often prolonged. Waiting for a required replacement component, often long-lead or OEM-specific, is a major recurring driver of extended isolator downtime. Sourcing the correct component needs familiarity with the equipment and supplier chain but not exceptional skill. Prolonged unavailability and expedited freight impose substantial opportunity and procurement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve missing or inconsistent cleaning and decontamination records before isolator release
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve inconsistent cleaning records before release is a documentation-reconciliation delay adjacent to release (modest relevance) but consequences are administrative delay, not breach (low severity). Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Because records live across paper logs and disparate systems, inconsistencies surface late and are slow to resolve, keeping release unpredictable. Chasing down and reconciling missing or inconsistent cleaning/decon records before release is a recurring delay that stalls isolator return to service. Reconstructing records requires knowing whom to ask and how the process ran, leaning on institutional memory. The delay ties up the isolator and staff time, imposing opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of air bypass around the newly installed filtration media at its sealing interface
rows.imp_rat: Air bypass at the filtration sealing interface matters to containment, but per the batch ceiling this ranks below prior items (capped low relevance); leakage could compromise product/operator protection yet is bounded here (capped low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Post-installation DOP/PAO leak testing at the seal is standard practice and reliably detects bypass, so undetected bypass is uncommon even if initial seating varies. Seating and sealing new filter media is a routine task done within a normal maintenance window. Achieving a leak-free seal at the gasket interface depends on the installer's care and technique, so skill drives the outcome. Filter media and gaskets are consumables of modest cost, with bypass forcing occasional re-work.
